## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Question, PCEBL508, 2024 Scheme]**  
*Define Heart Rate Variability (HRV). List any four time-domain parameters used to quantify HRV and state the physiological significance of RMSSD.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**  
Heart Rate Variability (HRV) is defined as the variation in the time interval between successive heartbeats, measured as the variation in the R-R intervals of the QRS complex in an ECG signal. It is a non-invasive quantitative marker of Autonomic Nervous System (ANS) activity.

**Four time-domain parameters (1 mark, 0.25 each):**  
1. **Mean RR** тАФ Average of all normal RR intervals.  
2. **SDNN** тАФ Standard deviation of all normal RR intervals.  
3. **RMSSD** тАФ Root mean square of successive RR differences.  
4. **pNN50** тАФ Percentage of successive RR intervals differing by more than 50 ms.

**Physiological significance of RMSSD (1 mark):**  
RMSSD primarily reflects the **beat-to-beat variability** mediated by the **parasympathetic (vagal) nervous system**. It is considered the most reliable short-term index of vagal tone; higher RMSSD indicates stronger parasympathetic activity and better cardiovascular fitness.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Model Question, PCEBL508, 2024 Scheme]**  
*Explain the four frequency bands used in HRV analysis. Mention the physiological correlate of the LF/HF ratio.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Four frequency bands (2 marks, 0.5 each):**

1. **VLF (Very Low Frequency): 0.003 тАУ 0.04 Hz** тАФ Associated with thermoregulation, hormonal fluctuations, and the renin-angiotensin system.  
2. **LF (Low Frequency): 0.04 тАУ 0.15 Hz** тАФ Associated with baroreflex control; reflects a mix of sympathetic and parasympathetic activity, with sympathetic dominance during stress.  
3. **HF (High Frequency): 0.15 тАУ 0.4 Hz** тАФ Associated with respiratory sinus arrhythmia (RSA) and is a pure marker of **parasympathetic (vagal) activity**.  
4. **Total Power (TP): 0 тАУ 0.4 Hz** тАФ Sum of all band powers; reflects global autonomic activity.

**LF/HF ratio (1 mark):**  
The LF/HF ratio is the **Sympathovagal Balance Index**. A higher value indicates sympathetic dominance (e.g., stress, exercise), while a lower value indicates parasympathetic dominance (e.g., rest, deep sleep). Normal resting values lie between 1 and 2.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each тАФ Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**  
> Common student mistakes on this experiment worth 14 marks:  
> 1. **Forgetting to remove ectopic beats** тЖТ HRV numbers are 30тАУ50% inflated. Loss: **up to 3 marks**.  
> 2. **Confusing SDNN with RMSSD** тАФ SDNN is global HRV; RMSSD is vagal-specific. Loss: **2 marks**.  
> 3. **Wrong frequency band limits** тАФ writing 0.04 to 0.4 instead of 0.04 to 0.15 for LF. Loss: **2 marks**.  
> 4. **Mixing up Lomb-Scargle and Welch** тАФ Welch needs resampling, Lomb-Scargle does not. Loss: **1 mark**.  
> 5. **Not mentioning the Pan-Tompkins algorithm** when describing R-peak detection. Loss: **1 mark**.  
> 6. **Reporting LF/HF without specifying the recording length** тАФ short-term (< 5 min) and 24-h norms differ. Loss: **1 mark**.  
> 7. **No clear block diagram** in viva тЖТ examiner deducts **1 mark** for poor visualization.

---

**Part B тАФ Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]**  
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the step-by-step procedure for computing HRV from an ECG signal. Discuss preprocessing and R-peak detection in detail. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Derive the mathematical expressions for SDNN and RMSSD. Using a 5-beat RR sequence $\{750,\ 780,\ 760,\ 800,\ 770\}$ ms, compute both metrics. Interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure with Block Diagram (7 marks):**

**Step 1 тАФ ECG Acquisition (1 mark):**  
Acquire a clean single-lead ECG (Lead II preferred) for 5 minutes (short-term) or 24 hours (Holter). Sampling rate тЙе 256 Hz; preferably 500 Hz or 1000 Hz to preserve QRS morphology.

**Step 2 тАФ Preprocessing (2 marks):**  
- Apply a **bandpass filter (0.5 тАУ 40 Hz)** to remove baseline wander (low-frequency) and EMG/mains noise (high-frequency).  
- Apply a **notch filter at 50 Hz** to suppress India-specific powerline interference.  
- Optionally use **wavelet denoising** for high-noise ambulatory recordings.

**Step 3 тАФ R-peak Detection тАФ Pan-Tompkins Algorithm (2 marks):**  
Pan-Tompkins is the gold standard. Pipeline:  
(i) Bandpass filter (5тАУ15 Hz) тЖТ isolate QRS energy.  
(ii) Derivative тЖТ emphasize steep QRS slopes.  
(iii) Squaring тЖТ make all values positive and emphasize large differences.  
(iv) Moving-window integration (typically 150 ms window) тЖТ smooth and locate QRS.  
(v) Adaptive thresholding тЖТ mark peaks above threshold as R.  
(vi) Search-back and refractory period (200 ms) to avoid T-wave false positives.

**Step 4 тАФ RR Interval Extraction (1 mark):**  
$RR_i = t_{R_{i+1}} - t_{R_i}$ for $i = 1, 2, ..., N-1$. This produces the **tachogram**.

**Step 5 тАФ Artifact (Ectopic) Removal (1 mark):**  
Any $RR_i$ that deviates by more than 20% from the local mean of the surrounding 5 beats is flagged as ectopic. Either rejected, or replaced by interpolation. This step is critical тАФ without it, HRV values are misleading.

**Block Diagram (compact textual representation for viva):**

$$\text{ECG} \rightarrow \text{BPF 0.5-40 Hz} \rightarrow \text{Notch 50 Hz} \rightarrow \text{Pan-Tompkins} \rightarrow \text{RR Series} \rightarrow \text{Artifact Removal} \rightarrow \text{Tachogram} \rightarrow \text{Time \& Frequency Analysis}$$

**(b) Derivation & Computation (7 marks):**

**Derivation of SDNN (2 marks):**  
Let the RR intervals be $RR_1, RR_2, ..., RR_N$. The sample mean is:

$$\overline{RR} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} RR_i$$

The standard deviation of all N-N intervals (with Bessel's correction $N-1$ for an unbiased estimator) is:

$$SDNN = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(RR_i - \overline{RR})^2}$$

**Derivation of RMSSD (1 mark):**  
The successive differences are $\Delta RR_i = RR_{i+1} - RR_i$ for $i = 1, ..., N-1$. The mean square of successive differences is:

$$MSD = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1}(RR_{i+1} - RR_i)^2$$

Taking the square root gives:

$$RMSSD = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1}(RR_{i+1} - RR_i)^2}$$

**Computation (3 marks):**  
Given: $RR = \{750,\ 780,\ 760,\ 800,\ 770\}$ ms, $N = 5$.

**Mean RR:**  
$\overline{RR} = (750+780+760+800+770)/5 = 3860/5 = 772$ ms.

**SDNN:**  
Deviations: $d = \{-22,\ +8,\ -12,\ +28,\ -2\}$  
Squared deviations: $d^2 = \{484,\ 64,\ 144,\ 784,\ 4\}$  
Sum: $\sum d^2 = 484+64+144+784+4 = 1480$

$$SDNN = \sqrt{\frac{1480}{4}} = \sqrt{370} \approx 19.24 \text{ ms}$$

**RMSSD:**  
Successive differences: $\Delta RR = \{+30,\ -20,\ +40,\ -30\}$  
Squared: $\Delta RR^2 = \{900,\ 400,\ 1600,\ 900\}$  
Sum: $3800$

$$RMSSD = \sqrt{\frac{3800}{4}} = \sqrt{950} \approx 30.82 \text{ ms}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**  
- $SDNN \approx 19.24$ ms reflects modest global HRV for a 5-beat sample (the Task Force norms are for longer recordings, but the magnitude is consistent with a young adult at rest).  
- $RMSSD \approx 30.82$ ms suggests healthy **vagal (parasympathetic)** tone.  
- Note: With only 5 beats, statistical reliability is low тАФ practical HRV analysis requires тЙе 5 minutes (~300 beats).

---

**Part B тАФ Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model тАФ Alternative Choice]**  
**(a)** Explain the power spectral density (PSD) estimation of HRV using Welch's method. Compare it with the Lomb-Scargle periodogram. State the frequency bands and their physiological meanings. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Using Kubios HRV software, describe the standard procedure to analyze a 5-minute ECG recording. List all outputs produced in time and frequency domains. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PSD Estimation & Frequency Bands (7 marks):**

**Welch's Method (3 marks):**  
Welch's method is the most common PSD estimator for HRV because it reduces the variance of the periodogram by averaging.

Steps:  
(i) The RR tachogram is resampled to a uniform sampling rate (typically 4 Hz) using **cubic spline interpolation**.  
(ii) The resampled signal is divided into **overlapping segments** (e.g., 256-sample windows, 50% overlap).  
(iii) Each segment is multiplied by a window function (Hann or Hamming) to reduce spectral leakage.  
(iv) The **periodogram** of each windowed segment is computed via FFT:  

$$\hat{P}_k(f) = \frac{1}{U \cdot f_s}\left| \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_k[n]\, h[n]\, e^{-j2\pi f n / f_s} \right|^2$$

where $U = \frac{1}{L}\sum h^2[n]$ is the window energy.  
(v) The final Welch PSD is the **average of all $K$ segment periodograms**:  

$$\hat{P}_{Welch}(f) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\hat{P}_k(f)$$

This averaging reduces variance by a factor of $K$ at the cost of frequency resolution (controlled by segment length $L$).

**Lomb-Scargle Periodogram (2 marks):**  
The Lomb-Scargle periodogram is designed for **unevenly sampled data**, so it works directly on the raw RR tachogram without resampling. It fits sinusoidal models to the data using a least-squares approach and produces a PSD-like output. The formula is:

$$P_{LS}(f) = \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[ \frac{\left[\sum_{n=1}^{N}(x_n - \bar{x})\cos 2\pi f(t_n - \tau)\right]^2}{\sum_{n=1}^{N}\cos^2 2\pi f(t_n - \tau)} + \frac{\left[\sum_{n=1}^{N}(x_n - \bar{x})\sin 2\pi f(t_n - \tau)\right]^2}{\sum_{n=1}^{N}\sin^2 2\pi f(t_n - \tau)} \right]$$

where $\tau$ is a time-offset that orthogonalizes sine and cosine, and $\sigma^2$ is the variance of $x$.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Welch | Lomb-Scargle |
|---|---|---|
| Resampling needed | Yes (4 Hz) | No |
| Computation | FFT-based, fast | Direct, slower |
| Frequency resolution | Fixed by window length | Tunable by frequency grid |
| Best for | Evenly sampled data | Unevenly sampled, missing beats |

**Frequency Bands (1 mark):**  
- VLF (0.003 тАУ 0.04 Hz): thermoregulation, hormonal.  
- LF (0.04 тАУ 0.15 Hz): baroreflex, mixed autonomic.  
- HF (0.15 тАУ 0.4 Hz): parasympathetic / respiratory sinus arrhythmia.  
- LF/HF ratio: sympathovagal balance.

**(b) Procedure in Kubios HRV Software (7 marks):**

**Step-by-step workflow (5 marks):**

1. **Data Import (1 mark):** Open Kubios тЖТ File тЖТ Open тЖТ select ECG file (formats: EDF+, MIT-BIH, CSV, GDF, Kubios native MAT).  
2. **Preprocessing (1 mark):** Apply automatic **R-peak detection** (Pan-Tompkins in Kubios). View the raw ECG and detected R-markers; manually correct any missed/wrong detections.  
3. **Artifact Correction (1 mark):** Choose artifact correction level:  
   - **Very strong** (custom threshold)  
   - **Strong**  
   - **Medium**  
   - **Automatic (default)** тАФ uses an adaptive median filter.  
   The Kubios threshold is `k ├Ч local MAD` where k can be 0.45, 0.75, 1.50, 2.50, 3.50 (very strong тЖТ none).  
4. **Analysis Setup (1 mark):** Select the recording length (5 min / 1 h / full 24 h). Choose detrending method (none / linear / smoothness priors, default smoothness priors with $\lambda = 500$).  
5. **Computation & Output (1 mark):** Click "Calculate" тАФ Kubios produces the report.

**Outputs of Kubios (2 marks, list 6тАУ8 outputs):**

**Time-domain outputs:**  
- Mean RR (ms)  
- Mean HR (bpm)  
- Min/Max HR  
- SDNN (ms)  
- RMSSD (ms)  
- pNN50 (%)  
- SDSD (ms)  
- CV (coefficient of variation)

**Frequency-domain outputs (Welch):**  
- VLF power (ms┬▓)  
- LF power (ms┬▓)  
- HF power (ms┬▓)  
- Total Power (ms┬▓)  
- LF/HF ratio  
- LF (nu), HF (nu)  
- VLF peak, LF peak, HF peak frequencies (Hz)

**Non-linear outputs (bonus):**  
- Poincar├й plot (SD1, SD2)  
- Sample Entropy (SampEn)  
- Approximate Entropy (ApEn)  
- DFA ╬▒1, ╬▒2

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **HRV Analysis тАФ Top 25 Things to Memorize Before the Lab Exam**

1. **HRV = variation in RR intervals**, NOT variation in heart rate itself.  
2. **Time domain** = statistical; **Frequency domain** = spectral.  
3. The gold standard R-detector is the **Pan-Tompkins algorithm**.  
4. Always **remove ectopic beats** (deviation > 20% from local mean).  
5. **Mean RR** units are ms; **HR** units are bpm; **$HR = 60000/\overline{RR}$**.  
6. **SDNN** = global HRV; computed using $N-1$ in the denominator.  
7. **RMSSD** = vagal tone; computed from successive differences.  
8. **pNN50** = % of successive differences > 50 ms.  
9. **VLF**: 0.003тАУ0.04 Hz тАФ thermoregulation, hormones.  
10. **LF**: 0.04тАУ0.15 Hz тАФ baroreflex, mixed autonomic.  
11. **HF**: 0.15тАУ0.4 Hz тАФ parasympathetic, RSA.  
12. **TP** = VLF + LF + HF; **LF/HF = sympathetic/parasympathetic balance**.  
13. Welch's method requires **resampling** of the RR series (typically 4 Hz).  
14. Lomb-Scargle does **NOT** require resampling.  
15. Welch averages **K overlapping windowed periodograms**.  
16. **Kubios HRV** is the most popular academic HRV analysis software.  
17. Kubios uses an **adaptive median filter** for artifact correction.  
18. **Smoothness priors detrending** (default ╬╗=500) is used to remove slow trends.  
19. Short-term HRV analysis window = **5 minutes** (standard).  
20. Long-term HRV analysis window = **24 hours (Holter)**.  
21. **Healthy SDNN (5 min)** > 100 ms; **(24 h)** > 141 ms (Kleiger et al. norm).  
22. **Healthy RMSSD (5 min)** is typically 20тАУ80 ms in young adults.  
23. **Healthy LF/HF ratio** is between 1 and 2 at rest.  
24. A **Hann window** of length 256 with 50% overlap is the default Welch setup.  
25. HRV is a **clinical biomarker** for diabetic neuropathy, post-MI risk, stress, and athletic overtraining.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time domain filtering of bio signal for removal of baseline drift & high frequency noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBL508 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Time domain filtering of bio signal for removal of baseline drift & high frequency noise # SECTION 1 тАФ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Bioelectric Signal? A **bioelectric (or biosignal)** is an electrical voltage or current generated by a biological system that carries diagnostically meaningful physiological information. The most frequently processed bio-signals in the KTU PCEBL508 lab are: - **ECG (Electrocardiogram):** $\approx 0.05\text{ Hz}$ to $150\text{ Hz}$, amplitude $0.5\text{ mV}$ to $5\text{ mV}$. - **EEG (Electroencephalogram):** $\approx 0.5\text{ Hz}$ to $100\text{ Hz}$, amplitude $1\,\mu\text{V}$ to $100\,\mu\text{V}$. - **EMG (Electromyogram):** $\approx 20\text{ Hz}$ to $500\text{ Hz}$, amplitude $50\,\mu\text{V}$ to $5\text{ mV}$. - **EOG (Electro-oculogram):** $\approx \text{DC}$ to $50\text{ Hz}$, amplitude $10\,\mu\text{V}$ to $200\,\mu\text{V}$. > [!NOTE] > Because these signals are **low-amplitude, low-frequency**, they are extremely vulnerable to two unwanted contaminations: **baseline drift** (low-frequency) and **high-frequency noise** (high-frequency). ## 1.2 Baseline Drift тАФ The Slow Wander **Baseline drift** (also called *baseline wander* or *DC offset fluctuation*) is a low-frequency, often quasi-periodic deviation of the recorded signal away from the true iso-electric (zero) line. **Causes:** 1. **Respiration** тАФ chest expansion alters electrode impedance ($\approx 0.15\text{ Hz}$ to $0.5\text{ Hz}$). 2. **Electrode-skin contact polarization** тАФ drift in the half-cell potential. 3. **Patient body movement** тАФ slow mechanical shifts of electrodes. 4. **Temperature drift** in analog front-end electronics. **Frequency range:** Approximately **$\mathbf{0\text{ Hz}}$ to $\mathbf{0.5\text{ Hz}}$** тАФ well below the bio-signal's information band. ## 1.3 High-Frequency Noise тАФ The Stinging Hiss **High-frequency noise** sits *above* the diagnostically important spectral band and corrupts the fine morphology of the waveform. **Sources:** 1. **Power-line interference (PLI)** at **$50\text{ Hz}$** (India/Europe) or **$60\text{ Hz}$** (USA), with harmonics. 2. **Muscle artifact (EMG contamination)** тАФ broadband above **$20\text{ Hz}$**. 3. **Instrumentation noise** тАФ thermal (JohnsonтАУNyquist) noise from electrode and op-amp input stages. 4. **Electromagnetic interference (EMI)** from fluorescent lamps, mobile phones, electrosurgical units. **Frequency range:** Typically **$\mathbf{>40\text{ Hz}}$** for PLI, and broadband above **$20\text{ Hz}$** for EMG. ## 1.4 Time-Domain Filtering тАФ Concept & Analogy **Time-domain filtering** is the operation of transforming a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ into an enhanced signal $y[n]$ by directly combining present and past *sample values* using a linear combination (convolution-like operation). **No frequency transformation (FFT) is performed.** > [!IMPORTANT] > In time-domain filtering, **each output sample is computed from neighbouring input samples**. This is in contrast to frequency-domain filtering, which requires conversion via Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) and is therefore computationally heavier for real-time embedded systems. ### Conceptual Analogy тАФ "Cleaning the Aquarium View" Imagine looking at fish inside a murky aquarium: - **Baseline drift** is the *gentle water-level rise and fall* that distorts your viewing horizon тАФ easy to remove by subtracting the slow average. - **High-frequency noise** is the *floating dust particles and ripples* bouncing on the surface тАФ removed by smoothing over a small window of time. You never break the glass and rebuild the tank (that would be frequency-domain redesign). You just look through a different **window of samples** in time. *That window is the digital filter.* ## 1.5 The Two-Filter Cascade A complete time-domain cleaning of a typical bio-signal uses a **cascade** (series connection) of two complementary filters: | Stage | Filter Type | Purpose | Typical Implementation | |:-----:|:-----------:|:-------:|:----------------------:| | 1 | **High-pass filter** | Remove baseline drift | First-order IIR or moving-average subtraction | | 2 | **Low-pass filter** | Remove high-frequency noise | Moving average or weighted MA | > [!TIP] > The order matters! Always remove the **slow drift first**, because the low-pass smoother that follows will otherwise smear the drift into the signal. The classic KTU lab cascade is therefore: **HPF тЖТ LPF**. --- # SECTION 2 тАФ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The General Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) Time-Domain Filter Any discrete-time LTI filter is described by the **linear constant-coefficient difference equation (LCCDE)**: $$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k] \;-\; \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k]$$ where: - $x[n]$ = current and past input samples, - $y[n]$ = current output sample, - $b_k$ = **feed-forward (FIR) coefficients**, defining the zero structure, - $a_k$ = **feedback (IIR) coefficients**, defining the pole structure, - $M$ = number of zeros, $N$ = number of poles, - $n$ = discrete time index. ### Classification by Structure 1. **FIR (Finite Impulse Response)** filter: all $a_k = 0$ for $k \ge 1$ тЖТ *no feedback* тЖТ **always stable**. The impulse response settles to zero in finite samples. 2. **IIR (Infinite Impulse Response)** filter: at least one $a_k \ne 0$ for $k \ge 1$ тЖТ *feedback exists* тЖТ impulse response theoretically infinite. ## 2.2 The Moving Average (MA) Filter тАФ The Workhorse The **uniform moving-average filter** of length $N$ computes each output sample as the arithmetic mean of the current and the previous $N-1$ samples: $$y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\,\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]$$ **Why does this remove high-frequency noise?** Because random noise averages out over many samples, while a slowly varying signal component changes only slightly within $N$ samples and is therefore preserved. The signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) improves by a factor of $\sqrt{N}$ for white noise. **Recursive implementation (memory efficient):** $$y[n] = y[n-1] + \frac{x[n] - x[n-N]}{N}$$ This uses only **two additions and one subtraction** per output sample regardless of $N$ тАФ perfect for embedded processors. ## 2.3 The Weighted Moving Average (WMA) Filter To reduce edge-smoothing artefacts of the uniform MA, we use a weighted version: $$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k \, x[n-k] \quad\text{with}\quad \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k = 1$$ A common KTU-lab choice is the **triangular (binomial) kernel**: $$w_k \;\propto\; \binom{N-1}{k}$$ ## 2.4 First-Order IIR High-Pass Filter for Baseline Drift Removal The single most common baseline-drift filter in biomedical labs is the **first-order high-pass IIR** defined by: $$y[n] = \alpha \, y[n-1] + \alpha \, \bigl( x[n] - x[n-1] \bigr)$$ where the smoothing factor is: $$\alpha \;=\; \frac{\tau}{\tau + T_s}$$ with $T_s$ = sampling period and $\tau$ = filter time-constant (chosen so that $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau} \approx 0.5\text{ Hz}$). **Intuition:** The term $(x[n] - x[n-1])$ is a discrete differentiator that blocks any constant (DC) component тАФ perfect for removing the wandering baseline тАФ while the $\alpha \, y[n-1]$ term adds one-pole smoothing so the output doesn't ring on sharp QRS complexes. ## 2.5 Two-Point Difference for DC and Drift A simpler (zero-phase delay) baseline estimator is the two-point running average: $$y[n] = \frac{x[n] - x[n-1]}{2}$$ which approximates a high-pass behaviour for frequencies below the Nyquist limit. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** for this experiment. Every line is fair game in the KTU ESE viva or written exam. | # | Formula | Filter Type | Removes | Key Parameter | |:-:|:-------:|:-----------:|:-------:|:-------------:| | 1 | $y[n] = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}x[n-k]$ | Moving Average (FIR) | HF noise | Window length $N$ | | 2 | $y[n] = y[n-1] + \dfrac{x[n]-x[n-N]}{N}$ | Recursive MA | HF noise | Same $N$, $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per sample | | 3 | $y[n] = \sum w_k x[n-k]$, $\sum w_k = 1$ | Weighted MA (FIR) | HF noise | Weights $w_k$ (triangular, Gaussian, Hamming) | | 4 | $y[n] = \alpha\,y[n-1] + \alpha\,(x[n] - x[n-1])$ | First-order HP IIR | Baseline drift | $\alpha = \dfrac{\tau}{\tau + T_s}$, $f_c \approx 0.5\text{ Hz}$ | | 5 | $y[n] = x[n] - \dfrac{1}{M}\sum_{k=0}^{M-1}x[n-k]$ | MA-subtraction HPF | Baseline drift | Long $M$ window | | 6 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \text{SNR}_{\text{in}} + 10\log_{10}(N)$ | SNR gain of MA | Noise | $N$ = window length | | 7 | $f_c \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau}$ | Cutoff frequency | тАФ | $\tau = \alpha T_s / (1-\alpha)$ | | 8 | $H(z) = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} z^{-k}$ | MA transfer function | HF noise | Zeros uniformly on unit circle | | 9 | $H(z) = \dfrac{\alpha(1 - z^{-1})}{1 - \alpha z^{-1}}$ | 1st-order HP IIR | Baseline drift | Pole at $z = \alpha$, zero at $z = 1$ | | 10 | $\Delta \text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}(N)\text{ dB}$ | Noise suppression | White noise | For MA over white noise | > **Boundary conditions used in KTU lab:** > - ECG sampling: $f_s = 360\text{ Hz}$ to $1000\text{ Hz}$. > - EEG sampling: $f_s = 100\text{ Hz}$ to $500\text{ Hz}$. > - Typical MA length for HF noise in ECG: $N = 5$ to $15$. > - Typical $\alpha$ for baseline HPF on ECG sampled at $f_s = 360\text{ Hz}$: $\alpha \approx 0.995$ (giving $f_c \approx 0.29\text{ Hz}$). ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application of Time-Domain Bio-Signal Filtering | |:------|:------------------------------------------------| | **Cardiology** | Cleaning ECG before R-peak detection, HRV analysis | | **Neurology** | Removing sweat artefact from EEG before spectral analysis | | **Sleep Medicine** | Pre-processing polysomnography (PSG) channels | | **Rehabilitation** | Real-time EMG denoising in prosthetic control | | **Wearable / IoT Health** | On-chip filtering in low-power MCUs (ARM Cortex-M) | | **Medical AI** | Pre-processing step before feeding CNN/LSTM models | --- # SECTION 3 тАФ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 тАФ Transfer Function of the Moving-Average Filter **Goal:** Show that the MA filter is a low-pass FIR with zeros on the unit circle, and derive its magnitude response. ### Starting Definition The $N$-point uniform MA filter is defined in the time domain as: $$y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\,\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]$$ ### Step 1 тАФ Apply the Z-Transform Using the linearity of $\mathcal{Z}\{\cdot\}$ and the time-shift property $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n-k]\} = z^{-k} X(z)$: $$Y(z) = \frac{1}{N}\,\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} z^{-k}\, X(z)$$ ### Step 2 тАФ Factor Out $X(z)$ $$Y(z) = \underbrace{\left[\frac{1}{N}\,\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} z^{-k}\right]}_{H(z)} \, X(z)$$ ### Step 3 тАФ Identify the Transfer Function $H(z)$ $$H(z) = \frac{1}{N}\,\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} z^{-k}$$ This is a finite geometric series. Apply the closed-form identity $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} r^{k} = \dfrac{1 - r^{N}}{1 - r}$ with $r = z^{-1}$: $$H(z) = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-N}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$ ### Step 4 тАФ Frequency Response by Setting $z = e^{j\omega}$ Substitute $z = e^{j\omega T_s}$ (and for simplicity take $T_s = 1$ so that $\omega$ is the normalised digital radian frequency in $[-\pi, \pi]$): $$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega N}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}$$ ### Step 5 тАФ Simplify Using Euler's Identity Use $1 - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin(\theta/2)\,e^{-j\theta/2}$. Applying this to numerator and denominator: $$\begin{aligned} 1 - e^{-j\omega N} &= 2j\sin\!\left(\frac{\omega N}{2}\right)\,e^{-j\omega N/2} \\[4pt] 1 - e^{-j\omega} &= 2j\sin\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)\,e^{-j\omega/2} \end{aligned}$$ Therefore: $$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega N / 2)}{\sin(\omega / 2)} \cdot e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}$$ ### Step 6 тАФ Magnitude Response The magnitude is the **aliased Dirichlet kernel**: $$\vert H(e^{j\omega})\vert = \frac{1}{N}\cdot \left\vert \frac{\sin(\omega N / 2)}{\sin(\omega / 2)} \right\vert$$ **Physical interpretation of zeros:** Setting $\sin(\omega N / 2) = 0$ gives zeros at: $$\omega_k = \frac{2\pi k}{N}, \qquad k = 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$ These uniformly spaced zeros on the unit circle are precisely why the MA filter strongly attenuates high frequencies. > [!NOTE] > **At $\omega = 0$** (DC): Use L'H├┤pital's rule to obtain $\vert H(e^{j0})\vert = 1$ тАФ the DC gain is unity (signal passes through). **At $\omega = \pi$** (highest frequency): $\vert H(e^{j\pi})\vert = \dfrac{1}{N}\cdot\dfrac{1}{\sin(\pi/2)} = \dfrac{1}{N}$ тАФ strong attenuation. This confirms low-pass behaviour. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 тАФ Cutoff Frequency of the First-Order IIR High-Pass Filter **Goal:** Show that the choice $\alpha = \dfrac{\tau}{\tau + T_s}$ gives a $-3\text{ dB}$ cutoff at $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau}$. ### Starting Difference Equation $$y[n] = \alpha\,y[n-1] + \alpha\,x[n] - \alpha\,x[n-1]$$ ### Step 1 тАФ Apply Z-Transform $$Y(z) = \alpha\,z^{-1} Y(z) + \alpha\,X(z) - \alpha\,z^{-1} X(z)$$ ### Step 2 тАФ Solve for $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$ Rearrange to isolate $Y(z)$: $$Y(z)\,\bigl[1 - \alpha\,z^{-1}\bigr] = \alpha\,X(z)\,\bigl[1 - z^{-1}\bigr]$$ $$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\alpha\,(1 - z^{-1})}{1 - \alpha\,z^{-1}}$$ ### Step 3 тАФ Substitute $z = e^{j\omega}$ $$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\alpha\,(1 - e^{-j\omega})}{1 - \alpha\,e^{-j\omega}}$$ ### Step 4 тАФ Compute Magnitude Squared $$\vert H(e^{j\omega})\vert^{2} = \frac{\alpha^{2}\,\vert 1 - e^{-j\omega}\vert^{2}}{\vert 1 - \alpha e^{-j\omega}\vert^{2}}$$ Using $\vert 1 - e^{-j\omega}\vert^{2} = 2 - 2\cos\omega = 4\sin^{2}(\omega/2)$ and similarly for the denominator: $$\vert H(e^{j\omega})\vert^{2} = \frac{4\alpha^{2}\sin^{2}(\omega/2)}{1 - 2\alpha\cos\omega + \alpha^{2}}$$ ### Step 5 тАФ Find the $-3\text{ dB}$ Cutoff At DC ($\omega \to 0$): numerator $\to 0$, so $\vert H\vert \to 0$ тАФ perfect high-pass behaviour, baseline drift blocked. At high frequency ($\omega = \pi$): $\sin^{2}(\pi/2) = 1$, $\cos\pi = -1$: $$\vert H(e^{j\pi})\vert^{2} = \frac{4\alpha^{2}}{(1+\alpha)^{2}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \vert H\vert = \frac{2\alpha}{1+\alpha}$$ The $-3\text{ dB}$ cutoff is defined by the continuous-time analogy. Matching the discrete IIR to the continuous one-pole high-pass $H(s) = \dfrac{s\tau}{1 + s\tau}$: $$H(s)\big\vert_{s=j\omega_c} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_c = \frac{1}{\tau} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi\tau}$$ For typical ECG lab requirement $f_c = 0.5\text{ Hz}$: $$\tau = \frac{1}{2\pi(0.5)} \approx 0.318\text{ s}$$ With $f_s = 360\text{ Hz}$ giving $T_s \approx 2.78\text{ ms}$: $$\alpha = \frac{\tau}{\tau + T_s} = \frac{0.318}{0.318 + 0.00278} \approx 0.9913$$ This is the **standard KTU lab value** you should remember. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 тАФ SNR Improvement of a Moving-Average Filter **Goal:** Show that a length-$N$ MA filter improves white-noise SNR by $10\log_{10}(N)$ dB. ### Setup Let $x[n] = s[n] + \eta[n]$, where $s[n]$ is the deterministic signal and $\eta[n]$ is zero-mean white noise with variance $\sigma^{2}$. ### Step 1 тАФ Output Noise Variance $$y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \eta[n-k]$$ Since the noise samples are independent: $$\sigma_{y}^{2} = \text{Var}(y) = \frac{1}{N^{2}}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\sigma^{2} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{N}$$ ### Step 2 тАФ SNR Comparison $$\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \frac{\text{Var}(s)}{\sigma_{y}^{2}} = N\cdot\frac{\text{Var}(s)}{\sigma^{2}} = N\cdot\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}$$ In decibels: $$\Delta\text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}}\right) = 10\log_{10}(N)\text{ dB}$$ **Example:** $N = 16$ тЖТ $\Delta\text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}(16) \approx 12\text{ dB}$ improvement. > [!TIP] > Doubling the window length $N$ gains only **$10\log_{10}(2) \approx 3\text{ dB}$**, but doubles the signal lag. KTU examiners love this trade-off question. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix тАФ Choosing the Right Filter | Signal Problem | Best Time-Domain Filter | $N$ or $\alpha$ Hint | Trade-off | |:---------------|:-----------------------:|:--------------------:|:----------| | Severe baseline drift | 1st-order HP IIR | $\alpha \approx 0.99$ | Ringing on sharp edges | | Small baseline drift + preserve QRS | MA-subtraction HPF | $M \approx 200$ samples at $f_s=360$ Hz | Computational cost | | 50 Hz PLI | Notch (IIR) or $N=7$ MA at $f_s=360$ Hz | тАФ | None for MA | | Random HF noise | Uniform MA | $N=5\text{ to }15$ | Smoothing of sharp peaks | | Need to preserve peaks | Weighted MA (Gaussian) | $\sigma \approx 3$ | Slightly weaker noise reduction | | Real-time DSP | Recursive MA | Any $N$, $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Memory of $N$ samples | --- # SECTION 4 тАФ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied All node IDs are alphanumeric (`sigIn`, `hpf`, `lpf`, etc.) and labels contain no markdown formatting characters. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Two-Stage Time-Domain Filter ```mermaid flowchart LR rawSig["RAW BIO SIGNAL
x of n with
Baseline Drift + HF Noise"] adc["ADC
fs equal 360 Hz
Quantisation"] hpfIn["x of n
Discrete Samples"] hpf{"STAGE 1
HIGH PASS IIR
alpha approx 0.99
fc approx 0.5 Hz"} baseEst["ESTIMATED BASELINE
b of n from MA"] subNode(("Subtraction Node")) hpfOut["DRIFT FREE SIGNAL
d of n"] lpf{"STAGE 2
MOVING AVERAGE LPF
N equals 7 to 15"} lpfOut["CLEANED SIGNAL
y of n"] display["Display and
Storage"] rawSig --> adc --> hpfIn hpfIn --> hpf hpfIn --> baseEst baseEst --> subNode hpf --> subNode subNode --> hpfOut hpfOut --> lpf lpf --> lpfOut lpfOut --> display ``` ## 4.3 Signal Flow тАФ Recursive Moving Average Implementation ```mermaid flowchart TB xN["Input x of n"] xNm["Delayed x of n minus N"] sum1(("Add")) div1["Divide by N"] delta["Delta = x of n minus x of n minus N"] memY["Memory y of n minus 1"] yN["Output y of n"] xN --> sum1 xNm --> sum1 sum1 --> div1 div1 --> yN div1 --> memY memY --> sum1 xN --> delta xNm --> delta delta --> div1 ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagram shows the **order-N recursive MA** running in just three arithmetic operations per sample тАФ the classic $\mathcal{O}(1)$ digital filter implementation demanded in KTU viva questions. ## 4.4 Frequency-Band Separation Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR fAxis["FREQUENCY AXIS f in Hz"] lowBand["LOW BAND
0 to 0.5 Hz
BASELINE DRIFT
Blocked by HPF"] infoBand["INFORMATION BAND
0.5 Hz to 40 Hz
ECG EEG SIGNAL
Preserved"] midBand["MID BAND
40 to 50 Hz
Transition"] highBand["HIGH BAND
above 50 Hz
HF NOISE and PLI
Blocked by LPF"] fAxis --> lowBand fAxis --> infoBand fAxis --> midBand fAxis --> highBand ``` ## 4.5 ECG Pre-Processing Pipeline (Lab Reference) ```mermaid flowchart TB patient["Patient Electrodes"] amp["Instrumentation
Amplifier
Gain 1000"] hpf1["Analog HPF
fc equals 0.05 Hz"] lpf1["Analog LPF
fc equals 150 Hz"] adcLab["ADC
fs equals 360 Hz"] digHPF["Digital HPF
Time Domain
alpha approx 0.99"] digLPF["Digital MA LPF
Time Domain
N equals 8"] storage["Lab PC Storage
and Display"] patient --> amp --> hpf1 --> lpf1 --> adcLab --> digHPF --> digLPF --> storage ``` > [!TIP] > Note that **analog hardware filtering happens first** (anti-aliasing and basic band-limiting), and the **digital time-domain filtering is the final refinement** before display. This is the typical KTU lab record-diagram expected in your practical record. --- # SECTION 5 тАФ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024, Model Paper]** Define **baseline drift** in a bioelectric signal. List any four sources and state the typical frequency range. **Model Answer:** > Baseline drift is the slow, low-frequency deviation of a recorded bioelectric signal (e.g., ECG, EEG) from its true iso-electric baseline, appearing as an unwanted wandering of the trace. > > **Four sources:** > 1. Respiration-induced electrode movement ($\approx 0.15$тАУ$0.5$ Hz). > 2. Electrode-skin half-cell potential drift. > 3. Patient body movement and perspiration. > 4. Thermal drift in the analog front-end amplifier. > > **Typical frequency range:** approximately **$0\text{ Hz}$ to $0.5\text{ Hz}$**, well below the information band of ECG/EEG. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023, Supplementary]** Explain how a **moving-average filter** of length $N$ removes high-frequency noise. State the SNR gain achieved over white noise. **Model Answer:** > A moving-average filter computes each output sample as the arithmetic mean of the current and previous $N-1$ samples: > $$y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]$$ > High-frequency noise oscillates rapidly, so positive and negative noise excursions cancel when averaged, while the slowly varying signal component remains almost unchanged within the window. Hence the noise variance is reduced by factor $N$, while the signal is preserved. > > **SNR gain** (in dB): > $$\Delta\text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}(N)\,\text{dB}$$ > For $N=16$, this is $12\text{ dB}$. --- ## 5.2 Part B тАФ Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) тАФ 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024, Main Paper, Module 2]** **(a)** Derive the transfer function $H(z)$ of an $N$-point moving-average filter and obtain an expression for its magnitude response. **[7 marks]** **(b)** An ECG signal sampled at $f_s = 360$ Hz contains severe baseline wander. Design a first-order IIR high-pass filter with $-3$ dB cutoff at $f_c = 0.5$ Hz and write the difference equation. Compute the value of $\alpha$. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **(a)** [Full derivation given in Section 3.1, steps 1тАУ6 of these notes] Key results to write: $$H(z) = \frac{1}{N}\cdot\frac{1 - z^{-N}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$ $$\vert H(e^{j\omega})\vert = \frac{1}{N}\cdot\left\vert \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}\right\vert$$ with zeros at $\omega_k = 2\pi k / N$ and DC gain unity. **(b)** The first-order IIR high-pass difference equation is: $$y[n] = \alpha\,y[n-1] + \alpha\bigl(x[n] - x[n-1]\bigr)$$ with $\alpha = \dfrac{\tau}{\tau + T_s}$. Required cutoff $f_c = 0.5$ Hz gives: $$\tau = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c} = \frac{1}{2\pi(0.5)} = 0.3183 \text{ s}$$ Sampling period at $f_s = 360$ Hz: $$T_s = \frac{1}{360} = 2.778\times10^{-3}\text{ s}$$ Therefore: $$\alpha = \frac{0.3183}{0.3183 + 0.002778} = \frac{0.3183}{0.3211} \approx 0.9913$$ So the final difference equation is: $$y[n] = 0.9913\,y[n-1] + 0.9913\bigl(x[n] - x[n-1]\bigr)$$ --- ### Question B (Choice 2) тАФ 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023, Main Paper, Module 2]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the two-stage time-domain filtering cascade used for simultaneous removal of baseline drift and high-frequency noise from a noisy ECG signal. **[7 marks]** **(b)** For a moving-average filter of length $N = 16$ operating on white noise with input variance $\sigma^{2} = 0.04$, compute the output noise variance and the SNR improvement in dB. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **(a)** The cascade is **High-Pass Filter (Stage 1) тЖТ Low-Pass Filter (Stage 2)**. - **Stage 1 тАФ High-pass IIR (baseline removal):** $$y_1[n] = \alpha\,y_1[n-1] + \alpha\bigl(x[n] - x[n-1]\bigr)$$ Removes frequencies below $\approx 0.5$ Hz. - **Stage 2 тАФ Moving-average low-pass (HF noise removal):** $$y_2[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} y_1[n-k]$$ Smooths high-frequency components. **Block diagram** (draw the cascade H тЖТ L with intermediate signal $d[n]$): ``` x[n] тЖТ [H] тЖТ d[n] тЖТ [L] тЖТ y[n] ``` **(b)** Output noise variance of the MA filter: $$\sigma_{y}^{2} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{N} = \frac{0.04}{16} = 2.5\times10^{-3}$$ SNR improvement (in dB): $$\Delta\text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}(N) = 10\log_{10}(16) = 10 \times 1.2041 \approx 12.04 \text{ dB}$$ So the SNR improves by approximately **$12.04$ dB**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps тАФ Read Before Writing the Exam!** > > 1. **Order of cascade reversed.** Many students write the LPF first, then HPF. This *will* lose marks. The correct order is **HPF first, then LPF** тАФ otherwise the LPF smears the drift into the signal. > 2. **Confusing the cutoff formula.** Students often write $f_c = 1/\tau$ instead of $f_c = 1/(2\pi\tau)$. The $2\pi$ is the trap. > 3. **Forgetting the normalisation.** For the MA filter, the coefficients must sum to **$1/N$** each, not just sum to $1$. Wrong normalisation alters the DC gain. > 4. **Wrong SNR formula.** Use $\Delta\text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}(N)$ **only for white noise**. For coloured noise the formula is different тАФ examiners dock marks if you state the wrong applicability. > 5. **Forgetting to specify units and the value of $\alpha$ to 3тАУ4 decimal places.** Round-off errors in $\alpha$ propagate; always show the substitution. > 6. **Writing time-domain filter code blocks** in a paper exam. KTU PCEBL508 is a lab, but theory questions require derivation, not code. > 7. **Not mentioning zero-phase vs causal filtering.** When asked "preserve the QRS complex", mention the trade-off: causal IIR has phase delay; zero-phase (filtfilt-style forward-backward) filtering removes it but is non-causal. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Bio-signals are low-amplitude, low-frequency** тАФ easily corrupted by drift and HF noise. - **Baseline drift** = low-frequency wander below $\approx 0.5$ Hz; sources are respiration, electrode-skin, body movement, thermal drift. - **High-frequency noise** = PLI ($50$/$60$ Hz), EMG ($>20$ Hz), thermal, EMI. - **Time-domain filtering** = directly combines samples without FFT тАФ fast for real-time use. - **Two-stage cascade:** **HPF (drift removal) тЖТ LPF (HF noise removal)**. - **Moving average (MA) length-$N$ filter:** $y[n] = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}x[n-k]$. - **Recursive MA:** $y[n] = y[n-1] + \dfrac{x[n]-x[n-N]}{N}$ тАФ $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per sample. - **MA transfer function:** $H(z) = \dfrac{1 - z^{-N}}{N(1 - z^{-1})}$, magnitude $\dfrac{1}{N}\left\vert \dfrac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} \right\vert$. - **1st-order IIR HPF (baseline):** $y[n] = \alpha y[n-1] + \alpha(x[n] - x[n-1])$, $\alpha = \tau/(\tau+T_s)$, $f_c = 1/(2\pi\tau)$. - **SNR gain of MA:** $10\log_{10}(N)$ dB for white noise. - **Standard KTU lab ECG:** $f_s = 360$ Hz, $\alpha \approx 0.9913$ for $f_c = 0.5$ Hz, $N \approx 8$ for LPF. - **Always state the order** HPF тЖТ LPF in any cascade answer. - **Doubling $N$** adds only $\approx 3$ dB SNR but doubles latency. - **FIR filters are always stable** (no feedback), **IIR filters need pole-stability check** ($\vert \alpha\vert < 1$). - **Zero-phase filtering** (forward-backward) avoids QRS distortion but is non-causal. - **Validation of filtering:** always overlay the original and filtered signal, compute RMSE, and inspect the residual signal тАФ these are standard KTU lab report sections. ---

topics.title: Power line interference rejection from bio signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL508 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Power line interference rejection from bio signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Power Line Interference (PLI)** is a narrow-band, sinusoidal contamination artifact that corrupts biomedical signals (such as ECG, EEG, EMG, and EOG) due to electromagnetic coupling from the alternating current (AC) mains supply. In India, the fundamental interference occurs at **50 Hz**, while in North America it is **60 Hz**, often accompanied by odd-order harmonics (e.g., 150 Hz, 250 Hz).

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, this experiment is formally classified under the domain of **Adaptive and Non-Adaptive Filtering for Bio-signal Denoising**, where students are required to design, simulate, and evaluate filters capable of suppressing PLI while preserving the diagnostic morphology of the underlying physiological signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEBL508 - Module 2):**
> *"Design and implementation of digital filters (IIR/FIR Notch and Adaptive LMS) for the cancellation of 50/60 Hz power line interference in ECG/EEG signals, and evaluation using SNR, MSE, and spectral analysis."*

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are recording your friend's voice using a mobile phone in a room where an old ceiling fan is running. The fan motor creates a constant low-pitched **"hummmm"** that mixes with your friend's speech. The voice you want is the *bio-signal*; the fan's hum is the *power line interference*.

Now, to recover your friend's clean voice, you have **two practical strategies**:

1. **Strategy A тАФ Notch Filter (Static Surgery):** Use a surgical tool (a narrow band-stop filter) to "cut out" exactly the fan's hum frequency while leaving everything else untouched. This works *only* if the fan hum stays at a fixed pitch.
2. **Strategy B тАФ Adaptive Canceller (Smart Headphones):** Wear smart headphones with a microphone on the *outside* of the cup that picks up *only* the fan hum (the reference noise). The headphone circuitry then *subtracts* this hum from what your ear hears, leaving only the friend's voice. This works *even if* the fan speed changes (frequency drift).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Mapping for Engineering Students:**
> - Fan hum тЖТ **50 Hz PLI**
> - Friend's voice тЖТ **ECG/EEG waveform**
> - Strategy A тЖТ **IIR/FIR Notch Filter**
> - Strategy B тЖТ **Adaptive LMS Noise Canceller**

---

### 1.3 Sources and Characteristics of PLI

| Source of Interference | Coupling Mechanism | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| AC Mains Wiring (50/60 Hz) | Capacitive / Inductive coupling | 0.5 mV тАУ 5 mV |
| Fluorescent Lamps | Harmonic-rich electromagnetic field | Up to 10 mV |
| Medical Equipment Leakage | Improper grounding loops | Highly variable |
| Patient Cable Motion | Triboelectric effect | Transient bursts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Quantitative Fact:** The standard ECG signal amplitude is approximately **1 mV**, and the QRS complex spans roughly **1тАУ2 Hz bandwidth** diagnostically. A 50 Hz PLI of even **50 ┬╡V** can completely obscure low-amplitude features like the **P-wave** and **T-wave**, making automated diagnosis impossible without cancellation.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standards

- **Standard Indian Mains Frequency:** $f_0 = 50$ **Hz**
- **Normalized Digital Frequency:** $\omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi f_0}{F_s}$, where $F_s$ is the sampling rate
- **Required Q-factor for a typical PLI notch:** $Q = 25$ to $50$ (sharpness)
- **Minimum SNR Improvement Target:** $\geq 20$ **dB** (industry benchmark for diagnostic-grade ECG)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Philosophy of PLI Removal

The entire architecture of PLI suppression rests on **three pillars** evaluated in the KTU lab:

**Pillar 1 тАФ Frequency Selectivity**
Since PLI is a narrow-band tone at a known frequency ($f_0 = 50$ Hz), the most efficient removal is a *band-stop* (notch) filter centered exactly at $\omega_0$.

**Pillar 2 тАФ Minimal Signal Distortion**
The diagnostic content of the bio-signal (QRS complex, alpha rhythm, etc.) must remain unaltered. This forces the filter to have a *very high Q-factor* and *linear phase* (for FIR variants).

**Pillar 3 тАФ Robustness to Frequency Drift**
Real-world mains frequency drifts by $\pm 0.5$ Hz. A *static* notch filter may become misaligned; hence *adaptive* methods are used in clinical-grade equipment.

---

### 2.2 The Three Major Filter Architectures

#### A. IIR Notch Filter (Second-Order)
Designed by placing **zeros on the unit circle** at the PLI frequency and **poles inside the unit circle** close to those zeros to control bandwidth.

**Transfer Function:**

$$H_{IIR}(z) = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^{2}z^{-2}}$$

where $r$ is the pole radius ($0 < r < 1$), with $r \to 1$ giving a sharper notch.

#### B. FIR Notch Filter
Designed by convolving two all-pass sections with a phase shift of $\pi$ at $\omega_0$. The simplest FIR notch is:

$$H_{FIR}(z) = \frac{1 + z^{-2}}{2} - \cos(\omega_0)\, z^{-1}$$

For a higher-order FIR (e.g., 64-tap), the impulse response is obtained by windowing the ideal frequency response with a Hamming or Blackman window.

#### C. Adaptive LMS Noise Canceller (Widrow-Hoff Architecture)
The bio-signal $s(n)$ is corrupted by PLI $n_0(n)$. A **reference input** $x(n)$ is taken from the mains line (via a separate sensor). The adaptive filter $W(z)$ adjusts its coefficients to *estimate* $n_0(n)$ from $x(n)$, and the estimated noise is *subtracted* from the primary input $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$.

The **LMS Update Equation:**

$$w_k(n+1) = w_k(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\, x(n-k)$$

where $\mu$ is the step-size (convergence parameter) and $e(n)$ is the error signal.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| 3 | $BW = \pi (1-r)$ (approx.) | тИТ3 dB bandwidth of notch | rad/sample | тнРтнР Medium |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real Clinical Equipment

- **Patient Monitors (Philips, GE):** Use adaptive 50 Hz notch filters in hardware DSP chips.
- **Holter Recorders (24-hr ECG):** Rely on LMS-based PLI removal because patient environment is uncontrolled.
- **EEG Systems:** Multi-stage filtering: 0.5 Hz HPF тЖТ 50 Hz Notch тЖТ 30 Hz LPF.
- **Implantable Pacemakers:** Use ultra-low-power analog notch filters to avoid microprocessor battery drain.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: IIR Notch Filter Transfer Function

**Goal:** Derive the transfer function of a second-order IIR notch filter from the pole-zero placement principle.

**Step 1 тАФ Place the zeros:**
To create a null at $\omega_0$, place two complex-conjugate zeros on the unit circle at angles $\pm \omega_0$:

$$z_1 = e^{j\omega_0}, \quad z_2 = e^{-j\omega_0}$$

The numerator polynomial is:

$$N(z) = (z - e^{j\omega_0})(z - e^{-j\omega_0}) = z^2 - 2\cos(\omega_0) z + 1$$

In terms of $z^{-1}$:

$$N(z^{-1}) = 1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + z^{-2}$$

**Step 2 тАФ Place the poles:**
To control the notch sharpness, place two complex-conjugate poles inside the unit circle at radius $r$ (where $0 < r < 1$):

$$p_1 = r e^{j\omega_0}, \quad p_2 = r e^{-j\omega_0}$$

The denominator polynomial is:

$$D(z) = (z - r e^{j\omega_0})(z - r e^{-j\omega_0}) = z^2 - 2r\cos(\omega_0) z + r^2$$

In terms of $z^{-1}$:

$$D(z^{-1}) = 1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}$$

**Step 3 тАФ Form the transfer function:**

$$H_{IIR}(z) = \frac{N(z^{-1})}{D(z^{-1})} = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^{2}z^{-2}}$$

This completes the derivation. The value $r$ governs the bandwidth:

$$BW_{-3dB} \approx \pi(1 - r) \quad \text{rad/sample}$$

For $r = 0.95$, $BW \approx 0.157$ rad/sample, giving a sharp notch suitable for clinical ECG.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: LMS Adaptive Algorithm Convergence

**Goal:** Show that the LMS algorithm minimizes the Mean Squared Error (MSE) of the noise estimate.

**Step 1 тАФ Define the error signal:**
The primary input to the adaptive canceller is:

$$d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$$

where $s(n)$ is the clean bio-signal and $n_0(n)$ is the PLI. The adaptive filter output is $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k(n)\, x(n-k)$. The error is:

$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = s(n) + n_0(n) - \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k(n)\, x(n-k)$$

**Step 2 тАФ Define the cost function (MSE):**

$$J(n) = E[e^{2}(n)]$$

**Step 3 тАФ Apply gradient descent:**
The optimum weights are found by moving in the direction of the negative gradient:

$$w_k(n+1) = w_k(n) - \mu \frac{\partial J(n)}{\partial w_k(n)}$$

**Step 4 тАФ Instantaneous estimate of gradient:**
Since the true expectation $E[\cdot]$ is unknown, the LMS algorithm uses the instantaneous estimate:

$$\frac{\partial e^{2}(n)}{\partial w_k(n)} = -2 e(n) x(n-k)$$

**Step 5 тАФ Substitute and simplify:**

$$\begin{aligned}
w_k(n+1) &= w_k(n) - \mu \left[ -2 e(n) x(n-k) \right] \\
w_k(n+1) &= w_k(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\, x(n-k)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the canonical LMS weight update equation. The stability bound for $\mu$ is:

$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{N \cdot P_x}$$

where $N$ is the filter order and $P_x$ is the input signal power.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of PLI Removal Methods

| Parameter | IIR Notch (2nd Order) | FIR Notch (3-tap) | Adaptive LMS (32-tap) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Computational Cost | Very Low (5 mults/sample) | Very Low (2 mults/sample) | High ($N$ mults/sample) |
| Frequency Tracking | No (Static) | No (Static) | Yes (Dynamic) |
| SNR Improvement (typical ECG) | 18 тАУ 25 dB | 12 тАУ 18 dB | 25 тАУ 35 dB |
| Transient Response | Slow (phase distortion) | Fast (Linear Phase) | Adaptive |
| Robustness to Drift | Poor | Poor | Excellent |
| Signal Distortion | Moderate (QRS ringing) | Minimal | Minimal |
| KTU Lab Implementation | Mandatory | Mandatory | Mandatory |
| Memory Required | 2 delay elements | $N$ taps | $N$ taps + state |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Logical Trap:** Students often state that "FIR notch is always better than IIR notch." This is **FALSE**. FIR has linear phase but lower selectivity per tap. IIR achieves sharper notches with fewer coefficients but introduces nonlinear phase distortion. The correct answer is *application-dependent* тАФ for **ECG** (where QRS shape is critical), adaptive or FIR is preferred; for **EEG** (where narrow-band noise dominates), IIR is preferred.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** An ECG is sampled at $F_s = 500$ Hz. Design a second-order IIR notch filter to remove 50 Hz PLI with $r = 0.95$.

**Solution:**

$$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi \times 50}{500} = \frac{\pi}{5} = 0.2\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

Compute $\cos(\omega_0) = \cos(0.2\pi) = \cos(36┬░) \approx 0.8090$.

The transfer function becomes:

$$H(z) = \frac{1 - 2(0.8090)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2(0.95)(0.8090)z^{-1} + (0.95)^{2}z^{-2}}$$

Simplifying numerically:

$$H(z) = \frac{1 - 1.6180\, z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 1.5371\, z^{-1} + 0.9025\, z^{-2}}$$

The тИТ3 dB bandwidth is $BW \approx \pi(1 - 0.95) = 0.157$ rad/sample, i.e., approximately 25 Hz wide тАФ a sharp, clinically acceptable notch.

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Adaptive LMS Noise Canceller for PLI Removal

This is the **gold-standard architecture** for clinical PLI suppression, attributed to Widrow and Hoff (1975).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ECG Primary Input d of n equals s of n plus n0 of n] --> SUM[Adder Node]
    B[Mains Reference x of n] --> AF[Adaptive Filter W of z adjustable weights]
    AF --> Y[Noise Estimate y of n]
    Y --> SUB[Subtract Node]
    SUM --> SUB
    SUM2[Clean Bio-signal s of n contaminated input] --> SUM
    SUB --> E[Error Signal e of n drives the LMS update]
    E --> LMS[LMS Algorithm mu step size]
    LMS --> AF

    style AF fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00
    style LMS fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style OUT fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
```

**Operational Flow Explained:**
1. The contaminated ECG enters the summer from the patient cable.
2. The mains reference (50 Hz tone) is fed into the adaptive filter.
3. The filter estimates the noise replica using LMS-updated weights.
4. The estimated noise is subtracted from the contaminated input.
5. The error signal (ideally equal to the clean ECG) is fed back to update the weights.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is power line interference in biomedical signals, and what is its typical frequency in India?**

**Model Answer:**
Power line interference (PLI) is a sinusoidal contamination artifact introduced into biomedical signals due to electromagnetic coupling from the AC mains supply. It is caused by capacitive and inductive coupling between the patient cables and the surrounding power wiring, as well as by ground loops in medical equipment. In India, the fundamental PLI frequency is **50 Hz**, accompanied by odd-order harmonics (150 Hz, 250 Hz, etc.). PLI amplitudes typically range from **0.5 mV to 5 mV**, which can completely mask low-amplitude diagnostic features like the P-wave and T-wave of an ECG. **(3 marks)**

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the LMS weight update equation and explain the role of the step-size parameter $\mu$.**

**Model Answer:**
The LMS (Least Mean Squares) weight update equation is:

$$w_k(n+1) = w_k(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\, x(n-k)$$

where $w_k(n)$ is the $k$-th filter weight at time $n$, $e(n)$ is the error signal, $x(n-k)$ is the reference input, and $\mu$ is the **step-size** (convergence parameter). The role of $\mu$ is to control the **speed-vs-stability trade-off**:
- **Large $\mu$** тЖТ fast convergence but high steady-state error and possible instability.
- **Small $\mu$** тЖТ slow convergence but low steady-state error and high stability.

The stability bound is $0 < \mu < 1/(N \cdot P_x)$, where $N$ is the filter order and $P_x$ is the input signal power. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each тАФ Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a second-order IIR notch filter using the pole-zero placement method. Clearly state the role of the pole-radius parameter $r$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 тАФ Define the objective:**
We require a filter $H(z)$ such that $|H(e^{j\omega_0})| = 0$ at the PLI frequency $\omega_0$, and $|H(e^{j\omega})| = 1$ elsewhere.

**Step 2 тАФ Place the zeros:**
Set $z_1 = e^{j\omega_0}$ and $z_2 = e^{-j\omega_0}$ on the unit circle.

$$N(z) = z^2 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z + 1 \implies N(z^{-1}) = 1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + z^{-2}$$

**Step 3 тАФ Place the poles:**
Set $p_1 = re^{j\omega_0}$ and $p_2 = re^{-j\omega_0}$ inside the unit circle ($0 < r < 1$).

$$D(z) = z^2 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z + r^2 \implies D(z^{-1}) = 1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}$$

**Step 4 тАФ Form the transfer function:**

$$H(z) = \frac{N(z^{-1})}{D(z^{-1})} = \frac{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos(\omega_0)z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}$$

**Step 5 тАФ Role of $r$:**
The parameter $r$ controls the **тИТ3 dB bandwidth** of the notch. As $r \to 1$, the poles approach the zeros, the notch becomes infinitely sharp, and the filter has high $Q$-factor. The relationship is:

$$BW_{-3dB} \approx \pi(1 - r) \quad \text{rad/sample}$$

For $r = 0.95$, $BW \approx 0.157$ rad/sample (тЙИ 25 Hz), suitable for clinical ECG. **(7 marks тАФ full marks distributed: Step 1 = 1, Step 2 = 2, Step 3 = 2, Step 4 = 1, Step 5 = 1)**

---

**(b)** With reference to the Widrow-Hoff adaptive noise canceller, explain how PLI is removed from an ECG signal. Draw the block diagram and write the LMS update equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** (See Section 4.1 above тАФ adaptive canceller with primary input, reference input, adaptive filter, summer, and LMS update loop.)

**Step 1 тАФ Signal Model:**
Let $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$ be the contaminated ECG, where $s(n)$ is the clean signal and $n_0(n)$ is the 50 Hz PLI. Let $x(n)$ be a reference signal correlated with $n_0(n)$ but uncorrelated with $s(n)$. This reference is typically taken from the mains supply via a dedicated sensor.

**Step 2 тАФ Adaptive Filtering:**
The adaptive filter $W(z)$ produces an estimate $\hat{n}_0(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k(n) x(n-k)$ of the PLI component.

**Step 3 тАФ Noise Cancellation:**
The estimated noise is subtracted from the primary input:

$$e(n) = d(n) - \hat{n}_0(n) = s(n) + n_0(n) - \hat{n}_0(n)$$

When the filter converges, $\hat{n}_0(n) \approx n_0(n)$, so $e(n) \approx s(n)$ тАФ the clean ECG.

**Step 4 тАФ LMS Update Equation:**

$$w_k(n+1) = w_k(n) + 2\mu e(n) x(n-k)$$

The step-size $\mu$ is chosen such that $0 < \mu < 1/(N P_x)$ for guaranteed stability.

**Step 5 тАФ Key Advantage:**
Unlike a static notch filter, the adaptive canceller **tracks frequency drift** in the mains and is robust to amplitude variations, making it the **gold standard** in patient monitors and Holter recorders. **(7 marks тАФ full marks: Block diagram = 2, Steps 1тАУ3 = 3, Step 4 = 1, Step 5 = 1)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare IIR notch filter, FIR notch filter, and adaptive LMS filter for PLI removal in ECG, with respect to (i) phase response, (ii) frequency tracking, and (iii) SNR improvement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Comparison Criterion | IIR Notch (2nd Order) | FIR Notch (3-tap) | Adaptive LMS (32-tap) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Phase Response** | Non-linear (phase distortion near notch) | **Linear phase** (no distortion) | Approximately linear in passband |
| **(ii) Frequency Tracking** | **None** (fixed at $\omega_0$) | None (fixed) | **Yes** (tracks drift) |
| **(iii) SNR Improvement** | 18 тАУ 25 dB | 12 тАУ 18 dB | **25 тАУ 35 dB** |

**Conclusion:** For short-duration clinical recordings with stable mains, the IIR notch is computationally optimal. For diagnostic ECG where QRS morphology is critical, the adaptive LMS is preferred due to its high SNR improvement and drift tracking. The FIR notch is rarely used standalone but is valuable when linear phase is mandatory (e.g., in feature extraction pipelines). **(7 marks тАФ 1.5 marks per comparison column + 2.5 marks for table + conclusion)**

---

**(b)** An ECG is sampled at $F_s = 250$ Hz. Design a second-order IIR notch filter to remove 50 Hz PLI with pole radius $r = 0.9$. Compute the filter coefficients and the тИТ3 dB bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 тАФ Normalize the notch frequency:**

$$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi f_0}{F_s} = \frac{2\pi \times 50}{250} = \frac{2\pi}{5} = 0.4\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

**Step 2 тАФ Compute $\cos(\omega_0)$:**

$$\cos(0.4\pi) = \cos(72┬░) \approx 0.3090$$

**Step 3 тАФ Numerator coefficients:**

$$b_0 = 1, \quad b_1 = -2\cos(\omega_0) = -0.6180, \quad b_2 = 1$$

**Step 4 тАФ Denominator coefficients:**

$$a_1 = -2r\cos(\omega_0) = -2(0.9)(0.3090) = -0.5562$$
$$a_2 = r^2 = (0.9)^2 = 0.81$$

**Step 5 тАФ Transfer function:**

$$H(z) = \frac{1 - 0.6180\, z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 0.5562\, z^{-1} + 0.81\, z^{-2}}$$

**Step 6 тАФ Compute тИТ3 dB bandwidth:**

$$BW_{-3dB} \approx \pi(1 - r) = \pi(0.1) = 0.1\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

Converting to Hz:

$$BW_{Hz} = \frac{0.1\pi \times 250}{2\pi} = 12.5 \text{ Hz}$$

This 12.5 Hz notch is wider than the previous example, suitable when some tolerance to mains drift (up to ┬▒6 Hz) is acceptable. **(7 marks тАФ Step 1: 1, Step 2: 1, Step 3: 1, Step 4: 1, Step 5: 1, Step 6: 2)**
