### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing $f_0$ with $\omega_0$:** Students frequently write the transfer function using $f_0$ directly. Always convert to normalized digital frequency $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / F_s$ *before* computing $\cos(\omega_0)$. **(тИТ2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the stability bound for $\mu$:** Writing the LMS equation without specifying $0 < \mu < 1/(N P_x)$ is treated as an incomplete answer. **(тИТ1 mark)**
> 3. **Wrong sign in LMS update:** The correct sign is **positive** ($+$), not negative. The minus sign in the gradient descent derivation cancels with the negative gradient of MSE. **(тИТ2 marks)**
> 4. **Not stating that $0 < r < 1$:** Pole-radius $r$ must be strictly less than 1 for stability. Omitting this is a frequent error. **(тИТ1 mark)**
> 5. **Confusing poles and zeros:** Zeros go on the unit circle; poles go *inside* the unit circle. Reversing these creates a *resonator* (peaking filter) instead of a notch тАФ a fatal conceptual error. **(тИТ3 marks)**
> 6. **Skipping the reference input explanation in adaptive filtering:** Many students describe LMS but forget to explain *where* the reference signal comes from (mains line sensor). This loses 1тАУ2 marks.
> 7. **Reporting SNR in linear scale instead of dB:** Always convert using $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{signal}/P_{noise})$. **(тИТ1 mark)**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ЁЯУМ **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PCEBL508 - Module 2 Experiment:**

- тнР **PLI is a sinusoidal artifact at 50 Hz (India) or 60 Hz (USA)** caused by EM coupling from AC mains.
- тнР **Three primary removal methods:** IIR Notch, FIR Notch, Adaptive LMS Canceller.
- тнР **IIR Notch transfer function** has zeros on the unit circle, poles inside at radius $r$.
- тнР **Pole radius $r$ controls bandwidth:** $BW \approx \pi(1-r)$; quality factor $Q = \omega_0 / BW$.
- тнР **LMS Update:** $w_k(n+1) = w_k(n) + 2\mu e(n) x(n-k)$; bounded by $0 < \mu < 1/(N P_x)$.
- тнР **Adaptive canceller requires a reference input** correlated with PLI but uncorrelated with the bio-signal.
- тнР **SNR Improvement** is the standard KTU performance metric: $SNR_{imp} = SNR_{out} - SNR_{in}$ (in dB).
- тнР **FIR notch has linear phase** but lower selectivity per tap compared to IIR.
- тнР **IIR notch has phase distortion** near the notch but higher Q per coefficient.
- тнР **Clinical equipment uses adaptive methods** because mains drifts by ┬▒0.5 Hz in real conditions.
- тнР **Poles inside, zeros on the unit circle** тАФ this is the cardinal rule of IIR notch design.
- тнР **For the KTU lab, simulate all three methods** in MATLAB/Python and plot input/output spectra.
- тнР **Always report SNR, MSE, and waveform plots** in your lab record тАФ these are mandatory viva questions.
- тнР **Adaptive filter length** $N$ is typically 16, 32, or 64 taps for ECG; 64 or 128 for EEG.
- тнР **Convergence time** of LMS depends on $\mu$ and eigenvalue spread of input autocorrelation matrix.
- тнР **Final Viva Question to Expect:** *"Why is the adaptive LMS preferred over static notch in Holter monitors?"* тАФ Answer: Because the mains frequency drifts and amplitude varies with patient movement, and the adaptive filter tracks these changes in real-time, providing a self-tuning cancellation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detection of Premature Ventricular Contraction in ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL508 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Detection of Premature Ventricular Contraction in ECG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ЁЯФм SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Premature Ventricular Contraction (PVC)** is a cardiac arrhythmia in which an ectopic focal point within the ventricular myocardium generates an impulse earlier than the next expected sinus beat, producing a **wide, bizarre QRS complex (>120 ms)** on the Electrocardiogram (ECG) without a preceding P wave, typically followed by a **compensatory pause**.

In the context of **Biomedical Signal Processing**, PVC detection refers to the algorithmic identification and classification of such ectopic beats from the continuous ECG time-series using a combination of **morphological analysis, temporal (R-R interval) analysis, and energy/spectral features**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCEBL508):** This experiment evaluates the student's ability to pre-process a noisy ECG signal, extract clinically meaningful features, and apply decision logic to classify normal vs. ectopic beats. The standard reference database used is the **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a perfectly synchronized orchestra led by a principal conductor (the **SA node**). Every musician (heart muscle cell) waits for the conductor's cue to play. 

Now, suppose a random violinist in the back row suddenly decides to play a loud, off-beat note just before the conductor is ready. This rogue note is **premature**, **loud**, and **sounds wrong** compared to the rest of the orchestra. After this disruption, the orchestra needs a brief silence (the **compensatory pause**) to re-sync with the conductor.

The rogue note = **PVC**. The conductor = **SA node**. The orchestra = **Ventricles**.

## 1.3 Standard Physiological Constants & Metrics

| Metric | Normal Value | PVC Value | Unit |
|--------|--------------|-----------|------|
| QRS Duration | 80 тАУ 100 | **> 120** | ms |
| Heart Rate | 60 тАУ 100 | Variable | bpm |
| R-R Interval (Rest) | 0.6 тАУ 1.0 | Shortened + Pause | s |
| PVC Coupling Interval | N/A | **< 0.45** typically | s |
| ST Segment | Isoelectric | Often Discordant | mV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Note:** PVCs are graded using the **Lown Classification** (Grade 0 to Grade 5). Frequent or complex PVCs (e.g., multifocal, R-on-T phenomenon) may indicate underlying ischemic heart disease and require clinical attention.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ЁЯУЪ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the ECG Signal тАФ Fiducial Points

The PQRST waveform components are extracted as **fiducial points** for any beat-by-beat classification algorithm:

- **P Wave**: Atrial depolarization (small, rounded, 80 ms)
- **QRS Complex**: Ventricular depolarization (tall, sharp, 80тАУ100 ms)
- **T Wave**: Ventricular repolarization (rounded, 160 ms)
- **U Wave**: Often invisible, papillary muscle repolarization

> [!TIP]
> A **PVC** is identifiable because the QRS is **wide AND has no preceding P wave** тАФ the ectopic ventricular impulse bypasses the AV node entirely.

## 2.2 The Pan-Tompkins QRS Detection Pipeline (Foundational Algorithm)

Jiapu Pan & Willis Tompkins (1985) proposed the most cited real-time QRS detector. It is the **core preprocessing** for almost every PVC detection experiment in the KTU lab.

### Stage-by-Stage Logic:

1. **Bandpass Filter (5 тАУ 15 Hz):** Isolates QRS energy, suppresses baseline wander (<5 Hz) and EMG noise (>15 Hz).
2. **Derivative Operator (5-point):** Emphasizes the steep slopes of the R wave.
3. **Squaring Function:** Makes all values positive and amplifies large differences (high-frequency content).
4. **Moving-Window Integration:** Smooths the squared signal over a 150 ms window to locate the QRS energy envelope.
5. **Adaptive Thresholding:** Uses dynamically updated thresholds (signal level + noise level) to detect R peaks.

## 2.3 PVC Detection Decision Logic

A PVC is declared when **TWO OR MORE** of these criteria are satisfied:

| # | Feature | Threshold Rule |
|---|---------|----------------|
| 1 | QRS Width | $\text{W} > 120$ ms |
| 2 | R-R Interval | $RR_{\text{current}} < 0.85 \cdot RR_{\text{avg}}$ |
| 3 | No Preceding P | P-wave absent in 200 ms window before R |
| 4 | QRS Morphology | Correlation with normal template < 0.9 |
| 5 | Compensatory Pause | $RR_{\text{post}} > 1.2 \cdot RR_{\text{avg}}$ |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description |
|--------------------|-------------|
| $y[n] = 2y[n-1] - y[n-2] + x[n] - 2x[n-6] + x[n-12]$ | Pan-Tompkins 5-point derivative |
| $y_{\text{sq}}[n] = \left( y_{\text{deriv}}[n] \right)^2$ | Squaring nonlinearity |
| $y_{\text{int}}[n] = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} y_{\text{sq}}[n-k]$ | Moving average integration |
| $\text{Thresh}_{\text{level}} = \text{NPK} + 0.25 \cdot (\text{SPK} - \text{NPK})$ | Adaptive threshold (signal/noise) |
| $\text{Corr}_{i} = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(t - \bar{t})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (t-\bar{t})^2}}$ | Template matching (Pearson) |
| $RR_{\text{ratio}} = \dfrac{RR_n}{RR_{n-1}}$ | Prematurity index |
| $\text{Wavelet Energy: } E_j = \sum_{k} \vert d_j[k] \vert^2$ | Multi-resolution analysis (Mallat) |
| $\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Detection performance metric |
| $\text{Positive Predictivity} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Detection performance metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-breaking Tip:** All `|x|` notation in formulas above uses `\vert` LaTeX delimiters to preserve the markdown table integrity тАФ never use the bare pipe character `|` inside tables.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Holter Monitors & Wearables:** Continuous 24-hr ambulatory ECG analysis.
- **ICU Bedside Monitors:** Real-time arrhythmia alarms.
- **Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillators (ICD):** Detect lethal PVC runs (Ventricular Tachycardia тЖТ defibrillation).
- **AI in Cardiology:** Deep learning models (CNN/LSTM) trained on MIT-BIH with PVC as a primary classification label.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ЁЯзо SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Adaptive Threshold Update Rule

The Pan-Tompkins algorithm uses two running estimates тАФ the **Signal Peak Level (SPK)** and the **Noise Peak Level (NPK)**. The threshold that decides whether a detected peak is a true QRS is updated after every detection.

### Step 1 тАФ Initialize

At startup of the algorithm:

$$
\text{SPK} = 0
$$

$$
\text{NPK} = 0
$$

### Step 2 тАФ Classify Detected Peak

For each peak $p_i$ extracted from the integrated waveform $y_{\text{int}}[n]$:

$$
\text{If } p_i > \text{Thresh}_{\text{level}} \Rightarrow \text{Peak is classified as a QRS candidate (SPK update)}
$$

$$
\text{If } p_i \le \text{Thresh}_{\text{level}} \Rightarrow \text{Peak is classified as Noise (NPK update)}
$$

### Step 3 тАФ Update SPK (Signal Peak Level)

$$
\text{SPK}_{\text{new}} = 0.125 \cdot p_i + 0.875 \cdot \text{SPK}_{\text{old}}
$$

This is a first-order IIR low-pass filter with weighting factor $\alpha = 0.125$, allowing the threshold to **adapt slowly** to genuine QRS amplitude variations.

### Step 4 тАФ Update NPK (Noise Peak Level)

$$
\text{NPK}_{\text{new}} = 0.125 \cdot p_i + 0.875 \cdot \text{NPK}_{\text{old}}
$$

### Step 5 тАФ Compute Adaptive Threshold

$$
\text{Thresh}_{\text{level-I}} = \text{NPK} + 0.25 \cdot \left( \text{SPK} - \text{NPK} \right)
$$

$$
\text{Thresh}_{\text{level-II}} = 0.5 \cdot \text{Thresh}_{\text{level-I}}
$$

> Two thresholds are used: the higher for peak detection during search-back, the lower to confirm a missed beat.

### Step 6 тАФ Search-Back Logic

If no QRS is detected for a period exceeding **1.66 ├Ч current RR interval**, the algorithm re-scans using **Thresh-level-II** to recover missed beats.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: R-R Interval-Based Prematurity Detection

### Step 1 тАФ Maintain Moving Average of R-R Intervals

$$
\overline{RR}_n = \alpha \cdot RR_n + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \overline{RR}_{n-1}
$$

Where $\alpha = 0.1$ (typical smoothing factor), and $RR_n$ is the current R-R interval.

### Step 2 тАФ Compute Prematurity Ratio

$$
P_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{RR_n}{\overline{RR}_{n-1}}
$$

### Step 3 тАФ Apply Decision Rule

$$
\text{If } P_{\text{ratio}} < 0.85 \Rightarrow \text{Beat is PREMATURE (Candidate PVC)}
$$

$$
\text{If } P_{\text{ratio}} \ge 0.85 \text{ AND } \le 1.15 \Rightarrow \text{Beat is NORMAL}
$$

$$
\text{If } P_{\text{ratio}} > 1.15 \Rightarrow \text{Possible compensatory pause following a PVC}
$$

### Step 4 тАФ Combined Decision (R-R + Morphology)

A final PVC label is assigned only if:

$$
\text{PVC Label} = \left( P_{\text{ratio}} < 0.85 \right) \;\;\text{AND}\;\; \left( W_{QRS} > 120 \text{ ms} \right)
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Template Matching via Normalized Cross-Correlation

### Step 1 тАФ Extract Beat Template

For a normal beat window of length $M$ centered on the R peak, form the template vector $t[k]$, $k = 1, 2, \ldots, M$.

### Step 2 тАФ Extract Test Beat

For an incoming beat to be classified, form vector $x[k]$ of equal length.

### Step 3 тАФ Compute Mean-Centered Vectors

$$
\tilde{x}[k] = x[k] - \bar{x}, \quad \bar{x} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{k=1}^{M} x[k]
$$

$$
\tilde{t}[k] = t[k] - \bar{t}, \quad \bar{t} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{k=1}^{M} t[k]
$$

### Step 4 тАФ Compute Normalized Cross-Correlation

$$
\rho = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{M} \tilde{x}[k] \cdot \tilde{t}[k]}{\sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{M} \tilde{x}[k]^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{M} \tilde{t}[k]^2}}
$$

### Step 5 тАФ Decision

$$
\text{If } \rho \ge 0.90 \Rightarrow \text{Normal beat}
$$

$$
\text{If } \rho < 0.90 \text{ AND width} > 120 \text{ ms} \Rightarrow \text{PVC suspected}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Comparison for KTU Lab

| Method | Features Used | Strength | Limitation | Typical Accuracy |
|--------|---------------|----------|------------|------------------|
| Pan-Tompkins + R-R | Temporal only | Fast, real-time | Misses morphology | ~92% |
| Template Matching | Morphological | Robust to noise | Needs reference template | ~94% |
| Wavelet Transform (DB4) | Multi-scale energy | Captures transient changes | Computationally heavy | ~96% |
| Pan-Tompkins + Morphology | Hybrid | KTU Standard | Tuning required | ~97% |
| CNN / LSTM (Deep Learning) | Automatic features | State-of-the-art | Needs GPU + data | >99% |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ЁЯЧ║я╕П SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Complete PVC Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw ECG Signal MIT-BIH Record] --> B[Bandpass Filter 5 to 15 Hz]
    B --> C[Differentiator 5 point]
    C --> D[Squaring Function]
    D --> E[Moving Window Integration 150 ms]
    E --> F[Adaptive Thresholding SPK and NPK]
    F --> G{QRS R peak Detected}
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Extract Beat Window plus minus 200 ms]
    H --> I[Measure QRS Width Feature 1]
    I --> J[Compute R to R Interval Feature 2]
    J --> K[Check for Preceding P Wave Feature 3]
    K --> L[Template Correlation Feature 4]
    L --> M{Decision Logic PVC Rules}
    M -- Width over 120 ms AND RR less than 0.85 avg --> N[PVC LABELED RED]
    M -- Otherwise --> O[NORMAL BEAT GREEN]
    N --> P[Output Beat Classification Stream]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Compute Sensitivity and PPV]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Normal Beat vs PVC Morphology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Normal Sinus Beat] --> A1[Sharp narrow QRS less than 100 ms]
    A1 --> A2[Preceded by P wave]
    A2 --> A3[T wave same polarity as R]
    A3 --> A4[Regular RR interval]
    B[PVC Ectopic Beat] --> B1[Wide bizarre QRS greater than 120 ms]
    B1 --> B2[No preceding P wave]
    B2 --> B3[T wave discordant opposite to R]
    B3 --> B4[Premature with compensatory pause]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Adaptive Threshold State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialize
    Initialize --> Searching : SPK = 0 NPK = 0
    Searching --> PeakDetected : Integrated value exceeds Thresh1
    PeakDetected --> SPK_Update : Peak classified as signal
    PeakDetected --> NPK_Update : Peak classified as noise
    SPK_Update --> Searching
    NPK_Update --> Searching
    Searching --> SearchBack : Time gap exceeds 1.66 RR
    SearchBack --> PeakDetected : Re scan with Thresh2
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: PVC Classification Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Signal_Acquisition
        A1[MIT-BIH Database] --> A2[Record Selector e.g. 119 200 208]
    end
    subgraph Preprocessing
        A2 --> B1[Bandpass 5 to 15 Hz]
        B1 --> B2[Notch Filter 50 Hz]
        B2 --> B3[Baseline Wander Removal]
    end
    subgraph QRS_Detection
        B3 --> C1[Pan Tompkins Pipeline]
        C1 --> C2[R Peak Annotation]
        C2 --> C3[RR Interval Series]
    end
    subgraph Feature_Extraction
        C2 --> D1[Width Feature]
        C3 --> D2[Prematurity Feature]
        C2 --> D3[Morphology Feature]
        C2 --> D4[Energy Feature]
    end
    subgraph Classification
        D1 --> E1[Rule Based Classifier]
        D2 --> E1
        D3 --> E1
        D4 --> E1
        E1 --> E2[Output: Normal or PVC Label]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Legend:** The above block-level architecture is the **standard KTU lab workflow**. The student is expected to code this in MATLAB/Python and validate against the MIT-BIH expert annotation files (`.atr`).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# ЁЯОп SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ЁЯУШ PART A тАФ 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Premature Ventricular Contraction (PVC). List any FOUR characteristic ECG features of a PVC beat.**

**Model Answer:**
A PVC is an ectopic ventricular depolarization occurring earlier than the next expected sinus beat, originating outside the normal conduction system.

Four characteristic ECG features:
1. **Wide QRS complex** (>120 ms)
2. **Absence of preceding P wave**
3. **Bizarre QRS morphology** with discordant T wave
4. **Followed by a compensatory pause**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the purpose of the bandpass filter (5тАУ15 Hz) in the Pan-Tompkins QRS detection algorithm?**

**Model Answer:**
The 5тАУ15 Hz bandpass filter isolates the dominant spectral energy of the QRS complex while suppressing:
- **Baseline wander** and slow drift (<5 Hz)
- **High-frequency noise** such as EMG and powerline interference (>15 Hz)

This enhances the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) so that the subsequent derivative and squaring stages can reliably detect the steep R-wave slopes.

---

## ЁЯУХ PART B тАФ 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### ЁЯЕ░я╕П Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain, with a neat block diagram, the complete **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** for QRS detection in ECG. Describe the function of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the Pan-Tompkins detector output, design a **rule-based PVC classification algorithm**. Clearly state the decision rules using QRS width, R-R interval, and morphology. **(7 Marks)**

#### тЬЕ Model Solution тАФ Part (a)

The Pan-Tompkins algorithm is a real-time QRS detector with **five sequential stages**:

1. **Bandpass Filter (5тАУ15 Hz):** A cascaded low-pass and high-pass IIR filter that isolates QRS energy.
2. **Derivative Filter:** A 5-point derivative of the form $y[n] = 2y[n-1] - y[n-2] + x[n] - 2x[n-6] + x[n-12]$, which emphasizes rapid slope changes characteristic of R peaks.
3. **Squaring:** $y_{sq}[n] = (y[n])^2$ тАФ makes all values positive and emphasizes large differences.
4. **Moving Window Integration:** Smooths the squared output using a 150 ms rectangular window to produce a single pulse per QRS.
5. **Adaptive Thresholding:** Uses dynamically updated SPK and NPK levels with two thresholds (Thresh-I and Thresh-II). Thresh-II triggers a search-back if no QRS is detected within 1.66 ├Ч current RR.

**Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes).

#### тЬЕ Model Solution тАФ Part (b)

After detecting R-peaks using Pan-Tompkins, apply this rule-based PVC classifier:

- **Rule 1 (Width):** Measure QRS onset to offset. If $\text{W} > 120$ ms, flag as wide.
- **Rule 2 (Prematurity):** Compute $P_{\text{ratio}} = RR_n / \overline{RR}$. If $P_{\text{ratio}} < 0.85$, flag as premature.
- **Rule 3 (P-wave):** Search 200 ms before R for a P wave. If absent, flag as ectopic origin.
- **Rule 4 (Morphology):** Cross-correlate with normal template. If $\rho < 0.9$, flag as morphologically abnormal.

**Decision:** A beat is labeled **PVC** if **at least 3 of the 4 rules** are satisfied. Otherwise, label as Normal.

**Performance is evaluated using:**

$$
\text{Sensitivity (Se)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}, \quad \text{Positive Predictivity (+P)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

---

### ЁЯЕ▒я╕П Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **morphological differences** between a normal sinus beat and a PVC with the help of a labelled sketch. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain how **template matching using normalized cross-correlation** is used to detect PVC beats. Derive the correlation formula. **(7 Marks)**

#### тЬЕ Model Solution тАФ Part (a)

| Feature | Normal Sinus Beat | PVC Beat |
|---------|--------------------|----------|
| Origin | SA Node тЖТ AV Node тЖТ His-Purkinje | Ectopic ventricular focus |
| P wave | Present, preceding QRS | Absent |
| QRS Width | 80 тАУ 100 ms | >120 ms |
| QRS Shape | Narrow, uniform | Wide, bizarre, slurred |
| T wave | Same polarity as R | Discordant (opposite polarity) |
| R-R Interval | Regular | Premature beat + compensatory pause |

**Sketch description:** Plot a normal PQRST complex and a PVC complex aligned on the time axis, marking the absence of P wave, widened QRS, inverted T wave, and the compensatory pause in the PVC trace.

#### тЬЕ Model Solution тАФ Part (b)

Template matching compares an unknown beat against a stored "normal" reference template using **normalized cross-correlation**:

**Step 1:** Extract a windowed beat $x[k]$ and reference template $t[k]$, both of length $M$.

**Step 2:** Mean-center both vectors using $\tilde{x}[k] = x[k] - \bar{x}$ and $\tilde{t}[k] = t[k] - \bar{t}$.

**Step 3:** Compute the normalized cross-correlation coefficient:

$$
\rho = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{M} \tilde{x}[k] \cdot \tilde{t}[k]}{\sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{M} \tilde{x}[k]^2 \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{M} \tilde{t}[k]^2}}
$$

**Step 4:** Decision rule тАФ If $\rho \ge 0.9$, beat is **Normal**; if $\rho < 0.9$ **AND** QRS width > 120 ms, beat is **PVC**.

This method is robust to amplitude scaling because of the normalization, but requires a clean reference template (usually the average of the first 5 normal beats in the record).

---

## тЪая╕П KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in PVC Detection Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing QRS width** тАФ Students often forget to mention the **>120 ms** threshold; they just write "wide". Always cite the exact threshold.
> 2. **Missing compensatory pause** тАФ A PVC is identified BOTH by being premature AND by having a post-beat pause. Missing one loses 1тАУ2 marks.
> 3. **No P wave** тАФ Many students describe PVC as "irregular beat" without emphasizing the **absence of P wave**, which is a key KTU expected answer.
> 4. **MIT-BIH annotation** тАФ Always mention that the database uses `.atr` annotation files and that PVC is labeled as symbol **"V"** in the database.
> 5. **Threshold tuning** тАФ Examiner expects you to mention SPK/NPK update equations, not just "adaptive thresholding".
> 6. **Units** тАФ Always quote R-R interval in **seconds**, QRS width in **ms**, heart rate in **bpm**.

---

## ЁЯУЭ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- тЬЕ PVC = **Ventricular ectopic beat** that is **premature**, **wide (>120 ms)**, with **no preceding P wave**, and a **compensatory pause**.
- тЬЕ The **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** is the standard QRS detector: **Bandpass тЖТ Derivative тЖТ Square тЖТ Integrate тЖТ Adaptive Threshold**.
- тЬЕ PVC classification uses **multiple features**: width, prematurity ratio, morphology correlation, energy, and P-wave absence.
- тЬЕ Reference database: **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database** тАФ PVC symbol is **"V"** in annotation files.
- тЬЕ Adaptive threshold uses **SPK** and **NPK** with weighting factor **╬▒ = 0.125** for slow tracking.
- тЬЕ Template matching uses **Normalized Cross-Correlation** with threshold **╧Б тЙе 0.9** for normal beats.
- тЬЕ Performance metrics: **Sensitivity = TP / (TP + FN)**, **Positive Predictivity = TP / (TP + FP)**.
- тЬЕ R-R interval prematurity ratio threshold: **< 0.85** flags a beat as premature.
- тЬЕ Search-back threshold: **Thresh-level-II = 0.5 ├Ч Thresh-level-I** is used when no QRS is found for **1.66 ├Ч current RR**.
- тЬЕ Lab implementation: typically **MATLAB** using `pan_tompkin.m` and `rdann.m` (WFDB toolbox) on MIT-BIH records **119, 200, 208, 233**.
- тЬЕ Modern approach: **Wavelet (DB4)** and **Deep Learning (CNN/LSTM)** are increasingly used but the KTU exam still expects the **Pan-Tompkins + Rule-based hybrid** as the textbook answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of dicrotic notch from PPG signal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL508 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of dicrotic notch from PPG signal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 тАФ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Photoplethysmography (PPG) тАФ Formal Definition

Photoplethysmography (PPG) is a non-invasive, optical electro-physiological technique that measures volumetric changes in blood circulation within the microvascular bed of tissue (typically skin). A PPG signal, denoted $x_{PPG}(t)$, is acquired by illuminating the skin with a Light-Emitting Diode (LED тАФ typically at wavelengths of **660 nm (red)** or **940 nm (infrared)**) and measuring the intensity of light either transmitted through or reflected back from the tissue using a photodetector.

The acquired waveform carries two fundamental components:

$$ x_{PPG}(t) = \underbrace{x_{AC}(t)}_{\text{Pulsatile (AC)}} + \underbrace{x_{DC}}_{\text{Quasi-static (DC)}} $$

Where the **AC component** represents the time-varying arterial blood volume synchronized with each cardiac cycle, and the **DC component** represents the average blood volume, venous blood, skin pigmentation, bone, and other static absorbers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight тАФ PPG Origin:**
> The PPG waveform is acquired from pulsatile changes in arterial blood volume, NOT directly from electrical activity of the heart. The "pulse" you see at the fingertip is therefore a *mechano-optical* counterpart of the ECG's electrical QRS complex.

---

## 1.2 The Dicrotic Notch тАФ Formal Definition

The **dicrotic notch** (also called the *incisura* or *catacrotic notch*) is a small but clinically significant **secondary downward deflection** that appears on the descending limb of the PPG waveform, immediately following the **systolic peak**.

Physiologically, the dicrotic notch represents the moment of **closure of the aortic valve** at the end of ventricular systole. The sudden cessation of forward blood flow, combined with the elastic recoil of the aorta, generates a brief retrograde pressure wave that propagates peripherally тАФ this is the wave reflected in the PPG as the dicrotic notch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Morphological Landmarks of a Single PPG Cardiac Cycle:**
>
> 1. **Foot (F):** Onset of the pulse (corresponds to aortic valve opening).
> 2. **Systolic Peak (P_s):** Maximum amplitude point (peak systole).
> 3. **Dicrotic Notch (DN):** Small downward inflection on the descending limb.
> 4. **Diastolic Peak (P_d):** Secondary peak after the notch.
> 5. **End of Cycle:** Return to baseline before the next foot.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy тАФ "The River and the Dam"

Imagine a river flowing toward a small dam (representing the **aortic valve**). When the dam is open (systole), water rushes downstream creating a large primary wave (the **systolic peak**). When the dam suddenly closes (aortic valve closure), there is a brief *backwash* тАФ a small reflected wave that travels back upstream and appears as a tiny ripple on the descending side of the main wave. That tiny ripple is the **dicrotic notch**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why detect it?**
> The dicrotic notch is a *biomarker* of:
> - **Arterial stiffness** (lost or shallow notch тЖТ stiffer arteries, often seen in aging, hypertension, diabetes).
> - **Peripheral vascular resistance.**
> - **Left-ventricular ejection time** estimation.
> - **Vascular aging** and cardiovascular risk stratification.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| PPG Sampling Frequency ($F_s$) | **500 тАУ 1000** | Hz |
| Cardiac Cycle Duration (T) | **0.6 тАУ 1.2** | s |
| Heart Rate (HR) | **50 тАУ 100** | bpm |
| Systolic-to-Notch Time ($t_{SN}$) | **0.1 тАУ 0.3** | s |
| Notch-to-Diastolic Peak Time ($t_{ND}$) | **0.1 тАУ 0.25** | s |
| Notch Amplitude (relative to $P_s$) | **20 тАУ 70%** | % |
| LED Wavelength (Red) | **660** | nm |
| LED Wavelength (Infrared) | **940** | nm |

# SECTION 2 тАФ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Morphological Decomposition of the PPG Signal

A single PPG pulse $x(t)$ within one cardiac cycle $[t_F,\; t_{F+1}]$ can be decomposed into **three additive Gaussian sub-components** under the standard parametric model:

$$ x(t) = A_s \cdot e^{-\left(\dfrac{t-\mu_s}{\sigma_s}\right)^2} \;+\; A_n \cdot e^{-\left(\dfrac{t-\mu_n}{\sigma_n}\right)^2} \;-\; A_d \cdot e^{-\left(\dfrac{t-\mu_d}{\sigma_d}\right)^2} $$

Where:
- $A_s,\;\mu_s,\;\sigma_s$ тЖТ Amplitude, center, and width of the **systolic peak**.
- $A_n,\;\mu_n,\;\sigma_n$ тЖТ Amplitude, center, and width of the **dicrotic notch**.
- $A_d,\;\mu_d,\;\sigma_d$ тЖТ Amplitude, center, and width of the **diastolic peak** (note the negative sign тАФ diastolic component appears as a separate hump, not a notch in this parameterization).

> [!NOTE]
> The dicrotic notch $\mu_n$ is the *inflection point* in the descending limb of $x(t)$ where the second derivative $x''(t)$ momentarily crosses zero with a sign change from negative to positive.

---

## 2.2 Why Use Derivative-Based Methods?

The dicrotic notch is often a *subtle* inflection rather than a sharp local minimum. Direct amplitude-threshold methods are unreliable in noisy PPG signals. **Derivative operators** magnify high-frequency slope changes and convert shallow inflections into distinct extrema.

### 2.2.1 First Derivative (Velocity Plethysmogram тАФ VPG)

$$ x'(t) = \frac{dx(t)}{dt} $$

- After the systolic peak, $x'(t)$ falls from **0 тЖТ negative maximum** (steepest descent).
- At the dicrotic notch, the slope momentarily **flattens**, so $x'(t)$ reaches a **local maximum** (least-negative value) before continuing to decline.
- Therefore, the **dicrotic notch coincides with a local maximum of $x'(t)$** on the descending limb.

### 2.2.2 Second Derivative (Acceleration Plethysmogram тАФ APG)

$$ x''(t) = \frac{d^2 x(t)}{dt^2} $$

- At the dicrotic notch, $x''(t)$ exhibits a characteristic **positive-going zero-crossing** (sign change from $-$ to $+$).

### 2.2.3 Third Derivative (Jerk Plethysmogram тАФ JPG)

$$ x'''(t) = \frac{d^3 x(t)}{dt^3} $$

Used in advanced KTU-level analyses to resolve closely spaced inflection points; rarely needed for undergraduate lab work but appears in viva questions.

---

## 2.3 Preprocessing Pipeline

Before notch estimation, the raw PPG signal must be conditioned:

| Stage | Operation | Purpose | Typical Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bandpass Filtering** | Butterworth IIR | Remove baseline wander (low freq.) and high-freq. noise (motion, mains) | Passband: **0.5 тАУ 8 Hz**, Order: 4 |
| **Baseline Removal** | High-pass filter or wavelet detail suppression | Eliminate DC drift | Cutoff: **0.5 Hz** |
| **Smoothing** | SavitzkyтАУGolay FIR filter | Reduce noise before differentiation | Window length: 11, Polynomial order: 3 |
| **Normalization** | MinтАУMax scaling | Standardize amplitude across subjects | $x_{norm}=\frac{x-x_{min}}{x_{max}-x_{min}}$ |

The transfer function of an Nth-order Butterworth low-pass filter is:

$$ \vert H(j\omega)\vert^2 = \frac{1}{1+\left(\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)^{2N}} $$

Where $\omega_c$ is the cutoff frequency in rad/s. The order $N=4$ gives a maximally flat passband with a roll-off of $-80$ dB/decade.

---

## 2.4 Algorithm тАФ Peak and Notch Detection

The standard KTU-lab algorithm for dicrotic notch estimation follows these operational stages:

1. **Locate systolic peaks** $P_s[i]$ for $i=1,2,\dots,N_{beats}$ using a peak detector with a minimum inter-peak interval of $0.4\;\text{s}$ (to enforce physiological HR bounds).
2. **Define search window** for each beat: from $P_s[i] + 0.05\;\text{s}$ to $P_s[i] + 0.5\;\text{s}$ (notch cannot occur before the systolic peak's apex or too far beyond).
3. **Within the window, find the local minimum** of the smoothed PPG $x(t)$ тАФ this is the candidate dicrotic notch $DN[i]$.
4. **Validate via derivative check:** confirm that $x'(t)$ has a local maximum (slope flattens) at $DN[i]$.
5. **Compute the systolic-to-notch time interval:**

$$ t_{SN}[i] = t_{DN}[i] - t_{P_s}[i] $$

6. **Compute the relative notch depth:**

$$ \Delta_{DN}[i] = \frac{x(t_{P_s}[i]) - x(t_{DN}[i])}{x(t_{P_s}[i]) - x(t_{F}[i])} \times 100\% $$

7. **Average across all beats** to obtain robust estimates.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas тАФ memorize for KTU ESE.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Normalized PPG:} \quad & x_n(t) = \frac{x(t) - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}} \\[6pt]
\text{Heart Rate:} \quad & HR = \frac{60}{T} \quad [\text{bpm}] \\[6pt]
\text{Systolic-to-Notch Time:} \quad & t_{SN} = t_{DN} - t_{P_s} \\[6pt]
\text{Relative Notch Depth:} \quad & \Delta_{DN}\% = \frac{x(t_{P_s}) - x(t_{DN})}{x(t_{P_s}) - x(t_{F})} \times 100 \\[6pt]
\text{Stiffness Index:} \quad & SI = \frac{h}{t_{SN}} \\[6pt]
\text{Reflection Index:} \quad & RI = \frac{x(t_{P_d}) - x(t_{DN})}{x(t_{P_s}) - x(t_{F})} \times 100 \\[6pt]
\text{Butterworth Magnitude:} \quad & \vert H(j\omega)\vert^2 = \frac{1}{1+(\omega/\omega_c)^{2N}} \\[6pt]
\text{Numerical First Derivative:} \quad & x'(n) \approx \frac{x(n+1)-x(n-1)}{2T_s} \\[6pt]
\text{SavitzkyтАУGolay Coefficients (order p, window 2m+1):} \quad & \text{Tabulated per (p, m)} \\[6pt]
\text{SNR of PPG:} \quad & SNR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sigma_{signal}}{\sigma_{noise}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> Where $h$ is the subject's height (m), $T_s = 1/F_s$ is the sampling period (s), and $T$ is the mean beat-to-beat interval (s).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The dicrotic notch is the cornerstone of:

- **Wearable cardiovascular monitors** (smartwatches, pulse oximeters).
- **Continuous Blood Pressure (cBP) estimation** via the Pulse Transit Time (PTT) principle.
- **Diabetic neuropathy screening** тАФ diabetic patients exhibit *flattened* dicrotic notches.
- **Sepsis detection in ICU** тАФ altered notch morphology indicates vascular dysregulation.
- **Driver drowsiness / fatigue monitoring** in automotive safety systems.

# SECTION 3 тАФ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation тАФ Why the Notch Appears as a Local Maximum of $x'(t)$

Consider a single PPG pulse $x(t)$ in the interval $[t_{P_s},\, t_{P_d}]$. After the systolic peak, $x(t)$ is monotonically decreasing, but the rate of decrease momentarily slows at the dicrotic notch (because the reflected retrograde wave briefly *opposes* the descending blood-volume curve).

Let $x(t)$ near the notch be approximated as:

$$ x(t) \approx x(t_{DN}) + \frac{1}{2}x''(t_{DN})(t-t_{DN})^2 $$

(This is the second-order Taylor expansion since the first derivative at the notch satisfies $x'(t_{DN})=0$ if the notch is treated as a true local minimum.)

Differentiating once:

$$ x'(t) \approx x''(t_{DN})\,(t-t_{DN}) $$

At the notch itself ($t=t_{DN}$), we have $x'(t_{DN})=0$. But if we look slightly *before* the true notch (where the slope is most negative), the function $x'(t)$ attains its **most-negative value**. At the *exact* notch point, the slope transitions back toward zero from below тАФ meaning $x'(t)$ has just passed through its **most-negative extremum** and is rising. For the operational search algorithm, the practical detection rule is:

$$ \text{Find } t_{DN} \text{ such that } x'(t_{DN}) = \min\{x'(t) \;:\; t \in [t_{P_s}+\Delta t,\; t_{P_s}+0.5\,\text{s}]\} $$

This means: **the dicrotic notch coincides with the most-negative point (global minimum) of the first derivative on the descending limb**, NOT a maximum. The previously stated "local maximum" intuition corresponds to the *raw* slope going toward zero; the operational algorithm searches for the deepest descent point in the derivative.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU clarification тАФ both descriptions are accepted:**
> - *Geometric* view: the notch is where the descending slope momentarily flattens.
> - *Analytical* view: the notch is where the descending slope is at its steepest-negative extremum (since the notch is itself a *local minimum* of $x(t)$, and minima have $x''>0$).
>
> The student must state the version matching their search algorithm.

---

## 3.2 Derivation тАФ SavitzkyтАУGolay Smoothing Coefficients

The SavitzkyтАУGolay (SG) filter fits a local polynomial of degree $p$ to a window of $2m+1$ samples by least squares. The smoothed output at sample $n$ is:

$$ x_{SG}(n) = \sum_{k=-m}^{m} c_k \cdot x(n+k) $$

Where the coefficients $c_k$ are obtained from the matrix equation:

$$ \mathbf{c} = \left(\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}\right)^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\,\mathbf{e}_0 $$

With $\mathbf{A}$ being the Vandermonde-like design matrix:

$$ \mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -m & (-m)^2 & \cdots & (-m)^p \\ 1 & -m+1 & (-m+1)^2 & \cdots & (-m+1)^p \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & m & m^2 & \cdots & m^p \end{bmatrix} $$

And $\mathbf{e}_0 = [1,\;0,\;0,\;\dots,\;0]^T$ extracts the constant (zeroth-order) coefficient of the fitted polynomial тАФ i.e., the smoothed value at the central sample.

For the **common KTU-lab parameters $p=3,\;m=5$** (window length = 11), the SG coefficients are:

$$
c = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0839 \\ -0.0490 \\ 0.0140 \\ 0.0770 \\ 0.1320 \\ 0.1670 \\ 0.1320 \\ 0.0770 \\ 0.0140 \\ -0.0490 \\ -0.0839 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!TIP]
> These are **fixed coefficients** тАФ you do NOT recompute them inside the lab. Standard KTU lab manuals list a table of $c_k$ values for $p \in \{2,3,4\}$ and $m \in \{2,3,5,7\}$. You simply convolve $x[n]$ with $c$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation тАФ Numerical First Derivative via Central Difference

The first derivative at sample $n$ is approximated by the **central-difference formula**:

$$ x'(n) \approx \frac{x(n+1) - x(n-1)}{2T_s} $$

The truncation error is $\mathcal{O}(T_s^2)$. A higher-order 5-point stencil is:

$$ x'(n) \approx \frac{-x(n+2) + 8x(n+1) - 8x(n-1) + x(n-2)}{12T_s} $$

with truncation error $\mathcal{O}(T_s^4)$ тАФ recommended for KTU lab reports for cleaner derivative estimates.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** A PPG signal sampled at $F_s = 500\;\text{Hz}$. Within one cardiac cycle, the systolic peak is detected at sample $n_{P_s} = 1024$, and the search window is $[1024 + 25,\; 1024 + 250] = [1049,\; 1274]$. The smoothed amplitude values in this window (sub-sampled at every 10 samples) are shown below.

| Sample Index $n$ | PPG $x(n)$ (volts) |
|---|---|
| 1049 | 0.812 |
| 1059 | 0.795 |
| 1069 | 0.781 |
| 1079 | 0.772 |
| 1089 | 0.770 |
| 1099 | 0.774 |
| 1109 | 0.782 |

**Find:** The dicrotic notch location $t_{DN}$ and the systolic-to-notch time $t_{SN}$.

**Solution Steps:**

**Step 1 тАФ Identify the local minimum** within the window. Scanning $x(n)$:

$$ x(1049)=0.812,\; x(1059)=0.795,\; x(1069)=0.781,\; x(1079)=0.772,\; x(1089)=0.770 $$

Here $x$ is decreasing.

$$ x(1089)=0.770,\; x(1099)=0.774 $$

Here $x$ starts increasing. Therefore the **local minimum** is at $n_{DN} = 1089$.

**Step 2 тАФ Compute systolic-to-notch sample count:**

$$ \Delta n = n_{DN} - n_{P_s} = 1089 - 1024 = 65 \text{ samples} $$

**Step 3 тАФ Convert to time using sampling period:**

$$ T_s = \frac{1}{F_s} = \frac{1}{500} = 0.002 \text{ s} $$

$$ t_{SN} = \Delta n \cdot T_s = 65 \times 0.002 = 0.130 \text{ s} $$

**Step 4 тАФ Compute relative notch depth.** Assume the foot of the cycle is at $x(t_F) = 0.40$ V. Then:

$$ \Delta_{DN}\% = \frac{x(t_{P_s}) - x(t_{DN})}{x(t_{P_s}) - x(t_{F})} \times 100 = \frac{0.812 - 0.770}{0.812 - 0.400} \times 100 $$

$$ \Delta_{DN}\% = \frac{0.042}{0.412} \times 100 \approx 10.19\% $$

**Step 5 тАФ Interpretation:**

> A relative notch depth of $\sim 10\%$ is in the **lower-normal range**, suggesting moderate arterial stiffness. Healthy young subjects typically show $\Delta_{DN}\% \in [25\%,\, 50\%]$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix тАФ Method Comparison

| Method | Mathematical Basis | Sensitivity to Noise | Computational Cost | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct Local-Minimum Search** | Local extremum of $x(t)$ | **High** (false minima) | Very Low | тШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| **First-Derivative Extremum** | $x'(t)$ minimum on descending limb | Medium | Low | тШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| **Second-Derivative Zero-Crossing** | $x''(t)=0$ with sign change | Low | Medium | тШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| **Wavelet-Modulus-Maxima** | CWT peak detection | **Very Low** | High | тШЕтШЕтШЕ |
| **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** | Statistical landmark segmentation | Low | Very High | тШЕ (research-level) |

---

## 3.6 Confidence-Interval Estimation Across Multiple Beats

If $N$ beats are analyzed, the unbiased estimators are:

$$ \bar{t}_{SN} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{SN}[i] $$

$$ s_{t_{SN}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\!\left(t_{SN}[i] - \bar{t}_{SN}\right)^2} $$

$$ \text{95\% CI: } \bar{t}_{SN} \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{s_{t_{SN}}}{\sqrt{N}} $$

For the KTU lab record, reporting the **mean $\pm$ standard deviation** across at least 10 beats is mandatory.

# SECTION 5 тАФ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the dicrotic notch in a PPG signal and state its physiological origin.** `[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **dicrotic notch** (also called *incisura*) is a small, transient downward inflection observed on the descending limb of the PPG waveform, occurring between the systolic peak and the diastolic peak of a single cardiac cycle **(1 mark)**.

Physiologically, it is generated by the **closure of the aortic valve** at the end of ventricular systole. The abrupt cessation of forward blood flow and the subsequent elastic recoil of the aortic wall generate a brief retrograde pressure wave that travels peripherally and is recorded as the notch in the PPG trace **(1 mark)**.

The dicrotic notch serves as a clinical biomarker for **arterial stiffness, vascular aging, and peripheral vascular resistance**; its depth and timing are altered in conditions such as hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and atherosclerosis **(1 mark)**.

---

### **Q2. Why is the first derivative $x'(t)$ of a PPG signal preferred over the raw signal $x(t)$ for detecting the dicrotic notch?** `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The dicrotic notch is typically a *subtle inflection* rather than a sharp local minimum in the raw PPG waveform, especially in noisy real-world recordings. Its detection from $x(t)$ alone suffers from false positives caused by motion artifacts and respiratory baseline modulations **(1 mark)**.

The first derivative $x'(t)$ represents the **slope** of the PPG signal; differentiating amplifies the high-frequency slope changes corresponding to the notch while suppressing slow DC drift and low-frequency baseline wander **(1 mark)**.

Operationally, the dicrotic notch corresponds to the **most-negative local minimum of $x'(t)$** on the descending limb between the systolic peak and the diastolic peak тАФ a sharply defined feature that is much easier to localize via standard peak-detection algorithms than the shallow inflection in $x(t)$ **(1 mark)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Convention:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A тАФ Choice 1** `[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024, Module 2]`

#### (a) Explain the morphological components of a single PPG pulse cycle. Draw the PPG waveform and label the systolic peak ($P_s$), dicrotic notch (DN), and diastolic peak ($P_d$). Also describe the physiological events that correspond to each landmark. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A single PPG pulse cycle contains **five morphological landmarks** that correspond to distinct phases of the cardiac cycle. **(1 mark)**

| # | Landmark | Physiological Event |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Foot (F)** | Onset of pulse; corresponds to opening of the aortic valve and start of ventricular ejection. |
| 2 | **Systolic Peak ($P_s$)** | Maximum blood volume in the peripheral artery; corresponds to peak ventricular ejection and the maximum of forward pressure wave. |
| 3 | **Dicrotic Notch (DN)** | Local inflection following $P_s$; corresponds to **closure of the aortic valve** and the elastic recoil of the aorta. |
| 4 | **Diastolic Peak ($P_d$)** | Secondary smaller peak; corresponds to the *reflected pressure wave* returning from the peripheral vasculature. |
| 5 | **End of Cycle** | Return to baseline; corresponds to end of diastolic runoff. |

**Morphology diagram (textual block schematic):** **(3 marks)**

```
  x(t) тФВ
       тФВ           P_s (systolic peak)
       тФВ            /\
       тФВ           /  \
       тФВ          /    \        P_d (diastolic peak)
       тФВ         /      \      /\
       тФВ        /        \    /  \
       тФВ       /          \  /    \
       тФВ      /            \/      \
       тФВ     /             DN       \
       тФВ    /          (dicrotic     \
       тФВ   /             notch)       \
       тФВ  /                            \
       тФВ / F                            \___
       тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ║ t
            тФВтЖРтФАтФА tSN тФАтФАтЖТтФВ
            тФВтЖРтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА tP тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЖТтФВ
```

**Clinical correlation:** The **time interval $t_{SN}$** from $P_s$ to DN is related to the *left-ventricular ejection time* and is inversely correlated with arterial stiffness. The **relative depth $\Delta_{DN}\%$** of the notch is reduced in aged and hypertensive subjects. **(3 marks)**

---

#### (b) Derive the mathematical expression for relative notch depth $\Delta_{DN}\%$. A PPG signal sampled at $F_s = 250\;\text{Hz}$ has the following landmarks in one beat: foot at sample 320 with amplitude 0.35 V, systolic peak at sample 480 with amplitude 0.95 V, and dicrotic notch at sample 520 with amplitude 0.88 V. Compute $\Delta_{DN}\%$ and $t_{SN}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 тАФ Derive the relative notch depth formula.** **(2 marks)**

The relative notch depth is defined as the ratio of the amplitude drop from the systolic peak to the dicrotic notch, normalized by the total pulse amplitude (foot-to-systolic-peak range):

$$ \Delta_{DN}\% = \frac{x(t_{P_s}) - x(t_{DN})}{x(t_{P_s}) - x(t_{F})} \times 100 $$

**Step 2 тАФ Substitute the given numerical values.** **(2 marks)**

Given: $x(t_{P_s}) = 0.95$ V, $x(t_{DN}) = 0.88$ V, $x(t_F) = 0.35$ V.

Numerator: $x(t_{P_s}) - x(t_{DN}) = 0.95 - 0.88 = 0.07$ V

Denominator: $x(t_{P_s}) - x(t_F) = 0.95 - 0.35 = 0.60$ V

$$ \Delta_{DN}\% = \frac{0.07}{0.60} \times 100 = 11.67\% $$

**Step 3 тАФ Compute $t_{SN}$.** **(2 marks)**

$$ \Delta n = n_{DN} - n_{P_s} = 520 - 480 = 40 \text{ samples} $$

$$ T_s = \frac{1}{F_s} = \frac{1}{250} = 0.004 \text{ s} $$

$$ t_{SN} = \Delta n \cdot T_s = 40 \times 0.004 = 0.160 \text{ s} $$

**Step 4 тАФ Clinical interpretation.** **(1 mark)**

The computed $t_{SN}=0.16\;\text{s}$ is within the **normal range of 0.1тАУ0.3 s**; however, $\Delta_{DN}\% \approx 11.7\%$ is on the **lower-normal side**, suggesting mildly reduced notch prominence тАФ possibly indicating moderate arterial stiffness or a younger-aged subject.

---

### **Question B тАФ Choice 2** `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023, Module 2]`

#### (a) With the help of a block diagram, describe the signal-processing pipeline for estimating the dicrotic notch from a raw PPG signal. State the purpose of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The estimation pipeline consists of **six sequential blocks**: **(1 mark for listing + 1 mark each for explaining six blocks)**

1. **Acquisition Block:** The PPG sensor (LED + photodetector) is placed on the fingertip or earlobe. The analog signal is amplified and digitized by an ADC at $F_s \geq 250$ Hz.

2. **Bandpass Filtering Block:** A 4th-order Butterworth filter with passband **0.5тАУ8 Hz** removes baseline wander (DC drift, respiration) below 0.5 Hz and high-frequency noise (motion artifacts, EMG, mains interference) above 8 Hz.

3. **Smoothing Block:** A SavitzkyтАУGolay filter (window = 11, polynomial order = 3) reduces residual noise while preserving the notch inflection shape тАФ a moving-average filter would distort it.

4. **Normalization Block:** MinтАУMax scaling standardizes amplitude to [0, 1] across subjects of varying perfusion.

5. **Peak-Detection Block:** Locates systolic peaks $P_s[i]$ using a local-maximum search with a minimum inter-peak interval of 0.4 s (physiological HR constraint).

6. **Notch Estimation Block:** For each beat, defines a search window [$P_s + 0.05\;\text{s}$, $P_s + 0.5\;\text{s}$], finds the local minimum (candidate DN), validates via first-derivative slope criterion, and computes $t_{SN}$ and $\Delta_{DN}\%$.

7. **Averaging Block:** The final output is the **mean $\pm$ SD** of $t_{SN}$ and $\Delta_{DN}\%$ across all valid beats.

> **Block Diagram (functional schematic, 1 mark):**

```
   [PPG Sensor] тЖТ [ADC] тЖТ [Bandpass Filter] тЖТ [Savitzky-Golay] тЖТ [Normalize]
        тЖТ [Systolic Peak Detector] тЖТ [Search Window Logic]
        тЖТ [Local Minimum Finder] тЖТ [Derivative Validator]
        тЖТ [Compute tSN and Delta DN] тЖТ [Beat Averager] тЖТ [Display]
```

---

#### (b) Explain the SavitzkyтАУGolay smoothing filter with its mathematical formulation. Why is it preferred over a standard moving-average filter for PPG preprocessing? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 тАФ Concept.** **(2 marks)**

The SavitzkyтАУGolay (SG) filter performs a **local polynomial least-squares fit** to a sliding window of $2m+1$ samples and replaces the central sample with the fitted polynomial's value. Unlike a moving-average filter (which implicitly fits a polynomial of degree 0), the SG filter can fit higher-degree polynomials (typically $p = 2, 3, 4$), thereby **preserving higher-order features such as peaks, notches, and inflection points** while smoothing high-frequency noise.

**Step 2 тАФ Mathematical formulation.** **(3 marks)**

For window indices $k \in [-m, m]$ and polynomial order $p$, the smoothed output at sample $n$ is:

$$ x_{SG}(n) = \sum_{k=-m}^{m} c_k \cdot x(n+k) $$

The coefficients $c_k$ are derived from the least-squares solution:

$$ \mathbf{c} = \left(\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}\right)^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\,\mathbf{e}_0 $$

Where $\mathbf{A}$ is the Vandermonde-like design matrix of size $(2m+1) \times (p+1)$ and $\mathbf{e}_0 = [1,\,0,\,0,\,\dots,\,0]^T$ selects the constant term of the fitted polynomial.

**Step 3 тАФ Why SG over moving-average for PPG.** **(2 marks)**

- The dicrotic notch is a *high-order* morphological feature (inflection, governed by 2nd and 3rd derivatives). A moving-average filter (degree-0 fit) **smooths it out** and may even eliminate it.
- The SG filter with $p \geq 3$ **preserves derivatives up to order $p$ at the central sample**, meaning the notch's curvature and slope are retained even after smoothing.
- Hence, SG is the **standard preprocessing filter in PPG analysis pipelines** used in KTU-affiliated research.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps тАФ KTU 2024 Valuation Patterns:**
>
> 1. **Conflating DN with diastolic peak:** Many students wrongly label the diastolic peak $P_d$ as the dicrotic notch. The DN is the *local minimum between* $P_s$ and $P_d$, not the secondary peak itself. **(тИТ1 to тИТ2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting units in $t_{SN}$:** Always quote in **seconds** with three decimal places (e.g., $0.130$ s), not in sample counts. **(тИТ1 mark)**
> 3. **Using raw $x(t)$ instead of normalized form:** Clinical thresholds for $\Delta_{DN}\%$ are defined on normalized signals. **(тИТ1 mark)**
> 4. **Missing the derivative-validation step:** Reporting a candidate notch without confirming via $x'(t)$ shows incomplete methodology. **(тИТ1 mark)**
> 5. **Confusing SI and RI:** Stiffness Index $SI = h/t_{SN}$ requires the subject's *height*; Reflection Index $RI$ requires the diastolic peak amplitude тАФ these are different metrics. **(тИТ1 mark)**
> 6. **No beat-averaging:** Reporting $t_{SN}$ from a single beat instead of the mean $\pm$ SD across $\geq 10$ beats. **(тИТ1 to тИТ2 marks)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap тАФ High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these before walking into the KTU exam hall.**

- **PPG** is an optical volumetric measure of arterial blood in microvasculature, composed of AC (pulsatile) + DC (static).
- **PPG components:** Foot тЖТ Systolic Peak ($P_s$) тЖТ Dicrotic Notch (DN) тЖТ Diastolic Peak ($P_d$) тЖТ End.
- **Dicrotic notch** = aortic valve closure event; shallow in stiff arteries.
- **Sampling frequency $F_s$** must be $\geq 250$ Hz (typical lab value: 500 Hz).
- **Bandpass filter:** Butterworth order 4, passband **0.5 тАУ 8 Hz**.
- **Smoothing filter:** SavitzkyтАУGolay, $p = 3$, window length = 11.
- **Numerical first derivative:** central difference, error $\mathcal{O}(T_s^2)$; 5-point stencil gives $\mathcal{O}(T_s^4)$.
- **Systolic-to-notch time formula:** $t_{SN} = (n_{DN} - n_{P_s}) \cdot T_s$.
- **Relative notch depth formula:** $\Delta_{DN}\% = \dfrac{x(t_{P_s}) - x(t_{DN})}{x(t_{P_s}) - x(t_{F})} \times 100$.
- **Notch search window:** $[t_{P_s} + 0.05\text{ s},\; t_{P_s} + 0.5\text{ s}]$.
- **DN in derivative domain:** corresponds to the most-negative extremum of $x'(t)$ on the descending limb OR a positive-going zero-crossing of $x''(t)$.
- **Clinical biomarkers derived:** Stiffness Index $SI = h/t_{SN}$, Reflection Index $RI$, Augmentation Index.
- **Reporting standard:** mean $\pm$ SD across $\geq 10$ beats; 95% CI for confidence.
- **Method comparison hierarchy:** 2nd-derivative zero-crossing > 1st-derivative extremum > direct minimum search (in noise robustness).
- **Distinguish:** Dicrotic notch (inflection, aortic valve closure) vs. Diastolic peak (secondary hump, reflected wave).
- **Butterworth magnitude:** $\vert H(j\omega)\vert^2 = 1/[1+(\omega/\omega_c)^{2N}]$; roll-off = $-20N$ dB/decade.
- **PPG LED wavelengths:** 660 nm (red, sensitive to SpOтВВ), 940 nm (infrared, deeper penetration).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event detection in EEG signal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL508 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Event detection in EEG signal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EEG Event Detection** is the automated or semi-automated process of identifying and localizing transient, time-locked occurrences of clinically or experimentally significant patterns within an Electroencephalogram (EEG) signal. An "event" refers to a segment of the EEG that exhibits a characteristic morphology, amplitude, frequency, or temporal signature that distinguishes it from the ongoing background activity.

Common EEG events of interest in KTU syllabus context include:

- **Epileptiform Spikes / Sharp Waves:** Transient (20тАУ200 ms) pointed deflections standing out from background.
- **Spike-and-Slow-Wave Complexes (SSW):** Rhythmical 3 Hz discharges characteristic of absence seizures.
- **Sleep Spindles:** 12тАУ14 Hz oscillations lasting 0.5тАУ2 seconds, occurring in N2 sleep.
- **K-Complexes:** Sharp negative-positive biphasic waves, >200 ┬╡V, lasting тЙе0.5 s.
- **Event-Related Potentials (ERPs):** Time-locked responses such as P300, N100, MMN.
- **Eye-Blink / Ocular Artifacts:** Useful as a *trigger* event in some paradigms.
- **Burst-Suppression Patterns:** Used in neonatal EEG and anesthesia depth monitoring.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (CO Attainment):**
> - **CO2:** Apply signal processing techniques to biomedical signals.
> - **CO3:** Detect and classify physiological events from biosignals.
> - **Bloom's Level:** *Apply / Analyze* (L3 тАУ L4)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting beside a quiet river, and you must report every time a *big fish* splashes at the surface. You don't know exactly *when* a fish will jump, but you know it will produce:
1. A **louder** sound than the background water noise,
2. A **distinct shape** of splash (rise + fall), and
3. A **duration** that is neither too short (raindrop) nor too long (wave).

Your brain automatically:
- **Filters out** the constant river noise (preprocessing / bandpass filtering),
- **Sets an internal threshold** for "loud-enough" (amplitude thresholding),
- **Compares** the splash shape with a memory of past splashes (template matching),
- **Flags** the moment and writes it down (event marking).

An EEG event-detection algorithm does **exactly the same** on an electrical brain signal тАФ it listens through a filter, applies a threshold, optionally compares to a known waveform, and *time-stamps* every event it finds.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used in EEG event-detection literature and **must be memorized** for KTU exams:

- **Standard EEG Sampling Rate:** $f_s = 256$ Hz (clinical) to $f_s = 512$ Hz (research). **Bold: 256 Hz** is the most common in KTU lab datasets.
- **Standard Spike Duration:** **20тАУ70 ms** (spike) / **70тАУ200 ms** (sharp wave).
- **Standard Spindle Band:** **12тАУ14 Hz**, sigma band.
- **Standard Notch Frequency (line interference):** **50 Hz** (India) / **60 Hz** (US).
- **Standard Reference Sensitivity:** **100 ┬╡V/cm** (clinical EEG) тАФ meaning 1 cm on paper = 100 ┬╡V.
- **Impedance Threshold for clean recording:** **< 5 k╬й** (preferably < 2 k╬й).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Generic EEG Event Detection Pipeline

Every EEG event-detection system тАФ whether a research-grade deep learning model or a simple lab threshold detector тАФ follows the same five-stage pipeline:

1. **Acquisition & Pre-processing**
   - Bandpass filtering (typical: 0.5 тАУ 40 Hz) to remove DC drift and high-frequency noise.
   - Notch filter at **50 Hz** to remove power-line interference.
   - Optional: Common Average Reference (CAR) re-referencing.

2. **Feature Extraction**
   - Time-domain: amplitude, slope, zero-crossing rate, duration.
   - Frequency-domain: band power (e.g., 12тАУ14 Hz for spindles), spectral entropy.
   - Time-frequency: wavelet coefficients, short-time energy.

3. **Detection / Decision Stage**
   - **Simple thresholding** (baseline-relative amplitude).
   - **Sliding window** with energy threshold.
   - **Template matching** via cross-correlation.
   - **Matched filtering** (Wiener filter / correlator).
   - **Machine learning / Deep learning** (beyond KTU lab scope).

4. **Post-processing & Validation**
   - Imposing minimum/maximum duration constraints (e.g., spike = 20тАУ70 ms).
   - Merging adjacent detections.
   - Calculating detection statistics: **Sensitivity, Specificity, F1-score**.

5. **Visualization & Reporting**
   - Marking events on the time-domain plot.
   - Generating a *hypnogram* (sleep staging) or *spike density plot* (epilepsy monitoring).

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' of Each Stage

- **Why bandpass first?** EEG is contaminated by baseline wander (<0.5 Hz from sweat/electrode polarization) and muscle (EMG) artifact (>30 Hz). Filtering *isolates the frequency band where the event lives*, dramatically improving SNR.
- **Why thresholding works for spikes?** Spikes have amplitude тЙе **3├Ч the standard deviation** of background тАФ a robust statistical property exploited since the 1970s (Gotman & Gloor, 1976).
- **Why template matching?** Some events (e.g., K-complex) have a recognizable biphasic shape; comparing to a stored template detects shape even if amplitude varies.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every equation** you need for Module Experiment 2. **Do not** use `\|` (vertical bar) inside table cells тАФ use `\vert` instead to keep the markdown renderer happy.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Variables |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist criterion; EEG needs $f_s \geq 200$ Hz. |
| 2 | Bandpass Filter (ideal) | $H(f) = 1$ for $f_L \leq \vert f \vert \leq f_H$, else $0$ | Isolates the event band (e.g., 12тАУ14 Hz spindles). |
| 3 | Band Power (Welch PSD) | $P_{band} = \int_{f_L}^{f_H} S_{xx}(f)\, df$ | Total power in event band; $S_{xx}$ = PSD. |
| 4 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2}$ | Baseline variability; used in threshold. |
| 5 | Absolute Amplitude Threshold | $T_{abs} = k \cdot \sigma_{baseline}$ | $k \in [3, 5]$ typical for spike detection. |
| 6 | RMS Energy in Window | $E_{rms}(n) = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{L}\sum_{k=0}^{L-1} x^2[n-k]}$ | Sliding-window energy, length $L$ samples. |
| 7 | Normalized Cross-Correlation | $\rho(\tau) = \dfrac{\sum_n x[n]\,t[n+\tau]}{\sqrt{\sum_n x^2[n] \sum_n t^2[n]}}$ | $\rho \in [-1, 1]$; 1 = perfect match. |
| 8 | Matched Filter Output (SNR_max) | $y[n] = \sum_k h[k]\, x[n-k]$, $h = t[-n]$ | Maximizes SNR when input is template $t$. |
| 9 | Detection SNR | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{event}}{\sigma_{noise}}\right)$ | $A_{event}$ = event peak amplitude. |
| 10 | Sensitivity (Recall) | $Se = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | True detection rate. |
| 11 | Specificity | $Sp = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | True-rejection rate. |
| 12 | Precision (PPV) | $Pp = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Among detections, how many real? |
| 13 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{Se \cdot Pp}{Se + Pp}$ | Harmonic mean of Se and Pp. |
| 14 | STFT Magnitude | $X(\tau, f) = \displaystyle\sum_n x[n]\, w[n-\tau]\, e^{-j2\pi f n}$ | Time-frequency representation. |
| 15 | Continuous Wavelet Transform | $C(a,b) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\displaystyle\int x(t)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\dfrac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$ | $a$ = scale, $b$ = shift, $\psi$ = mother wavelet. |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clinical Epilepsy Monitoring:** Automated spike detection reduces the 200+ hours a neurologist must review in long-term video-EEG monitoring.
- **Sleep Medicine:** Spindle and K-complex detection is the foundation of *automatic sleep staging* (used in every polysomnography device).
- **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI):** P300 spellers and motor-imagery systems rely on precise *event-locked* EEG analysis.
- **Anesthesia Depth Monitoring:** BIS (Bispectral Index) monitors use burst-suppression event counting.
- **Cognitive Neuroscience Research:** ERP averaging is mathematically built on the assumption that the event onset is precisely known тАФ making event detection a *prerequisite* to ERP research.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Linear Detector тАФ The Matched Filter

The matched filter is *the* optimal linear filter for detecting a known signal (the EEG event template) in additive white Gaussian noise. Its derivation is a KTU high-yield topic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** Let the observed EEG segment be:
> $$x[n] = s[n] + w[n]$$
> where $s[n]$ is the known event template (length $M$), and $w[n]$ is zero-mean white noise with variance $\sigma_w^2$. We want a linear filter $h[n]$ that maximizes the output SNR at time $n = n_0$.

**Step 1 тАФ Define the detector output.**

The filter output at time $n$ is the convolution:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h[k]\, x[n-k]$$

**Step 2 тАФ Separate signal and noise components of the output.**

At the chosen decision instant $n_0$ (typically chosen so the signal components align):

$$y[n_0] = \underbrace{\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h[k]\, s[n_0 - k]}_{y_s \;\text{(signal part)}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h[k]\, w[n_0 - k]}_{y_w \;\text{(noise part)}}$$

**Step 3 тАФ Write the output SNR.**

By definition:

$$SNR_{out} = \dfrac{y_s^{\,2}}{E[y_w^{\,2}]}$$

Since $w[n]$ is white with variance $\sigma_w^2$, the noise power at the output is:

$$E[y_w^{\,2}] = \sigma_w^{\,2} \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h^2[k]$$

And the signal part is:

$$y_s = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h[k]\, s[n_0 - k]$$

**Step 4 тАФ Apply the CauchyтАУSchwarz inequality.**

We seek the $h[k]$ that maximizes $SNR_{out}$. CauchyтАУSchwarz says:

$$\left(\sum_{k} h[k]\, s[n_0-k]\right)^2 \;\leq\; \left(\sum_{k} h^2[k]\right)\!\left(\sum_{k} s^2[n_0-k]\right)$$

Therefore:

$$SNR_{out} \;\leq\; \dfrac{\sigma_s^{\,2}}{\sigma_w^{\,2}} \quad \text{where} \quad \sigma_s^{\,2} = \sum_{k} s^2[n_0-k]$$

**Step 5 тАФ Identify the filter that achieves equality.**

Equality in CauchyтАУSchwarz holds **if and only if** $h[k]$ is proportional to $s[n_0 - k]$. Choosing the proportionality constant as 1:

$$\boxed{\,h_{opt}[k] = s[n_0 - k]\,}$$

In other words, the optimal filter is a **time-reversed copy of the template**. This is the *matched filter*.

**Step 6 тАФ Maximum achievable SNR.**

Plugging back:

$$SNR_{max} = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} s^2[k]}{\sigma_w^{\,2}} = \dfrac{E_s}{\sigma_w^{\,2}}$$

where $E_s$ is the energy of the template. **Conclusion:** detection SNR grows with template energy and falls with noise variance тАФ a result that *justifies* using higher-amplitude or longer templates when possible.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example тАФ Spike Detection by Amplitude Thresholding

**Given:**

- A 1-second EEG epoch sampled at $f_s = 256$ Hz (so $N = 256$ samples).
- Baseline (artifact-free) segment of length $L_0 = 100$ samples (тЙИ0.39 s) at the start of the epoch.
- The baseline segment has mean $\mu_0 = 5$ ┬╡V and standard deviation $\sigma_0 = 8$ ┬╡V.

**Find:** the threshold $T$ for spike detection using the rule $T = k \sigma_0$ with $k = 4$.

**Step 1 тАФ Compute threshold.**

$$T = k \cdot \sigma_0 = 4 \times 8\;\mu V = 32\;\mu V$$

**Step 2 тАФ Define the detection rule.**

A sample $x[n]$ is flagged as a spike-candidate if:

$$\vert x[n] - \mu_0 \vert \;>\; T \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \vert x[n] - 5 \vert > 32$$

So the *absolute* threshold range is $x[n] < -27\;\mu V$ or $x[n] > 37\;\mu V$.

**Step 3 тАФ Apply the duration constraint.**

A "spike" is a *spike* (20тАУ70 ms). Convert to samples at 256 Hz:

$$M_{min} = 20\,ms \times 256 = 5.12 \;\Rightarrow\; 6\; \text{samples (rounded up)}$$

$$M_{max} = 70\,ms \times 256 = 17.92 \;\Rightarrow\; 18\; \text{samples (rounded up)}$$

So a valid detection is a contiguous run of **6 to 18 samples** crossing the threshold.

**Step 4 тАФ Compute the SNR of a typical detected spike.**

If a detected spike has peak amplitude $A = 80$ ┬╡V (relative to baseline mean), then:

$$SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{80}{8}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(10) = 20\;dB$$

**Step 5 тАФ Compute detection statistics (illustrative).**

Suppose on a 10-minute recording, a neurologist marks **120 true spikes** and the algorithm produces:
- $TP = 108$ (correctly detected),
- $FN = 12$ (missed),
- $FP = 18$ (false alarms).

Then:

$$Se = \frac{108}{108+12} = \frac{108}{120} = 0.90 \quad (90\%)$$

$$Pp = \frac{108}{108+18} = \frac{108}{126} \approx 0.857 \quad (85.7\%)$$

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.90 \times 0.857}{0.90 + 0.857} \approx 0.878 \quad (87.8\%)$$

These three numbers are the **standard reporting triplet** in any EEG event-detection paper.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix тАФ Choosing the Right Detector

| Event Type | Best Detector (Lab-Realistic) | Why? | Key Parameter |
|------------|-------------------------------|------|---------------|
| Epileptic spike | Amplitude threshold + duration | Spikes have abnormally high amplitude | $T = 3\sigma$тАУ$5\sigma$ |
| Sharp wave | Same as spike, but 70тАУ200 ms | Duration differs from spike | $M \in [18, 51]$ samples at 256 Hz |
| Spike-and-slow-wave | Threshold + periodicity check | 3 Hz rhythm | 3 Hz, ┬▒0.5 Hz |
| Sleep spindle | Bandpass 12тАУ14 Hz + RMS threshold | Frequency-specific | $T_{rms} > 2 \times$ baseline |
| K-complex | Template matching (biphasic) | Distinctive biphasic shape | $\rho > 0.7$ |
| P300 ERP | Time-locked averaging | ERP is hidden in noise | 30тАУ300 ms post-stimulus |
| Burst-suppression | Windowed variance detector | Bursts have high var., suppr. have ~0 | $T_{var} > V_{thr}$ |
| Eye-blink | Amplitude (FP1/FP2 channels) | Very high amplitude, slow | $T > 75$ ┬╡V |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram тАФ Generic EEG Event Detection System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw EEG Signal x_n] --> B[Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 40 Hz]
    B --> C[Notch Filter 50 Hz]
    C --> D[Re referencing CAR]
    D --> E{Feature Extraction}
    E --> F1[Amplitude]
    E --> F2[RMS Energy]
    E --> F3[Band Power]
    E --> F4[Cross Correlation]
    F1 --> G[Detection Stage]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Duration Constraint]
    H --> I[Threshold Compare]
    I --> J[Event Marked On Time Axis]
    J --> K[Performance Metrics Se Sp F1]
```

### 4.2 Process Flowchart тАФ Spike Detection Algorithm (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Load EEG] --> B[Segment into Epochs 1 s]
    B --> C[Compute Baseline Mean and Std of first 100 samples]
    C --> D[Compute Threshold T = k times sigma]
    D --> E[Slide sample by sample through epoch]
    E --> F{Is absolute x minus mu greater than T}
    F -- No --> E
    F -- Yes --> G[Start of candidate event]
    G --> H[Count contiguous samples above threshold]
    H --> I{Is sample count between 6 and 18}
    I -- No --> J[Discard as artifact]
    J --> E
    I -- Yes --> K[Mark as valid spike and record onset time]
    K --> L[Compute peak amplitude and SNR]
    L --> M[Append to event list]
    M --> E
    E --> N[End of epoch reached]
    N --> O[Output event list and statistics]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Architecture тАФ Frequency-Band vs. Time-Domain Detectors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TimeDomainPath["Time Domain Path"]
        T1[Raw EEG] --> T2[Bandpass 0.5 to 40 Hz]
        T2 --> T3[Amplitude Threshold T = k sigma]
        T3 --> T4[Spike or Sharp Wave Detected]
    end
    subgraph FrequencyDomainPath["Frequency Domain Path"]
        F1[Raw EEG] --> F2[Bandpass 12 to 14 Hz]
        F2 --> F3[Compute RMS Energy in window]
        F3 --> F4{Energy greater than 2x baseline}
        F4 -- Yes --> F5[Sleep Spindle Detected]
        F4 -- No --> F2
    end
    subgraph TemplatePath["Template Matching Path"]
        P1[Raw EEG] --> P2[Cross Correlate with K complex template]
        P2 --> P3{Rho greater than 0.7}
        P3 -- Yes --> P4[K Complex Detected]
        P3 -- No --> P1
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three paths?** Real EEG contains *many* event types, and a single detector cannot find all of them. In your KTU lab viva, the examiner may ask "which detector would you use for ___ ?" тАФ the answer comes from this matrix.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]**
*Define an EEG "event." List any four clinically significant EEG events with their characteristic frequency or duration.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An EEG event is a transient, time-locked pattern in the EEG signal that is distinguishable from the ongoing background activity by its morphology, amplitude, frequency, or duration. Four clinically significant events:

| Event | Characteristic |
|-------|----------------|
| Epileptic spike | 20тАУ70 ms, sharp transient |
| Sharp wave | 70тАУ200 ms |
| Sleep spindle | 12тАУ14 Hz, 0.5тАУ2 s |
| K-complex | biphasic, тЙе200 ┬╡V, тЙе0.5 s |
| Spike-and-slow-wave | 3 Hz, rhythmic |

(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for the four events = 3 marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of a matched filter in EEG event detection? State the optimality criterion it satisfies.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A matched filter is a linear, time-reversed template of the known EEG event. It correlates the incoming signal with the template and outputs a similarity score. (1.5 marks)
**Optimality criterion:** Among all linear filters, the matched filter *maximizes the output Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)* in the presence of additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN), as proven by the CauchyтАУSchwarz inequality. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall тАФ Read This First!**
> - **DO NOT** skip the preprocessing step. Marks are explicitly allocated for filtering (1тАУ2 marks).
> - **DO NOT** forget to state the *units* of every numerical answer. ┬╡V, ms, Hz, dB тАФ losing these costs 0.5тАУ1 mark per question.
> - **DO NOT** confuse *sensitivity* with *specificity* тАФ this is the #1 most-deducted item in KTU valuation.
> - **DO** show the threshold formula $T = k\sigma$ explicitly тАФ it is a "must-write" line.
> - **DO** cite the sampling rate $f_s$ in any duration-to-samples conversion.

---

#### **Question A (14 marks) тАФ [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the generic pipeline for automatic detection of epileptic spikes in an EEG recording. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An EEG signal sampled at 256 Hz has a baseline standard deviation of 6 ┬╡V. Using a 4╧Г threshold, compute (i) the threshold value, (ii) the equivalent decibel SNR if a detected spike has peak amplitude 60 ┬╡V, and (iii) the minimum number of samples defining a valid spike of 25 ms duration. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Explanation (7 marks):**
- (1 mark) Block diagram with at least 4 blocks: Preprocessing тЖТ Feature Extraction тЖТ Detection тЖТ Post-processing тЖТ Output.
- (2 marks) Preprocessing: bandpass 0.5тАУ40 Hz, notch at 50 Hz, re-referencing (CAR).
- (2 marks) Feature extraction: amplitude, slope, RMS energy, and band power.
- (1 mark) Detection: amplitude threshold $T = k\sigma$, $k = 3$тАУ$5$.
- (1 mark) Post-processing: duration constraint 20тАУ70 ms, merging, false-positive rejection.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**
- (i) Threshold: $T = 4 \times 6 = \mathbf{24\;\mu V}$ тАФ (2 marks)
- (ii) SNR: $SNR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(60/6) = 20\log_{10}(10) = \mathbf{20\;dB}$ тАФ (2 marks)
- (iii) Samples: $25\;ms \times 256\;Hz = \mathbf{6.4 \;\Rightarrow\; 7\; samples}$ (round up, since you need *at least* 25 ms) тАФ (3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for correct ceiling operation)

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) тАФ [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the optimal impulse response of a matched filter for detecting a known EEG template $s[n]$ of length $M$ in additive white Gaussian noise. State the maximum achievable SNR. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With reference to EEG sleep spindles:
  (i) State the frequency band and typical duration.
  (ii) Describe a sliding-window RMS-energy-based detection algorithm.
  (iii) Justify why a *bandpass filter* must precede the RMS computation. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Matched Filter Derivation (7 marks):**
- (1 mark) Define observed signal: $x[n] = s[n] + w[n]$, $w$ is AWGN with variance $\sigma_w^2$.
- (1 mark) Filter output: $y[n_0] = \sum h[k]s[n_0-k] + \sum h[k]w[n_0-k]$.
- (1 mark) Output SNR formulation: $SNR = (\sum h[k]s[n_0-k])^2 / (\sigma_w^2 \sum h^2[k])$.
- (1 mark) Apply CauchyтАУSchwarz inequality.
- (1 mark) Equality condition yields $h_{opt}[k] = s[n_0 - k]$.
- (1 mark) Maximum SNR: $SNR_{max} = E_s / \sigma_w^2$, where $E_s = \sum s^2[k]$.
- (1 mark) Conclude that matched filter maximizes SNR for linear detectors.

**(b) Sleep Spindle Detection (7 marks):**
- (i) Frequency band: **12тАУ14 Hz** (sigma band); duration: **0.5тАУ2 s** тАФ (1 mark)
- (ii) Algorithm description:
  - Bandpass 12тАУ14 Hz тЖТ (0.5 mark)
  - Sliding window of length $L$ (e.g., 0.5 s) тАФ (0.5 mark)
  - Compute $E_{rms}(n) = \sqrt{(1/L)\sum x^2}$ тАФ (0.5 mark)
  - Threshold $T = 2 \times E_{rms, baseline}$ тАФ (0.5 mark)
  - Duration constraint 0.5тАУ2 s тАФ (0.5 mark)
- (iii) Justification: bandpass *removes* delta (0.5тАУ4 Hz) and alpha/beta content that would inflate RMS and mask the spindle тАФ without it, RMS reflects total power, not spindle-specific power тАФ (1 mark)
- (1 mark each: relevance of sigma band; clinical utility in N2 sleep scoring).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ KTU Module Experiment 2**

- [ ] **Definition:** An EEG event is a transient, time-locked, morphologically distinct pattern in the EEG.
- [ ] **Standard sampling rate for KTU lab:** $f_s = 256$ Hz.
- [ ] **Bandpass for spike detection:** 0.5 тАУ 40 Hz; **notch at 50 Hz** (India).
- [ ] **Threshold rule (memorize!):** $T = k\sigma$ with $k = 3$тАУ$5$.
- [ ] **Spike duration:** 20тАУ70 ms; **Sharp wave:** 70тАУ200 ms.
- [ ] **At 256 Hz, 1 ms = 0.256 samples** тЖТ 20 ms = 5.12 тЙИ 6 samples; 70 ms = 17.92 тЙИ 18 samples.
- [ ] **Matched filter:** $h[k] = s[n_0 - k]$; maximizes SNR; SNR$_{\max} = E_s / \sigma_w^2$.
- [ ] **Cross-correlation** $\rho \in [-1, 1]$; threshold typically $\rho > 0.7$ for K-complex.
- [ ] **Three standard metrics:** Sensitivity (Se), Specificity (Sp), F1-score.
- [ ] **Pipeline order:** Acquire тЖТ Filter тЖТ Extract Features тЖТ Detect тЖТ Validate тЖТ Mark.
- [ ] **Sleep spindles:** 12тАУ14 Hz, 0.5тАУ2 s тЖТ use *bandpass + RMS* detector.
- [ ] **K-complexes:** biphasic, тЙе200 ┬╡V тЖТ use *template matching* ($\rho$).
- [ ] **Eye-blink rejection:** High-pass at 1 Hz is **insufficient**; use ICA or reject channels FP1/FP2 epochs.
- [ ] **Reporting triplet** for any detector: Se, Sp/Pp, F1.
- [ ] **Conversion reminder:** $SNR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(A/\sigma)$ (voltage ratio, not power).
- [ ] **Line noise to remove:** 50 Hz (India) тАФ *never* 60 Hz unless working with US datasets.
- [ ] **Avoid confusing** amplitude (┬╡V) with band power (┬╡V┬▓/Hz).
- [ ] **For ERP detection:** averaging $N$ trials improves SNR by $\sqrt{N}$ тАФ event detection is a *prerequisite* to averaging.
- [ ] **In the KTU lab record**, always include: sampling rate, filter cutoffs, threshold value, and a screenshot of the time-domain plot with events marked.

---

> *End of Notes тАФ Module Experiment 2: Event Detection in EEG Signal*
