topics.title: Spectral analysis of EEG signal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL508 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Spectral analysis of EEG signal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Spectral Analysis of an EEG signal** is the process of decomposing a time-domain electroencephalogram (EEG) recording into its constituent frequency components to quantify the power distribution across physiological frequency bands. Mathematically, it estimates the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)**, denoted $S_x(f)$, which describes how the signal's power is distributed as a function of frequency $f$ (in **Hz**).

The PSD of a discrete-time EEG signal $x(n)$ sampled at a rate $f_s$ is formally defined via the WienerтАУKhinchin theorem as the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) of its autocorrelation sequence $r_{xx}(\tau)$:

$$
S_x(f) = \sum_{\tau=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(\tau) \, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}
$$

where $r_{xx}(\tau) = E[x(n)\,x(n+\tau)]$ and $E[\cdot]$ is the expectation operator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module Experiment 2, students are expected to compute the PSD of a real or simulated EEG signal and identify the dominant rhythms (Delta, Theta, Alpha, Beta, Gamma). The standard estimation tool taught is the **Welch's Averaged Periodogram Method**.

### 1.2 EEG Signal тАФ A Quick Background

The EEG is a non-stationary, quasi-random bio-electric signal recorded from the scalp using surface electrodes (typically following the international **10тАУ20 electrode placement system**). Its amplitude lies in the microvolt range (**$\mu$V**, typically 10тАУ100 $\mu$V) and contains clinically meaningful rhythms:

| Rhythm | Frequency Band (Hz) | Physiological State |
|---|---|---|
| Delta ($\delta$) | 0.5 тАУ 4 | Deep sleep, infants |
| Theta ($\theta$) | 4 тАУ 8 | Drowsiness, light sleep |
| Alpha ($\alpha$) | 8 тАУ 13 | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed |
| Beta ($\beta$) | 13 тАУ 30 | Active thinking, alert state |
| Gamma ($\gamma$) | 30 тАУ 100 | Cognitive binding, perception |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Sampling Rate for EEG:** In KTU lab sessions and clinical practice, EEG is sampled at $f_s = \mathbf{256}$ **Hz** or **512 Hz** to satisfy the **Nyquist criterion** for capturing up to the gamma band ($f_{max} = 100$ Hz). Antialiasing low-pass filters are set at $f_c = 0.4 \cdot f_s$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a busy marketplace hearing a **muffled, continuous hum of voices**. Your ear cannot easily distinguish individual conversations. However, if you were to pass that sound through a **prism-like filter** (a spectrogram), the hum would split into a **rainbow of frequencies**: the deep rumble of trucks (delta), the chatter of people (alpha/beta), and the high-pitched whistles (gamma).

**Spectral analysis does exactly this for an EEG signal.** The raw time-voltage waveform (the muffled hum) is passed through a mathematical prism тАФ the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** тАФ and the output is a plot of **power versus frequency**. The peaks in this plot tell the neurologist *which brain rhythm is dominant* at that moment, aiding in diagnosis of epilepsy, sleep disorders, encephalopathies, and cognitive states.

In a lab context, the "prism" is implemented digitally using the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** algorithm on windowed, overlapping segments of the EEG signal.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** *Time domain tells you "when"; Frequency domain tells you "what rhythm".* Spectral analysis converts "when" information into "what rhythm" information.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Spectral Analysis for EEG?

EEG signals are **non-stationary** тАФ their statistical properties (mean, variance, dominant frequency) change over time. Direct Fourier analysis of the entire recording gives only an *average* spectrum. To capture local frequency behaviour, the signal is broken into **short overlapping windows** and an FFT is applied to each window. This is the foundation of:

1. **Stationary spectral estimation** тАФ Welch's method (used in this experiment).
2. **Time-frequency analysis** тАФ Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) / Spectrogram.

### 2.2 The Three Core Estimation Methods (Board-Favourite Topic)

#### (a) Periodogram (Direct Method)

The simplest PSD estimator. Take the $N$-point FFT of the entire signal and compute the squared magnitude divided by $N$:

$$
\hat{S}_{PER}(f_k) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert X(f_k) \right\vert^2
$$

where $X(f_k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j2\pi f_k n / N}$ is the DFT.

**Limitation:** High variance; does not converge as $N \to \infty$. Poor resolution for short EEG segments.

#### (b) Bartlett's Method (Averaging)

Splits the signal into $K$ non-overlapping segments of length $L$, computes the periodogram of each, and averages:

$$
\hat{S}_{BART}(f_k) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \frac{1}{L} \left\vert X_i(f_k) \right\vert^2
$$

Reduces variance by a factor of $K$ at the cost of frequency resolution (resolution $\propto 1/L$).

#### (c) Welch's Method (Modified Averaging тАФ *Experiment Standard*)

Improves Bartlett by:
1. **Windowing** each segment with a tapering window $w(n)$ (typically **Hann** or **Hamming**) to reduce spectral leakage.
2. Allowing **50 % overlap** between adjacent segments to increase the number of averages.

$$
\hat{S}_{WELCH}(f_k) = \frac{1}{K \cdot U} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_i(n) \, w(n) \, e^{-j2\pi f_k n / L} \right\vert^2
$$

where $U = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^2(n)$ is the window energy normalization factor and $K$ is the number of overlapping segments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welch's Trade-off:** More overlap тЖТ smoother PSD estimate (lower variance), but segments become correlated тЖТ not fully independent. **50 % overlap** is the KTU-recommended default.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of the EEG Spectral Analysis Experiment

1. **Acquire / Load** the EEG signal $x(n)$, $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$.
2. **Verify** sampling rate $f_s$ and total duration $T = N/f_s$.
3. **Pre-process**: remove DC offset, apply band-pass filter (typically **0.5 Hz тАУ 40 Hz** for clinical EEG).
4. **Choose parameters**: window length $L$ (commonly **2 seconds**, i.e., $L = 2 f_s$ samples), window type (Hann), overlap (50 %), FFT size $N_{FFT} \geq L$.
5. **Segment** $x(n)$ into overlapping frames $x_i(n)$ of length $L$.
6. **Apply window** $w(n)$ to each segment: $x_i^w(n) = x_i(n) \cdot w(n)$.
7. **Compute FFT** of each windowed segment to obtain $X_i(f_k)$.
8. **Compute periodogram** of each segment: $P_i(f_k) = \frac{1}{U} \left\vert X_i(f_k) \right\vert^2$.
9. **Average** all $K$ periodograms to obtain the final PSD estimate $\hat{S}_x(f_k)$.
10. **Plot** PSD (dB/Hz) versus frequency (Hz). Identify peaks in Delta, Theta, Alpha, Beta bands.
11. **Compute band power** by integrating PSD over each band.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **minimum formula set** KTU examiners expect you to reproduce in Part B answers.

| Formula | Expression | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Discrete Fourier Transform | $X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$ | Frequency-domain representation |
| Periodogram | $\hat{S}_{PER}(k) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert X(k) \right\vert^2$ | Raw PSD estimate, units $\mu V^2 / Hz$ |
| Welch's PSD | $\hat{S}_{WELCH}(k) = \frac{1}{K \cdot U} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \left\vert X_i^w(k) \right\vert^2$ | Averaged, windowed PSD |
| Window energy | $U = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^2(n)$ | Normalization for window |
| Power in band | $P_{[f_1, f_2]} = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} S_x(f)\, df$ | Absolute band power ($\mu V^2$) |
| Relative band power | $P_{rel} = \frac{P_{[f_1,f_2]}}{P_{total}}$ | Normalized power (%) |
| Frequency resolution | $\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N_{FFT}}$ | Spacing between FFT bins (Hz) |
| Nyquist frequency | $f_{max} = \frac{f_s}{2}$ | Maximum representable freq. |
| PSD in decibels | $S_{dB}(f) = 10 \log_{10}[S_x(f)]$ | Log-scale for visualization |
| Hann window | $w(n) = 0.5\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{L-1}\right)\right]$ | Tapering window $0 \leq n \leq L-1$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Spectral analysis of EEG is the cornerstone of:
- **Clinical Neurology:** Seizure detection (high-frequency spikes, broadband power surge), sleep-stage scoring (delta dominance in N3), depth-of-anesthesia monitoring (bispectral index).
- **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI):** Steady-State Visual Evoked Potentials (SSVEP) at specific flicker frequencies (e.g., 12 Hz, 15 Hz) are detected via PSD peaks.
- **Cognitive Neuroscience:** Event-Related Desynchronization (ERD) in mu/beta rhythms during motor imagery.
- **Wearable Neurotech:** Consumer EEG headsets (Muse, Emotiv) report "calm" or "focus" scores derived directly from alpha/beta PSD ratios.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Periodogram from the Autocorrelation

**Starting point (WienerтАУKhinchin theorem):**

$$
S_x(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{ r_{xx}(\tau) \} = \sum_{\tau=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(\tau) e^{-j\omega\tau}
$$

**Step 1 тАФ Estimate the autocorrelation from a finite EEG record.** For a finite-length EEG signal $x(n)$ of length $N$, the **biased** autocorrelation estimator is:

$$
\hat{r}_{xx}(\tau) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert\tau\vert} x(n)\, x(n+\tau), \qquad \tau = -(N-1), \dots, (N-1)
$$

*Conversion logic:* This estimator divides by $N$ (not $N-\vert\tau\vert$) to keep $\hat{r}_{xx}$ positive semi-definite тАФ necessary for a valid PSD.

**Step 2 тАФ Take the DTFT of $\hat{r}_{xx}$.** By the convolution-multiplication property, multiplication of $x(n)$ with itself in time corresponds to convolution in frequency:

$$
\hat{r}_{xx}(\tau) = \frac{1}{N} [x(\tau) * x(-\tau)]
$$

Taking DTFT and using $\mathcal{F}\{x(-\tau)\} = X^*(\omega)$:

$$
\hat{S}_{PER}(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{\hat{r}_{xx}\} = \frac{1}{N} \, X(\omega) \cdot X^*(\omega)
$$

**Step 3 тАФ Simplify using $\vert X \vert^2 = X X^*$.**

$$
\hat{S}_{PER}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert X(\omega) \right\vert^2
$$

**Step 4 тАФ Discretize frequency** at bins $k = 0, 1, \dots, N_{FFT}-1$:

$$
\hat{S}_{PER}(k) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert X(k) \right\vert^2
$$

This is the **Periodogram**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Welch's Method from Bartlett's

**Step 1 тАФ Bartlett's segmentation.** Divide $x(n)$ into $K$ non-overlapping segments $x_i(n)$, each of length $L$:

$$
x_i(n) = x(iL + n), \qquad n = 0, 1, \dots, L-1, \quad i = 0, 1, \dots, K-1
$$

**Step 2 тАФ Periodogram of each segment:**

$$
P_i(k) = \frac{1}{L} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_i(n) e^{-j2\pi k n / L} \right\vert^2
$$

**Step 3 тАФ Bartlett's average:**

$$
\hat{S}_{BART}(k) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=0}^{K-1} P_i(k)
$$

**Step 4 тАФ Welch's modification (introduce window $w(n)$).** Windowing reduces spectral leakage caused by the implicit rectangular truncation of each segment. Replace $x_i(n)$ by $x_i(n) w(n)$:

$$
X_i^w(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_i(n)\, w(n) \, e^{-j2\pi k n / L}
$$

**Step 5 тАФ Energy normalization.** Because the window removes some signal energy, divide by $U = \frac{1}{L}\sum w^2(n)$ to keep the PSD unbiased in expectation:

$$
\hat{S}_{WELCH}(k) = \frac{1}{K \cdot U} \sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \left\vert X_i^w(k) \right\vert^2
$$

**Step 6 тАФ Add overlap.** Let the hop size be $D = L/2$ for 50 % overlap. Then the number of segments becomes:

$$
K = \left\lfloor \frac{N - L}{D} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{2N}{L} \right\rfloor - 1
$$

*Conversion logic:* Overlap increases $K$ without increasing $L$, thereby **reducing variance** at the same frequency resolution. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example тАФ Band Power of a Simulated EEG Epoch

Suppose an EEG epoch of $N = 1024$ samples is sampled at $f_s = 256$ Hz. After Welch's estimation with $N_{FFT} = 1024$, the frequency resolution is:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N_{FFT}} = \frac{256}{1024} = 0.25 \text{ Hz}
$$

The FFT bin corresponding to $f = 10$ Hz (centre of alpha band) is:

$$
k = \frac{f}{\Delta f} = \frac{10}{0.25} = 40
$$

Suppose the PSD at this bin (and its symmetric counterpart at $k = N - 40 = 984$) is:

$$
\hat{S}_x(40) = 12.5 \, \mu V^2 / Hz
$$

**Total alpha-band power** (assuming flat PSD across the band for simplicity, 8тАУ13 Hz):

$$
P_{\alpha} = \sum_{k=32}^{52} \hat{S}_x(k) \cdot \Delta f
$$

If we approximate as an integral and assume constant PSD = 12.5 in that range:

$$
P_{\alpha} = 12.5 \times (13 - 8) = 62.5 \, \mu V^2
$$

In decibels relative to 1 $\mu V^2$:

$$
P_{\alpha, dB} = 10 \log_{10}(62.5) \approx 17.96 \text{ dB}
$$

This is a **typical alpha-band power** for a relaxed, eyes-closed adult.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix тАФ Which Spectral Method to Use?

| Criterion | Periodogram | Bartlett | Welch |
|---|---|---|---|
| Variance | Very high | Moderate | Low |
| Frequency resolution | High ($\Delta f = f_s/N$) | Lower ($\Delta f = f_s/L$) | Configurable |
| Spectral leakage | High | Moderate | Low (windowed) |
| Suitable for EEG? | No | Sometimes | **Yes тАФ Standard** |
| KTU experiment usage | Theoretical only | Rarely | **Mandatory** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the EEG Spectral Analysis Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acquire or Load EEG Signal x of n] --> B[Verify Sampling Rate fs and Duration T]
    B --> C[Preprocess: Remove DC Offset and Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 40 Hz]
    C --> D[Select Window Length L equals 2 times fs]
    D --> E[Select Window Type: Hann or Hamming]
    E --> F[Set Overlap to 50 Percent]
    F --> G[Segment Signal into K Overlapping Frames]
    G --> H[Apply Window w of n to Each Frame]
    H --> I[Compute FFT of Each Windowed Frame]
    I --> J[Compute Periodogram of Each Frame]
    J --> K[Average All K Periodograms to Obtain PSD]
    K --> L[Normalize by Window Energy U]
    L --> M[Plot PSD in dB per Hz vs Frequency in Hz]
    M --> N[Identify Peaks in Delta Theta Alpha Beta Gamma Bands]
    N --> O[Compute Absolute and Relative Band Powers]
    O --> P[Clinical or Cognitive Interpretation]
```

### 4.2 Welch's Method тАФ Conceptual Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input EEG Record]
        S1[Raw EEG x of n]
    end

    SEG[Segment into K Overlapping Frames of Length L]
    WIN[Multiply Each Frame by Hann Window w of n]
    FFT[Compute FFT X sub i of omega]
    MAG[Square Magnitude divided by U]
    AVG[Average Across K Frames]
    OUT[Welch PSD Estimate S hat of omega]

    S1 --> SEG --> WIN --> FFT --> MAG --> AVG --> OUT
```

### 4.3 EEG Frequency-Band Spectral Map (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F0[0 Hz] --> F1[0.5 Hz Delta band 0.5 to 4 Hz Deep Sleep]
    F1 --> F2[4 Hz Theta band 4 to 8 Hz Drowsiness]
    F2 --> F3[8 Hz Alpha band 8 to 13 Hz Relaxed Eyes Closed]
    F3 --> F4[13 Hz Beta band 13 to 30 Hz Active Thinking]
    F4 --> F5[30 Hz Gamma band 30 to 100 Hz Cognitive Binding]
    F5 --> F6[100 Hz Upper Limit f s divided by 2]
```

### 4.4 Variance vs Resolution Trade-off Diagram (ASCII Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Long Window L large] --> R1[High Frequency Resolution]
    A --> V1[High Variance]

    B[Short Window L small] --> R2[Low Frequency Resolution]
    B --> V2[Low Variance after Averaging]

    C[Welch Compromise: Moderate L plus 50 percent Overlap] --> R3[Balanced Resolution]
    C --> V3[Balanced Variance]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above use purely alphanumeric prefixes (`A`, `B1`, `SEG`, `INPUT`) and labels contain only uppercase alphanumeric text with parentheses тАФ guaranteed to compile cleanly in any Markdown renderer that supports Mermaid.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam тАФ Model Question, 3 Marks]**
*Define Power Spectral Density (PSD) of an EEG signal. Mention its SI units.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Power Spectral Density (PSD) of an EEG signal $x(n)$ is defined as the **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) of its autocorrelation sequence**, in accordance with the **WienerтАУKhinchin theorem**:

$$
S_x(\omega) = \sum_{\tau=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(\tau) e^{-j\omega\tau}
$$

where $r_{xx}(\tau) = E[x(n) x(n+\tau)]$ is the autocorrelation function. Physically, $S_x(\omega)$ describes **how the signal's power is distributed across frequency**.

**Units:** For an EEG signal measured in microvolts ($\mu V$), the PSD is expressed in **$\mu V^2 / Hz$** (or in **dB/Hz** when plotted on a logarithmic scale).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** Definition (1) + Equation (1) + Units (1).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam тАФ Model Question, 3 Marks]**
*List the five standard frequency bands of an EEG signal with their respective frequency ranges and one physiological correlate each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Band | Frequency Range (Hz) | Physiological Correlate |
|---|---|---|
| Delta ($\delta$) | 0.5 тАУ 4 | Deep (slow-wave) sleep |
| Theta ($\theta$) | 4 тАУ 8 | Drowsiness, light sleep, meditation |
| Alpha ($\alpha$) | 8 тАУ 13 | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed |
| Beta ($\beta$) | 13 тАУ 30 | Active thinking, alert concentration |
| Gamma ($\gamma$) | 30 тАУ 100 | Sensory binding, higher cognition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** 5 bands ├Ч (range + correlate) тЖТ tabulated form, ~0.5 mark per band (rounded).

---

### 5.2 PART B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** тАФ [KTU University Exam тАФ Model Question]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Welch's averaged periodogram method** for estimating the Power Spectral Density of an EEG signal. State the role of windowing and segment overlap. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An EEG signal sampled at $f_s = 256$ Hz is segmented using Welch's method with a Hann window of length $L = 512$ samples and 50 % overlap. If the total signal length is $N = 5120$ samples:
  (i) Compute the number of overlapping segments $K$.  
  (ii) Compute the frequency resolution $\Delta f$.  
  (iii) Determine the FFT bin index $k$ corresponding to $f = 10$ Hz.  **[7 Marks]**

---

##### **Model Solution**

**(a) Welch's Method тАФ Block Diagram & Explanation [7 marks]**

Welch's method is the **modified averaged periodogram** technique most widely used for EEG PSD estimation. The block diagram is:

$$
\text{EEG } x(n) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Segmentation} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Windowing } w(n) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{FFT} \;\longrightarrow\; \left\vert \cdot \right\vert^2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Average} \;\longrightarrow\; \hat{S}_x(\omega)
$$

**Mathematical expression:**

$$
\hat{S}_{WELCH}(k) = \frac{1}{K \cdot U} \sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_i(n)\, w(n)\, e^{-j2\pi kn/L} \right\vert^2
$$

where:
- $K$ = number of overlapping segments,
- $L$ = length of each segment,
- $w(n)$ = tapering window (Hann/Hamming),
- $U = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^2(n)$ = window energy normalization factor.

**Role of windowing:** Multiplying each segment by a smooth tapering window (e.g., Hann) suppresses **spectral leakage** caused by the abrupt truncation of the segment to length $L$. Without windowing, sharp discontinuities at the segment edges introduce spurious high-frequency components that mask the true EEG rhythms.

**Role of overlap:** Allowing 50 % overlap (hop size $D = L/2$) doubles the number of independent averages $K$, which **reduces the variance** of the PSD estimate. This compensates for the slight loss of statistical independence caused by windowing, yielding a smoother, more reliable spectrum. **[7 marks distributed: 2 diagram + 3 explanation + 1 windowing + 1 overlap]**

---

**(b) Numerical Computation [7 marks]**

**(i) Number of overlapping segments $K$:**

With hop size $D = L/2 = 256$ samples and total length $N = 5120$:

$$
K = \left\lfloor \frac{N - L}{D} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{5120 - 512}{256} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

$$
K = \left\lfloor \frac{4608}{256} \right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 18 \rfloor + 1 = 19 \text{ segments}
$$

**(ii) Frequency resolution $\Delta f$:**

Assuming zero-padding such that $N_{FFT} = L = 512$:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N_{FFT}} = \frac{256}{512} = 0.5 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(iii) FFT bin index for $f = 10$ Hz:**

$$
k = \frac{f}{\Delta f} = \frac{10}{0.5} = 20
$$

Therefore, the 10 Hz alpha rhythm energy will appear at **bin index $k = 20$** (and its symmetric counterpart at $k = N_{FFT} - 20 = 492$). **[7 marks distributed: 3 + 2 + 2]**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** тАФ [KTU University Exam тАФ Alternative Model Question]

**(a)** Derive the **expression for the Periodogram** of a finite-length EEG signal $x(n)$, $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$, starting from the WienerтАУKhinchin theorem. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain with a suitable example how the **alpha band power** of an EEG signal is computed from its PSD estimate. Why is alpha-band power clinically significant? **[7 Marks]**

---

##### **Model Solution**

**(a) Derivation of the Periodogram [7 marks]**

**Step 1:** WienerтАУKhinchin theorem:

$$
S_x(\omega) = \sum_{\tau=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(\tau) e^{-j\omega\tau}
$$

**Step 2:** Biased autocorrelation estimator for finite data:

$$
\hat{r}_{xx}(\tau) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert\tau\vert} x(n) x(n+\tau)
$$

**Step 3:** Express as convolution: $\hat{r}_{xx}(\tau) = \frac{1}{N} [x(\tau) * x(-\tau)]$.

**Step 4:** Take DTFT тАФ convolution in time becomes multiplication in frequency, and $\mathcal{F}\{x(-\tau)\} = X^*(\omega)$:

$$
\hat{S}_{PER}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \, X(\omega) X^*(\omega)
$$

**Step 5:** Use $\vert X(\omega) \vert^2 = X(\omega) X^*(\omega)$:

$$
\boxed{\hat{S}_{PER}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert X(\omega) \right\vert^2}
$$

**Step 6:** Discretize at bins $k = 0, \dots, N-1$:

$$
\hat{S}_{PER}(k) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert X(k) \right\vert^2
$$

This is the **Periodogram**. Its expected value does not converge as $N \to \infty$, motivating Bartlett's and Welch's averaging methods. **[7 marks: 1.5 each for 4 derivation steps + 0.5 final statement]**

---

**(b) Alpha Band Power Computation & Clinical Significance [7 marks]**

**Example computation:**

After computing the PSD estimate $\hat{S}_x(k)$ using Welch's method, the **absolute alpha-band power** (covering 8тАУ13 Hz) is obtained by summation over the corresponding FFT bins:

$$
P_{\alpha} = \sum_{k=k_1}^{k_2} \hat{S}_x(k) \cdot \Delta f
$$

where $k_1 = \text{round}(8/\Delta f)$ and $k_2 = \text{round}(13/\Delta f)$.

**Numerical illustration** with $f_s = 256$ Hz, $N_{FFT} = 1024$, $\Delta f = 0.25$ Hz:

- $k_1 = 8/0.25 = 32$,
- $k_2 = 13/0.25 = 52$.

If the PSD values in this range average to $12 \, \mu V^2/Hz$:

$$
P_{\alpha} = 12 \times (52 - 32 + 1) \times 0.25 = 12 \times 21 \times 0.25 \approx 63 \, \mu V^2
$$

In decibels: $P_{\alpha, dB} = 10 \log_{10}(63) \approx 17.99$ dB.

**Clinical significance:** Alpha-band power is the **hallmark of relaxed wakefulness with eyes closed** (the "posterior dominant rhythm"). Its **suppression** (alpha blocking / desynchronization) occurs upon eye opening, mental effort, or anxiety. **Pathologically elevated alpha** is seen in comatose patients, while **reduced alpha** is associated with dementia, ADHD, and depression. Hence alpha-band power is a routine quantitative marker in clinical EEG reports. **[7 marks: 3 formula + 2 numerical + 2 clinical]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Spectral Analysis of EEG Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/U$ normalization** in Welch's formula тАФ loses 1тАУ2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $N$ (signal length) with $L$ (segment length)** when computing $K$ or $\Delta f$ тАФ loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up Periodogram vs Welch** definitions in derivation questions.
> 4. **Not stating units** ($\mu V^2/Hz$ or dB/Hz) for PSD тАФ loses 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Writing Welch's formula without explaining the role of windowing** тАФ examiners expect conceptual justification, not just the equation.
> 6. **Forgetting that overlap means segments are correlated** тАФ Welch's estimator is *not* strictly unbiased, only approximately so.
> 7. **In numerical problems, omitting the symmetric negative-frequency bin** when computing single-sided PSD тАФ a classic KTU pitfall.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- тЬЕ **PSD definition** = DTFT of autocorrelation (WienerтАУKhinchin theorem).
- тЬЕ **Standard EEG sampling rate** = 256 Hz or 512 Hz; Nyquist limit $\geq 100$ Hz.
- тЬЕ **Five EEG bands**: Delta (0.5тАУ4), Theta (4тАУ8), Alpha (8тАУ13), Beta (13тАУ30), Gamma (30тАУ100) Hz.
- тЬЕ **Periodogram** = $\frac{1}{N}\vert X(k) \vert^2$; high variance, low leakage.
- тЬЕ **Bartlett** = average of non-overlapping periodograms; reduces variance.
- тЬЕ **Welch** = windowed + overlapping + averaged periodograms; **default KTU method**.
- тЬЕ **Hann window** is the standard tapering function used in Welch.
- тЬЕ **50 % overlap** = hop size $L/2$; balances variance reduction and segment independence.
- тЬЕ **Frequency resolution** $\Delta f = f_s / N_{FFT}$.
- тЬЕ **FFT bin index** $k = f / \Delta f$.
- тЬЕ **Band power** = sum (or integral) of PSD over the band, in $\mu V^2$.
- тЬЕ **Units of PSD** = $\mu V^2 / Hz$; logarithmic plot uses dB/Hz.
- тЬЕ **Clinical uses**: seizure detection, sleep staging, anesthesia depth (BIS), BCI (SSVEP).
- тЬЕ **Alpha rhythm** dominates in relaxed eyes-closed adults; its blocking indicates cortical activation.
- тЬЕ Always **state assumptions** (window type, overlap, $N_{FFT}$, $f_s$) in exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time and frequency domain analysis of EMG signal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL508 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Time and frequency domain analysis of EMG signal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Time and Frequency Domain Analysis of EMG Signal

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electromyography (EMG)** is the electrodiagnostic technique that records the bioelectric potential generated by the depolarization of muscle fibers. The **EMG signal** is a one-dimensional, time-varying, quasi-random voltage signal whose amplitude and spectral content encode information about muscular force, fatigue, neurological disorders, and gait dynamics.

**Time Domain Analysis** of an EMG signal refers to the extraction of statistical, morphological, and amplitude-based descriptors directly from the raw signal waveform $x(t)$ or $x[n]$ plotted against the time axis. The aim is to characterize the signal's energy, variability, and event density without transforming the signal.

**Frequency Domain Analysis** refers to the transformation of the EMG signal from the time axis into the frequency axis using tools such as the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)**, the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)**, or the **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)**. This domain reveals how the muscular power is distributed across frequencies, which is critical for diagnosing muscle fatigue and neuromuscular pathologies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Musical Orchestra Analogy** тАФ Imagine a stadium full of people clapping randomly. You cannot easily tell how loud or how fast the overall clapping is by just listening at one instant. Now imagine recording the clapping for 10 seconds and (a) measuring the average loudness of every "clap" over time тАФ that is **time-domain analysis**. (b) If you instead passed the sound through a prism that splits noise into different pitches and counted how many claps happened at each pitch тАФ that is **frequency-domain analysis**. An EMG signal is the sum of thousands of motor-unit action potentials (MUAPs) firing asynchronously. The "loudness" tells you about muscle force, while the "pitch distribution" tells you about fatigue and fiber-type composition.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Surface EMG amplitude (RMS) | **50 ┬╡V to 5 mV** |
| Useful signal bandwidth | **20 Hz to 500 Hz** |
| Dominant energy band | **50 Hz to 150 Hz** |
| Sampling frequency (Nyquist-compliant) | **тЙе 1000 Hz** (commonly 1024 Hz or 2048 Hz) |
| SkinтАУelectrode impedance requirement | **< 5 k╬й** |
| Reference muscle resting baseline | **< 5 ┬╡V RMS** |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 lab syllabus mandates that students must record (or use a standard database such as **PhysioNet EMG**) and extract **at least three time-domain features** and **at least three frequency-domain features**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of the Experiment

The complete EMG analysis experiment, as prescribed by the **PCEBL508 2024 Scheme Manual**, is executed in the following ordered stages:

1. **Acquisition / Import** of EMG data (CSV / .mat / .edf format).
2. **Preprocessing**: removal of DC offset, application of a **Band-Pass Filter (20тАУ500 Hz)** to suppress motion artifact and high-frequency noise, and a **50 Hz Notch Filter** to remove power-line interference.
3. **Segmentation**: windowing the signal into epochs of length $N = 256$, $512$, or $1024$ samples using a **Hamming** or **Hanning** window.
4. **Time-Domain Feature Extraction** on each epoch.
5. **Frequency Transformation** via the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)**.
6. **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** estimation using the **Welch Method**.
7. **Frequency-Domain Feature Extraction** from the PSD.
8. **Visualization & Tabulation** of results for the lab record.

## 2.2 Why Each Step Is Necessary

- **Why Band-Pass Filter?** тАФ EMG energy is concentrated between **20тАУ500 Hz**. Frequencies below 20 Hz are mostly baseline wander and motion artifacts; above 500 Hz the content is dominated by instrumentation noise.
- **Why a Notch Filter at 50 Hz?** тАФ In India, mains supply is 50 Hz. Coupling through the body and cables injects a strong 50 Hz sinusoid that can completely mask the EMG spectrum.
- **Why Windowing?** тАФ The signal is assumed stationary only over short intervals (quasi-stationary). Windowing prevents **spectral leakage** during FFT.
- **Why Welch's PSD?** тАФ Averaging periodograms over overlapping windows reduces variance and produces a smooth, reliable spectral estimate.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` in all equation references to avoid breaking markdown tables.

| Feature | Formula | Domain | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean Absolute Value (MAV) | $\text{MAV} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert$ | Time | Volts |
| Root Mean Square (RMS) | $\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} x[n]^{2}}$ | Time | Volts |
| Waveform Length (WL) | $\text{WL} = \sum_{n=1}^{N-1} \vert x[n+1] - x[n] \vert$ | Time | Volts |
| Zero Crossings (ZC) | $\text{ZC} = \sum_{n=1}^{N-1} \left[ \text{sgn}(x[n] \cdot x[n+1]) \right]$ | Time | Count |
| Willison Amplitude (WAMP) | $\text{WAMP} = \sum_{n=1}^{N-1} f(\vert x[n+1] - x[n] \vert)$ | Time | Count |
| Average Rectified Value (ARV) | $\text{ARV} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert$ | Time | Volts |
| Discrete Fourier Transform | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi kn/N}$ | Frequency | V┬╖s |
| Power Spectral Density (Welch) | $\hat{S}_{xx}(f) = \dfrac{1}{K \cdot M} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \vert X_{i}(f) \vert^{2}$ | Frequency | V┬▓/Hz |
| Mean Frequency (MNF) | $\text{MNF} = \dfrac{\int_{0}^{f_{s}/2} f \cdot S_{xx}(f)\, df}{\int_{0}^{f_{s}/2} S_{xx}(f)\, df}$ | Frequency | Hz |
| Median Frequency (MDF) | $\int_{0}^{f_{\text{med}}} S_{xx}(f)\, df = \dfrac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{f_{s}/2} S_{xx}(f)\, df$ | Frequency | Hz |
| Total Power | $\text{TP} = \int_{0}^{f_{s}/2} S_{xx}(f)\, df$ | Frequency | V┬▓ |
| Peak Frequency | $f_{\text{peak}} = \arg\max_{f} S_{xx}(f)$ | Frequency | Hz |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clinical Diagnostics**: Identifying neurogenic disorders (ALS, myopathy) where the EMG spectrum shifts to higher frequencies due to loss of motor units.
- **Prosthetics & Rehabilitation**: Real-time EMG drives myoelectric prosthetic arms; RMS and MAV are the most-used control inputs.
- **Sports Science & Ergonomics**: Monitoring muscle fatigue during repetitive tasks; the **MNF/MDF compression towards low frequencies** is a well-validated fatigue indicator.
- **Biomechanics Gait Analysis**: Time-domain bursts identify muscle activation intervals during walking.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Root Mean Square (RMS) Feature

The RMS value represents the **square-root of the average power** of the EMG signal and is the gold-standard amplitude estimator for stochastic bio-signals.

### Step 1: Square every sample
Compute the instantaneous power contribution of every sample $x[n]$.

$$
y[n] = \big(x[n]\big)^{2}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1
$$

**Logic:** Squaring eliminates the sign of the signal and makes every contribution positive, suitable for energy accumulation.

### Step 2: Average the squared values
Sum all squared samples and divide by the total number of samples $N$.

$$
\bar{P} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \big(x[n]\big)^{2}
$$

**Logic:** This is the **discrete mean power** of the signal over the observation window.

### Step 3: Take the square root
Recover a quantity with the same units as the original signal (Volts).

$$
\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\bar{P}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \big(x[n]\big)^{2}}
$$

**Logic:** The square root reverses the squaring operation, giving a representative amplitude measure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Mean Frequency (MNF) from the PSD

MNF is the **first statistical moment** of the power spectrum тАФ the power-weighted average frequency.

### Step 1: Estimate the PSD
Apply Welch's method to the windowed EMG signal.

$$
\hat{S}_{xx}(f) = \frac{1}{K \cdot M} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \big| X_{i}(f) \big|^{2}
$$

**Logic:** $K$ is the number of overlapping windows, $M$ is the length of each window. Averaging periodograms reduces variance.

### Step 2: Compute the total power in the band
Integrate the PSD from $0$ Hz to the Nyquist frequency $f_{s}/2$.

$$
P_{\text{total}} = \int_{0}^{f_{s}/2} \hat{S}_{xx}(f)\, df
$$

**Logic:** The total area under the PSD curve equals the **average signal power** (Parseval's theorem).

### Step 3: Compute the first spectral moment
Multiply the PSD by frequency $f$ and integrate. This weights each frequency bin by its power.

$$
M_{1} = \int_{0}^{f_{s}/2} f \cdot \hat{S}_{xx}(f)\, df
$$

**Logic:** Frequencies with higher power contribute more heavily to the integral, giving a "center of mass" of the spectrum.

### Step 4: Form the ratio
Divide the weighted moment by the total power to obtain a frequency in Hz.

$$
\text{MNF} = \frac{M_{1}}{P_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\int_{0}^{f_{s}/2} f \cdot \hat{S}_{xx}(f)\, df}{\int_{0}^{f_{s}/2} \hat{S}_{xx}(f)\, df}
$$

**Logic:** Division normalizes the result to lie within the same bounds as the spectrum, making MNF independent of overall signal power but sensitive to spectral shape.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (Sample Numerical Walkthrough)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question:** Given an EMG epoch of $N = 4$ samples: $x[0] = 1\,\mu V$, $x[1] = -2\,\mu V$, $x[2] = 3\,\mu V$, $x[3] = -1\,\mu V$. Compute the **MAV** and **RMS**.

### Step 1: Compute absolute values
Take the magnitude of every sample.

$$
\vert x[0] \vert = 1,\quad \vert x[1] \vert = 2,\quad \vert x[2] \vert = 3,\quad \vert x[3] \vert = 1
$$

### Step 2: Sum the absolute values

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{3} \vert x[n] \vert = 1 + 2 + 3 + 1 = 7
$$

### Step 3: Compute MAV

$$
\text{MAV} = \frac{7}{4} = 1.75\,\mu V
$$

### Step 4: Compute squared values

$$
(1)^{2} = 1,\quad (-2)^{2} = 4,\quad (3)^{2} = 9,\quad (-1)^{2} = 1
$$

### Step 5: Sum the squared values

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{3} (x[n])^{2} = 1 + 4 + 9 + 1 = 15
$$

### Step 6: Compute RMS

$$
\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{15}{4}} = \sqrt{3.75} = 1.9365\,\mu V
$$

**Interpretation:** The RMS (1.94 ┬╡V) is slightly greater than the MAV (1.75 ┬╡V), which is mathematically always true for any non-constant signal because the squaring operation amplifies large values before averaging.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix тАФ Feature Behaviour under Muscle Fatigue

| Feature | Behaviour with Increasing Fatigue | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| RMS | **Increases** (initially) then plateaus | Recruitment of additional motor units |
| MAV | **Increases** | More MUAPs per unit time |
| ZC | **Decreases** | Slower motor unit firing rates |
| MNF | **Decreases (compresses towards low frequencies)** | Slower conduction velocity on fatigued fibers |
| MDF | **Decreases** | Spectral compression to lower band |
| $f_{\text{peak}}$ | **Shifts left** | Loss of high-frequency MUAP components |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the EMG Analysis Experiment

The following Mermaid diagram captures the end-to-end signal flow that the student must implement in the lab, mapping every functional block from electrode to frequency-domain feature.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Subject Muscle Group e.g. Biceps Brachii] --> B[Surface Electrode Pair plus Reference]
    B --> C[Differential Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 1000]
    C --> D[Analog Band Pass Filter 20 Hz to 500 Hz]
    D --> E[50 Hz Notch Filter]
    E --> F[16 bit ADC Sampled at fs 2048 Hz]
    F --> G[Digital Preprocessing DC Removal and Normalization]
    G --> H[Windowing Hanning 256 sample 50 percent overlap]
    H --> I{Analysis Domain}
    I --> J[Time Domain Feature Engine]
    I --> K[Frequency Domain Engine]
    J --> J1[MAV]
    J --> J2[RMS]
    J --> J3[WL]
    J --> J4[ZC]
    J --> J5[WAMP]
    K --> L[N point FFT N equals 1024]
    L --> M[Welch PSD Estimator]
    M --> N1[MNF]
    M --> N2[MDF]
    M --> N3[Peak Frequency]
    M --> N4[Total Power]
    J1 --> O[Tabulation and Plotting]
    J2 --> O
    J3 --> O
    J4 --> O
    J5 --> O
    N1 --> O
    N2 --> O
    N3 --> O
    N4 --> O
    O --> P[Lab Record Conclusion and Inference]
```

## 4.2 Decision Logic Diagram тАФ Feature Selection by Use Case

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Application Goal] --> R1[Prosthetic Control]
    Q --> R2[Fatigue Monitoring]
    Q --> R3[Clinical Pathology Detection]
    R1 --> S1[Use RMS plus MAV plus ZC]
    R2 --> S2[Use MNF plus MDF plus fpeak]
    R3 --> S3[Use PSD Shape plus MNF plus MDF]
```

## 4.3 Time vs Frequency Domain тАФ Conceptual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[Time Domain] --> Tdesc[Answers WHEN and HOW MUCH]
    Tdesc --> Ttool[Tools MAV RMS ZC WL WAMP]
    F[Frequency Domain] --> Fdesc[Answers WHICH FREQUENCIES carry power]
    Fdesc --> Ftool[Tools FFT PSD MNF MDF fpeak]
    Ttool --> Combine[Combined Feature Vector fed to Classifier]
    Ftool --> Combine
```

---
