## SECTION 3 тАФ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Classification Performance Metrics from the Confusion Matrix

The **confusion matrix** is the foundational 2├Ч2 (binary) or N├ЧN (multi-class) table from which all evaluation metrics are derived.

For binary classification (Normal vs. Arrhythmic):
$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & \text{Predicted Normal} & \text{Predicted Arrhythmic} \\
\hline
\text{Actual Normal} & TN & FP \\
\hline
\text{Actual Arrhythmic} & FN & TP \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

#### Derivation of Accuracy

Accuracy is the probability that the classifier makes a correct prediction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{correct}) &= P(\text{Normal}) \cdot P(\text{predict N} \mid \text{actual N}) + P(\text{Arr}) \cdot P(\text{predict Arr} \mid \text{actual Arr}) \\
Acc &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} \\
Acc &= \frac{\text{Number of correct predictions}}{\text{Total number of predictions}} \\
Acc &\in [0, 1] \text{ or equivalently } [0\%, 100\%]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this works:** Each correctly classified sample (TP or TN) is summed in the numerator; the denominator is the entire dataset, which includes all four outcomes.

#### Derivation of Sensitivity (True Positive Rate)

Sensitivity is the proportion of *actual positives* correctly identified тАФ the **recall** of the positive class. It is critical for medical screening because a low Sensitivity implies **missed diagnoses** (false negatives are clinically dangerous).

$$
\begin{aligned}
Se &= P(\text{predict Arr} \mid \text{actual Arr}) \\
Se &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
\text{(since among all actual Arrhythmic cases, TP are correctly flagged and FN are missed)} \\
Se &\in [0, 1]
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Derivation of Specificity (True Negative Rate)

Specificity is the proportion of *actual negatives* correctly identified.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Sp &= P(\text{predict N} \mid \text{actual N}) \\
Sp &= \frac{TN}{TN + FP} \\
\text{(among all actual Normal cases, TN are correctly identified and FP are wrongly flagged)} \\
Sp &\in [0, 1]
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Derivation of Precision (Positive Predictive Value)

Precision is the proportion of *predicted positives* that are *truly positive* тАФ a measure of the classifier's reliability when it raises an alarm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Pr &= P(\text{actual Arr} \mid \text{predict Arr}) \\
Pr &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
\text{(among all cases flagged Arrhythmic, only TP are true alarms; FP are false alarms)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Derivation of F1-Score

F1 is the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Sensitivity. The harmonic mean is used (not the arithmetic mean) because it penalizes extreme imbalances тАФ if either $Pr$ or $Se$ is low, $F_1$ drops sharply.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2}{\frac{1}{Pr} + \frac{1}{Se}} \\
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot Pr \cdot Se}{Pr + Se} \\
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} \\
F_1 &\in [0, 1], \quad F_1 = 1 \text{ implies perfect classifier}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Harmonic Mean and not Arithmetic Mean?** Consider $Pr = 1.0$ and $Se = 0.1$ (predicts positives but misses 90% of them). Arithmetic mean gives $0.55$ (misleadingly "decent"); harmonic mean gives $F_1 \approx 0.18$ (correctly indicating poor performance). The harmonic mean is sensitive to small values, which is exactly the behavior required when *missing a positive case is life-threatening*.

### 3.2 Derivation of the SVM Decision Boundary

For a binary linear classifier, the decision hyperplane is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$
where $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the weight vector and $b$ is the bias.

The **margin** between the two parallel supporting hyperplanes ($\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = +1$ and $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = -1$) is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Margin} &= \frac{2}{\vert \mathbf{w} \vert}
\end{aligned}
$$
Maximizing the margin is equivalent to minimizing $\frac{1}{2}\vert \mathbf{w} \vert^2$, subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ for all training samples.

The **primal optimization problem** is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \quad & \frac{1}{2}\vert \mathbf{w} \vert^2 \\
\text{subject to} \quad & y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, N
\end{aligned}
$$

Using **Lagrange multipliers** $\alpha_i \geq 0$, the dual form becomes:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0, \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C
\end{aligned}
$$

The final decision function (with kernel trick for non-linear data):
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}) &= \text{sign}\left(\sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b\right)
\end{aligned}
$$
where the sum runs only over **support vectors** (samples with $\alpha_i > 0$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RBF Kernel for ECG?** ECG features are non-linearly separable in high-dimensional space. The **Radial Basis Function (RBF)** kernel maps the data to an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space, allowing a linear hyperplane to separate non-linear patterns (e.g., normal vs. PVC beats that differ subtly in QRS morphology).

### 3.3 Derivation of the Z-Score Normalization (Pre-processing Step)

Given a raw ECG signal segment $x = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N]$:

**Step 1 тАФ Compute the mean:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 тАФ Compute the standard deviation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 тАФ Apply the z-score transformation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{norm,i} &= \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The normalized signal $x_{norm}$ has $\mu_{norm} = 0$ and $\sigma_{norm} = 1$.

**Why needed?** ECG amplitude varies between patients (e.g., 0.5 mV to 3 mV). Normalization removes inter-patient amplitude variability, allowing the classifier to focus on **morphological patterns** rather than absolute voltage.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Confusion Matrix Computation

Suppose a classifier is tested on **200 ECG beats**:
- **TP = 80** (correctly classified Arrhythmic)
- **TN = 100** (correctly classified Normal)
- **FP = 5** (Normal wrongly flagged as Arrhythmic)
- **FN = 15** (Arrhythmic missed тАФ clinically critical!)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} = \frac{80+100}{200} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 \quad (90\%) \\
\text{Sensitivity} &= \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{80}{80+15} = \frac{80}{95} \approx 0.842 \quad (84.2\%) \\
\text{Specificity} &= \frac{TN}{TN+FP} = \frac{100}{100+5} = \frac{100}{105} \approx 0.952 \quad (95.2\%) \\
\text{Precision} &= \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{80}{80+5} = \frac{80}{85} \approx 0.941 \quad (94.1\%) \\
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot 0.941 \cdot 0.842}{0.941 + 0.842} = \frac{1.585}{1.783} \approx 0.889 \quad (88.9\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Interpretation:** The classifier has high Specificity (95.2%) тАФ very few false alarms on healthy patients. However, Sensitivity is only 84.2%, meaning **15 out of 95 arrhythmic beats are missed**. In a clinical setting, this is a concern and indicates the classifier needs improvement (e.g., via SMOTE, deeper network, or additional features).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Common Classifiers

| Classifier | Type | Strengths for ECG | Weaknesses | Typical Accuracy on MIT-BIH |
|------------|------|-------------------|------------|------------------------------|
| **KNN (k-Nearest Neighbors)** | Instance-based | Simple, no training phase | Slow inference, sensitive to noise | 92тАУ95% |
| **SVM (RBF kernel)** | Discriminative | Robust, high-dim data, small training set | Hyperparameter tuning ($C$, $\gamma$) | 96тАУ98% |
| **Random Forest** | Ensemble (bagging) | Handles non-linear, feature importance | Can overfit, large model size | 94тАУ97% |
| **1D-CNN** | Deep learning | End-to-end feature learning | Needs large data, black box | 98тАУ99.5% |
| **LSTM/GRU** | Recurrent DL | Captures temporal RR-interval dynamics | Computationally expensive | 97тАУ99% |

### 3.6 Feature Extraction Algorithm тАФ Step-by-Step Logical Trace

For each segmented beat $x = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{360}]$:

1. **Time-domain features:**
   - $f_1 = \max(x) - \min(x)$ (peak-to-peak amplitude)
   - $f_2 = \arg\max(x)$ (R-peak location in window)
   - $f_3 = \frac{1}{360}\sum_{i=1}^{360} x_i$ (mean amplitude)
   - $f_4 = \sqrt{\frac{1}{359}\sum_{i=1}^{360}(x_i - f_3)^2}$ (standard deviation)

2. **Wavelet features (db4, 4 levels):**
   - Compute approximation coefficients $cA_4$ and detail coefficients $cD_1, cD_2, cD_3, cD_4$.
   - Energy of each sub-band: $E_j = \sum_k \vert cD_j(k) \vert^2$.
   - Total energy: $E_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{4} E_j$.
   - Normalized energy ratios: $f_{5,6,7,8} = E_j / E_{total}$.

3. **Frequency-domain features:**
   - Compute FFT: $X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j 2\pi kn/N}$.
   - Power spectrum: $P(k) = \vert X(k) \vert^2$.
   - Dominant frequency: $f_9 = \arg\max_k P(k)$.
   - Spectral entropy: $f_{10} = -\sum_k p(k)\log_2 p(k)$, where $p(k) = P(k)/\sum P(k)$.

4. **RR-interval features:**
   - $f_{11} = RR_i$ (current RR interval in samples)
   - $f_{12} = \frac{RR_{i+1} - RR_i}{RR_i}$ (RR ratio, detects premature beats if $\ll 1$)

This yields a **12-dimensional feature vector** per beat, fed to the classifier.

### 4.5 Reference Python Implementation Skeleton (Lab Code)

```python
# Standard imports for the lab experiment
import numpy as np
import wfdb                          # PhysioNet MIT-BIH reader
from scipy.signal import butter, filtfilt, find_peaks
from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import (accuracy_score, confusion_matrix,
                             classification_report, roc_auc_score)
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Step 1 тАФ Load a record (e.g., record 100)
record = wfdb.rdrecord('100', pn_dir='mitdb')
annotation = wfdb.rdann('100', 'atr', pn_dir='mitdb')
ecg_signal = record.pvc_lead[0]   # MLII lead
fs = record.fs                     # 360 Hz
r_peaks = annotation.sample
beat_labels = annotation.symbol

# Step 2 тАФ Band-pass filter 0.5-40 Hz
b, a = butter(4, [0.5, 40], btype='band', fs=fs)
ecg_filtered = filtfilt(b, a, ecg_signal)

# Step 3 тАФ Pan-Tompkins-style R-peak refinement (optional; here use annotation R-peaks)
# Step 4 тАФ Segment beats
window = int(0.5 * fs)             # 180 samples each side
beats = []
labels = []
for i, r in enumerate(r_peaks):
    if r - window > 0 and r + window < len(ecg_filtered):
        beats.append(ecg_filtered[r-window : r+window])
        labels.append(beat_labels[i])

beats = np.array(beats)
labels = np.array(labels)

# Step 5 тАФ Feature extraction (example: time-domain features)
def extract_features(beat):
    f = []
    f.append(np.max(beat) - np.min(beat))   # peak-to-peak
    f.append(np.std(beat))                  # std deviation
    f.append(np.mean(np.abs(beat)))         # mean absolute value
    f.append(np.sum(beat**2))               # energy
    return f

X = np.array([extract_features(b) for b in beats])
y = labels

# Step 6 тАФ Train / test split
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

skf = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
for train_idx, test_idx in skf.split(X_scaled, y):
    X_train, X_test = X_scaled[train_idx], X_scaled[test_idx]
    y_train, y_test = y[train_idx], y[test_idx]

    # Step 7 тАФ Train classifier
    clf = SVC(kernel='rbf', C=10, gamma='scale', class_weight='balanced')
    clf.fit(X_train, y_train)

    # Step 8 тАФ Predict and evaluate
    y_pred = clf.predict(X_test)
    print("Accuracy:", accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred))
    print("Confusion Matrix:\n", confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred))
    print("Report:\n", classification_report(y_test, y_pred))
```

## SECTION 5 тАФ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam тАФ May 2024]**
Define the following terms in the context of arrhythmia classification: (a) Sensitivity, (b) Specificity, (c) F1-Score. Why is the harmonic mean used for F1 instead of the arithmetic mean?

**Model Answer:**

- **(a) Sensitivity** is the ratio of correctly identified arrhythmic beats (True Positives) to the total number of actual arrhythmic beats.
$$
Se = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$
It is also called the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** or **Recall**.

- **(b) Specificity** is the ratio of correctly identified normal beats (True Negatives) to the total number of actual normal beats.
$$
Sp = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}
$$
It is also called the **True Negative Rate (TNR)**.

- **(c) F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of Precision and Sensitivity, providing a single balanced metric.
$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot Pr \cdot Se}{Pr + Se}
$$

- **Why harmonic mean?** The harmonic mean penalizes extreme imbalances. If either Precision or Sensitivity is very low, F1 drops sharply, correctly reflecting poor classifier performance. The arithmetic mean would mask the deficiency (e.g., $Pr = 1.0$, $Se = 0.1$ yields arithmetic mean = 0.55 but $F_1 \approx 0.18$).

*(3 marks тАФ 1 mark each for definitions, 1 mark for the harmonic mean justification. Note: KTU also accepts the term "Recall" for Sensitivity.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]**
List and briefly explain the five stages of the **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** for R-peak detection in ECG signals. State the typical sampling frequency and refractory period.

**Model Answer:**

The Pan-Tompkins algorithm (1985) detects QRS complexes in real time using the following five stages:

1. **Band-pass filtering (5тАУ15 Hz)** тАФ Isolates the dominant QRS energy from P and T waves and high-frequency noise.
2. **Differentiation** тАФ Emphasizes the steep slope of the R-wave; output is $y(n) = x(n+1) - x(n-1)$.
3. **Squaring** тАФ Squares the differentiated signal point-by-point to make it strictly positive and amplify large values.
4. **Moving-window integration** тАФ Averages the squared signal over a 150 ms window to localize the R-peak and produce a smooth envelope.
5. **Adaptive thresholding with refractory period** тАФ Adjusts two thresholds (signal and noise levels) dynamically; a 200 ms refractory period prevents multiple detections of the same QRS.

**Typical sampling frequency:** **360 Hz** (MIT-BIH standard) or 500 Hz in clinical systems.

**Refractory period:** **200 ms** (тЙИ 333 bpm maximum detectable rate).

*(3 marks тАФ 1 mark for listing 5 stages, 1 mark for brief explanation, 1 mark for sampling frequency and refractory period.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common reasons students lose marks on this experiment:
> 1. **Confusing Sensitivity with Specificity** тАФ a classic 1тАУ2 mark loss. Mnemonic: *Sensitivity finds Sick* (positives), *Specificity confirms SNOt-sick* (negatives).
> 2. **Forgetting to specify the AAMI class mapping** (N, S, V, F, Q) when listing MIT-BIH labels тАФ examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing formulas without units or interpretation** тАФ every metric must be stated with its range and clinical meaning.
> 4. **Skipping the data leakage pitfall** тАФ students often normalize *before* splitting into train/test, causing test-set leakage. Always normalize *after* the split, or use a `Pipeline` with `StandardScaler` inside the `cross_val_score` loop.
> 5. **Not justifying the choice of classifier** тАФ always state *why* SVM / RF / CNN is chosen for ECG (e.g., "SVM with RBF kernel is chosen due to its robustness on small training sets and ability to handle non-linear QRS morphology").

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) тАФ Option 1

**[KTU University Exam тАФ June 2024, Module Experiment 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete machine learning pipeline for arrhythmia classification** from ECG signal acquisition to performance evaluation. Explain each block in detail. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** For a binary arrhythmia classifier tested on 500 beats, the following confusion matrix is obtained:
$$
\begin{aligned}
TP &= 180, \quad FP = 20 \\
FN &= 30, \quad TN = 270
\end{aligned}
$$
Compute the **Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, and F1-Score**. Comment on whether this classifier is suitable as a screening tool for cardiac arrhythmias. **[(7 marks)]**

#### Model Solution тАФ Question A

**Part (a) тАФ Pipeline (7 marks)**

The end-to-end machine learning pipeline for ECG arrhythmia classification consists of **six stages**:

1. **ECG Signal Acquisition (1 mark):**
   Source: PhysioNet MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database тАФ 48 records, 360 Hz, 11-bit resolution, тЙИ109,000 labeled beats. Each record contains a binary `.dat` file (raw signal) and an `.atr` file (beat annotations). Two leads are recorded: **MLII** (modified lead II) and **V1/V2/V4/V5**. Standard practice uses **MLII** as the primary analysis channel.

2. **Pre-processing (1 mark):**
   - **Band-pass filter (Butterworth, order 4тАУ6, 0.5тАУ40 Hz)** removes baseline wander (high-pass effect) and high-frequency muscle/EMG noise (low-pass effect).
   - **Notch filter at 50 Hz** (India) or 60 Hz (USA) suppresses powerline interference.
   - **Z-score normalization** (╬╝ = 0, ╧Г = 1) eliminates inter-patient amplitude variability.

3. **R-peak Detection (1 mark):**
   Pan-Tompkins algorithm produces accurate R-peak indices with 99.3% sensitivity on MIT-BIH. The 5 stages are: bandpass (5тАУ15 Hz) тЖТ differentiation тЖТ squaring тЖТ moving-window integration тЖТ adaptive thresholding with 200 ms refractory period.

4. **Beat Segmentation (1 mark):**
   A window of **┬▒180 samples (1 second total at 360 Hz)** is extracted around each R-peak, capturing the P-QRS-T complex. Each beat becomes a 360-dimensional vector.

5. **Feature Extraction (2 marks):**
   - **Time-domain:** RR interval, QRS duration, R-amplitude, peak-to-peak voltage, mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis.
   - **Frequency-domain:** FFT, PSD, dominant frequency, spectral entropy, band power ratios.
   - **Non-linear/wavelet:** Daubechies-4 (db4) wavelet coefficients, sample entropy, higher-order statistics.
   - Result: A feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ per beat, with $d$ typically 12тАУ50.

6. **Classification & Evaluation (1 mark):**
   - Train-test split (80-20) or 10-fold cross-validation.
   - Classifier: SVM (RBF), Random Forest, or 1D-CNN.
   - Output: AAMI class label (N, S, V, F, Q).
   - Evaluation: Confusion matrix, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1, ROC-AUC.

**Part (b) тАФ Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

Given: $TP = 180$, $FP = 20$, $FN = 30$, $TN = 270$, Total = 500.

**Accuracy (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Acc &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{180 + 270}{500} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.90 \\
Acc &= 90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sensitivity (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Se &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 30} = \frac{180}{210} \approx 0.8571 \\
Se &= 85.71\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Specificity (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Sp &= \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{270}{270 + 20} = \frac{270}{290} \approx 0.9310 \\
Sp &= 93.10\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Precision (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Pr &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 \\
Pr &= 90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**F1-Score (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot Pr \cdot Se}{Pr + Se} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.90 \cdot 0.8571}{0.90 + 0.8571} \\
&= \frac{1.5428}{1.7571} \approx 0.8780 \\
F_1 &= 87.80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Commentary (2 marks):**
- **Suitability for screening:** A screening test should have **high Sensitivity** (тЙе 95% preferred) to minimize missed diagnoses. Here, $Se = 85.71\%$ is **moderate** тАФ 30 out of 210 arrhythmic beats are missed, which is clinically risky for a screening tool.
- **Strengths:** High Specificity (93.10%) means few false alarms on healthy patients, and the overall Accuracy (90%) and F1 (87.80%) are reasonable.
- **Recommendation:** This classifier is **not yet suitable as a stand-alone screening tool** for cardiac arrhythmias. It can be used as a *first-pass filter* to prioritize cases for human cardiologist review, but must be improved (e.g., using SMOTE to balance classes, deeper features, or 1D-CNN) before clinical deployment.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam тАФ June 2024, Module Experiment 2, 14 Marks тАФ Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **SVM classifier** with the RBF kernel for arrhythmia classification. Derive the decision function and state the role of the parameters **C** and **╬│ (gamma)**. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** Describe the **time-domain and wavelet-domain feature extraction** methods for ECG beat classification. Give at least **four examples** of each type of feature. **[(7 marks)]**

#### Model Solution тАФ Question B

**Part (a) тАФ SVM with RBF Kernel (7 marks)**

**SVM Theory (3 marks):**
The **Support Vector Machine** is a discriminative classifier that finds the **optimal hyperplane** maximizing the margin between two classes. For non-linearly separable ECG features (e.g., normal vs. PVC beats), the **kernel trick** is used to map the data into a higher-dimensional feature space where it becomes linearly separable.

The **decision hyperplane** is:
$$
\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0
$$

The **decision function** is:
$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b\right)
$$
where $SV$ denotes the set of support vectors, $\alpha_i$ are the Lagrange multipliers, $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ are class labels, and $K$ is the kernel function.

**RBF Kernel (2 marks):**
The **Radial Basis Function (Gaussian) kernel** is:
$$
K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\left(-\gamma \vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \vert^2\right)
$$
It maps data to an **infinite-dimensional Hilbert space**, capturing non-linear QRS morphology differences (e.g., wide QRS in PVC vs. narrow QRS in normal beats).

**Role of parameters (2 marks):**
- **C (regularization parameter):** Controls the trade-off between margin maximization and classification error. 
  - Large C тЖТ low bias, high variance (overfitting).
  - Small C тЖТ high bias, low variance (underfitting).
- **╬│ (gamma, kernel coefficient):** Controls the width of the RBF influence region.
  - Large ╬│ тЖТ narrow Gaussian, complex decision boundary, risk of overfitting.
  - Small ╬│ тЖТ wide Gaussian, smoother boundary, may underfit.
- Typical tuning: Grid search with 5-fold cross-validation over $C \in \{0.1, 1, 10, 100\}$ and $\gamma \in \{0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1\}$.

**Part (b) тАФ Feature Extraction Methods (7 marks)**

**Time-Domain Features (3.5 marks):**
Time-domain features are computed directly from the raw or filtered ECG amplitude values. They capture morphological and statistical properties of the beat.

1. **RR Interval:** Time between consecutive R-peaks. $RR_i = (R_{i+1} - R_i) / f_s$ in seconds. Premature beats show short RR, compensatory pauses show long RR.
2. **QRS Duration:** Width of the QRS complex measured at half the maximum amplitude. Normal QRS тЙИ 80тАУ120 ms; PVC has QRS > 120 ms.
3. **R-peak Amplitude:** Maximum absolute voltage in the beat window, indicating ventricular depolarization magnitude.
4. **Peak-to-Peak Voltage:** $\max(x) - \min(x)$ over the window.
5. **Mean Absolute Value (MAV):** $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert x_i \vert$.
6. **Skewness & Kurtosis:** Higher-order statistical moments capturing asymmetry and tail-heaviness of the amplitude distribution.

**Wavelet-Domain Features (3.5 marks):**
The **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)** using the **Daubechies-4 (db4)** mother wavelet decomposes the beat into multi-scale sub-bands, capturing both time and frequency information.

For a 4-level decomposition, the beat is split into approximation coefficients $cA_4$ and detail coefficients $cD_1, cD_2, cD_3, cD_4$, corresponding to frequency bands $[fs/2, fs/4, fs/8, fs/16, fs/32]$.

1. **Sub-band Energy:** $E_j = \sum_k \vert cD_j(k) \vert^2$ for $j = 1, 2, 3, 4$. Energy concentrates differently in normal vs. arrhythmic beats.
2. **Normalized Energy Ratios:** $E_j / E_{total}$ where $E_{total} = \sum_j E_j$. Provides scale-invariant features.
3. **Coefficient Standard Deviation:** $\sigma_j = \text{std}(cD_j)$. Indicates variability in each sub-band.
4. **Coefficient Mean Absolute Value:** $\frac{1}{K}\sum_k \vert cD_j(k) \vert$.
5. **Wavelet Entropy:** $H = -\sum_j p_j \log_2 p_j$ where $p_j = E_j / E_{total}$. Quantifies the energy distribution across scales.
6. **Higher-Order Statistics of Sub-bands:** Skewness and kurtosis of $cD_j$.

**Why wavelets?** Wavelets provide **multi-resolution analysis** тАФ the QRS complex (high-frequency) is captured in $cD_1, cD_2$ while the T-wave (low-frequency) appears in $cD_3, cD_4$. This makes wavelet features particularly discriminative for **morphologically distinct** beats like PVC and LBBB.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ Module Experiment 2**

**A. Core Concepts**
- Arrhythmia classification is a **supervised pattern recognition** problem with discrete class labels.
- **AAMI 5-class scheme:** N, S, V, F, Q.
- **MIT-BIH Database:** 48 records, 360 Hz, тЙИ109,000 beats тАФ the standard benchmark.
- **Pan-Tompkins Algorithm:** 5 stages тАФ bandpass тЖТ differentiate тЖТ square тЖТ integrate тЖТ adaptive threshold.
- **Refractory period:** 200 ms prevents multiple R-peak detections within one cardiac cycle.

**B. Pre-processing**
- Band-pass filter: **0.5тАУ40 Hz** (clinical monitoring), **0.05тАУ150 Hz** (diagnostic).
- Notch filter at **50/60 Hz** for powerline interference.
- **Z-score normalization** removes inter-patient amplitude variability.

**C. Feature Extraction**
- **Time-domain:** RR interval, QRS duration, R-amplitude, mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis.
- **Frequency-domain:** FFT, PSD, spectral entropy, dominant frequency.
- **Wavelet-domain:** Daubechies-4 (db4), 4-level DWT, sub-band energies and ratios.
- **Non-linear:** Sample entropy, approximate entropy, HOS.

**D. Classifiers**
- **SVM (RBF):** Best for small datasets, non-linear boundaries, robust.
- **Random Forest:** Handles non-linearity, provides feature importance.
- **1D-CNN:** End-to-end, state-of-the-art accuracy (98тАУ99.5%), needs large data.
- **LSTM/GRU:** Captures temporal RR-interval dynamics.

**E. Evaluation Metrics**
- **Accuracy** = (TP+TN) / Total
- **Sensitivity (Recall)** = TP / (TP+FN) тАФ *finds sick*
- **Specificity** = TN / (TN+FP) тАФ *finds not-sick*
- **Precision (PPV)** = TP / (TP+FP)
- **F1-Score** = 2┬╖Pr┬╖Se / (Pr+Se) тАФ harmonic mean
- **ROC-AUC** тАФ threshold-independent performance

**F. Common Pitfalls**
- **Data leakage:** Normalize *after* train-test split, not before.
- **Class imbalance:** Use SMOTE or class-weight balancing (MIT-BIH has ~70% N beats).
- **Confusing TP/FP/FN/TN:** TN is the top-left of the confusion matrix.
- **Reporting only accuracy:** Always report per-class Sensitivity and Specificity, especially in imbalanced data.
- **Skipping cross-validation:** A single 80-20 split can give misleading results; use 10-fold CV.

**G. Clinical Relevance**
- High Sensitivity (тЙе 95%) is **mandatory for screening**; high Specificity is for confirmatory tests.
- The **Apple Heart Study (2019)** used a CNN-based classifier on Apple Watch to detect atrial fibrillation with **Sensitivity 71%, Specificity 78%** in the initial cohort тАФ illustrating the real-world challenge.
- ECG signal standards: **IEC 60601-2-25, IEC 60601-2-27** for diagnostic and monitoring ECGs.

**H. Key Numbers to Memorize**
- MIT-BIH sampling frequency: **360 Hz**
- Pan-Tompkins bandpass: **5тАУ15 Hz**
- Pan-Tompkins refractory period: **200 ms**
- Integration window: **150 ms**
- Standard beat window: **┬▒180 samples** (1 second)
- Normal QRS duration: **80тАУ120 ms**
- Normal heart rate: **60тАУ100 bpm**
- AAMI 5 classes: **N, S, V, F, Q**
- Threshold for high-Sensitivity screening: **тЙе 95%**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of speech signal (identification of voiced, unvoiced and silent portion, etc.)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL508 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis of speech signal (identification of voiced, unvoiced and silent portion, etc.)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Speech Signal?

A **speech signal** is a non-stationary, one-dimensional acoustic pressure waveform $s(t)$ that is produced by the human vocal apparatus. Mathematically, it is a slowly time-varying signal whose statistical properties (amplitude, frequency content, energy) change over time as the speaker produces different phonemes.

The speech production model follows the source-filter theory:

$$s(t) = e(t) * h(t)$$

where:
- $e(t)$ is the **excitation signal** (voiced pulse train, unvoiced noise, or silence)
- $h(t)$ is the **vocal tract impulse response** (time-varying filter)
- $*$ denotes convolution

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCEBL508):** The experiment focuses on segmenting a continuous speech waveform into three fundamental classes тАФ **voiced**, **unvoiced**, and **silent** regions тАФ using time-domain features such as Short-Time Energy (STE) and Zero Crossing Rate (ZCR).

---

## 1.2 The Three Fundamental Regions of Speech

| Region | Physical Origin | Waveform Shape | Amplitude | Frequency Content |
|--------|----------------|----------------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Voiced** | Vocal fold vibration (glottal pulses) | Quasi-periodic, sharp peaks | **High** | Low (60тАУ400 Hz fundamental + harmonics) |
| **Unvoiced** | Turbulent airflow at constrictions (fricatives) | Random, noise-like | **Medium / Low** | High (broadband noise) |
| **Silent** | No speech (background noise / pause) | Near-flat, low-amplitude | **Very Low** | Broadband, low-level noise |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voiced sounds** in English include vowels (a, e, i, o, u) and voiced consonants (b, d, g, m, n, l, r, v, z). Examples: */b/* in "bat", */a/* in "cat".
>
> **Unvoiced sounds** include fricatives and aspirates. Examples: */f/* in "fan", */s/* in "sun", */sh/* in "ship", */h/* in "hat".
>
> **Silent portions** correspond to pauses between words, phrases, or breathing intervals.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Imagine a garden hose with a nozzle at the end.**

- **Voiced sound** = When the hose is connected to a vibrating tuning fork (vocal cords). The vibration creates **regular, periodic pulses** of water тАФ like a heart beating in a steady rhythm. You can *predict* when the next pulse will come.

- **Unvoiced sound** = When you partially block the nozzle with your thumb, causing water to **spray chaotically** in all directions. The pattern is **random and unpredictable** тАФ like static on an old TV.

- **Silent region** = When you simply turn off the water. The hose is connected, but **nothing flows** тАФ you only hear ambient sounds (wind, distant traffic).

The brain of an Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) system must perform the same classification in real time. Short-Time Energy (STE) and Zero Crossing Rate (ZCR) are the two "senses" it uses to distinguish these three conditions.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in This Experiment

- **Sampling frequency** $F_s = \mathbf{8000\ Hz}$ (telephony standard) or $\mathbf{16000\ Hz}$ (wideband) тАФ bold per KTU convention.
- **Frame duration** $= \mathbf{20\text{тАУ}30\ ms}$ (standard for short-time analysis).
- **Frame shift** $= \mathbf{10\ ms}$ (50% overlap between consecutive frames).
- **Window function** $=$ **Hamming / Hanning** (to reduce spectral leakage at frame edges).
- **Pitch range for adult human voice** $F_0 = \mathbf{60\text{тАУ}400\ Hz}$ (males lower, females higher).

> [!TIP]
> Why 20тАУ30 ms per frame? Because within this interval, the speech signal can be assumed to be **quasi-stationary** (its statistical properties remain approximately constant). Shorter frames lose frequency resolution; longer frames violate the stationarity assumption.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Analysis Pipeline

The experiment follows a structured pipeline. Each step is essential and is graded individually in the KTU lab evaluation rubric.

### Step 1: Signal Acquisition
The speech signal $x(n)$ is recorded at a sampling rate $F_s$ satisfying the **Nyquist criterion** $F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$, where $F_{max} \approx 4000\ Hz$ for telephone speech. Therefore, $F_s = 8000\ Hz$ is the minimum acceptable.

### Step 2: Pre-emphasis Filtering
A first-order high-pass filter is applied to boost the higher frequency components (typically attenuated by the lips radiation effect):

$$y(n) = x(n) - \alpha \cdot x(n-1)$$

where $\alpha$ is the pre-emphasis coefficient, typically $0.95 \leq \alpha \leq 0.97$.

> **Why?** The voiced speech spectrum falls off at approximately **-12 dB/octave** above 800 Hz. Pre-emphasis flattens this slope, improving the performance of subsequent feature extraction (especially for voiced/unvoiced discrimination).

### Step 3: Frame Blocking
The pre-emphasized signal is sliced into overlapping frames of $N$ samples each. For $F_s = 8000\ Hz$ and 25 ms frame duration:

$$N = F_s \times T_{frame} = 8000 \times 0.025 = 200\ samples$$

The next frame starts $M$ samples later (with $M \approx N/2 = 100$ samples, i.e., 50% overlap).

### Step 4: Windowing
Each frame $x_i(m)$ is multiplied by a window $w(m)$:

$$x_i^{(w)}(m) = x_i(m) \cdot w(m), \quad m = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

Common choice: **Hamming window**:

$$w(m) = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi m}{N-1}\right)$$

### Step 5: Feature Computation
For each windowed frame, compute the two principal discriminative features:

#### 5a. Short-Time Energy (STE)
Measures the signal amplitude / power in a frame.

$$E_i = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} \left[ x_i^{(w)}(m) \right]^2$$

#### 5b. Zero Crossing Rate (ZCR)
Counts how many times the signal changes sign within a frame.

$$Z_i = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{m=1}^{N-1} \left\vert \operatorname{sgn}\left[x_i^{(w)}(m)\right] - \operatorname{sgn}\left[x_i^{(w)}(m-1)\right] \right\vert$$

where:

$$\operatorname{sgn}[x] = \begin{cases} 1, & x \geq 0 \\ -1, & x < 0 \end{cases}$$

### Step 6: Classification
Apply thresholds to STE and ZCR to label each frame:

| Frame Type | STE Magnitude | ZCR Magnitude |
|------------|---------------|---------------|
| **Voiced** | **High** ($E_i > E_{high}$) | **Low** ($Z_i < Z_{low}$) |
| **Unvoiced** | **Medium / Low** | **High** ($Z_i > Z_{high}$) |
| **Silent** | **Very Low** ($E_i < E_{low}$) | **Low / Irrelevant** |

### Step 7: Pitch Estimation (for voiced regions only)
Apply autocorrelation to voiced frames to find the fundamental frequency $F_0$:

$$R_i(k) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1-k} x_i^{(w)}(m) \cdot x_i^{(w)}(m+k)$$

The lag $k^*$ corresponding to the maximum of $R_i(k)$ (excluding $k=0$) gives the pitch period $T_0 = k^* / F_s$, hence $F_0 = F_s / k^*$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | Used For |
|---|---------|----------------|----------------------|----------|
| 1 | $E_i = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} \left[x_i^{(w)}(m)\right]^2$ | Short-time energy of $i$-th frame | Joules (normalized) | Amplitude measure |
| 2 | $Z_i = \tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{m=1}^{N-1} \vert \operatorname{sgn}[x(m)] - \operatorname{sgn}[x(m-1)] \vert$ | Zero crossing rate | Crossings per frame | Frequency measure |
| 3 | $R_i(k) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1-k} x(m) \cdot x(m+k)$ | Autocorrelation at lag $k$ | Normalized [-1, 1] | Pitch detection |
| 4 | $F_0 = F_s / k^*$ | Fundamental frequency | Hz | Pitch in Hz |
| 5 | $y(n) = x(n) - \alpha x(n-1)$ | Pre-emphasis | $\alpha = 0.95$ | Spectral flattening |
| 6 | $w(m) = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos(2\pi m/(N-1))$ | Hamming window | Dimensionless | Spectral leakage reduction |
| 7 | $N = F_s \cdot T_{frame}$ | Frame length in samples | 200 (for 25 ms at 8 kHz) | Frame blocking |
| 8 | $F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$ | Nyquist criterion | $F_s \geq 8000$ Hz | Sampling |
| 9 | $\text{AMDF}(k) = \sum_{m} \vert x(m) - x(m+k) \vert$ | Average Magnitude Diff. Function | Samples | Pitch (alt. method) |
| 10 | $w(m) = 0.5 - 0.5 \cos(2\pi m/(N-1))$ | Hanning window | Dimensionless | Alternative to Hamming |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Voiced/unvoiced/silence classification is the **front-end of every modern speech codec** (GSM, AMR, MELP) and is the first stage of every **Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR)** system. Voiced frames are encoded with higher bits because they carry more linguistic information; silent frames are encoded with the fewest bits or not transmitted at all (DTX тАФ Discontinuous Transmission in GSM, saving up to 50% battery in mobile phones).

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Feature Choice

| Feature | Discriminates because... |
|---------|--------------------------|
| **STE** | Voiced sounds have **large glottal pulses** тЖТ high energy. Silent regions have only **microphone/ambient noise** тЖТ very low energy. |
| **ZCR** | Unvoiced sounds are **high-frequency noise** тЖТ many zero crossings per ms. Voiced sounds are **low-frequency periodic** тЖТ few zero crossings. |
| **Autocorrelation** | Voiced signals are **quasi-periodic** тЖТ autocorrelation shows a strong peak at the pitch period. Unvoiced/silent signals are **random** тЖТ autocorrelation drops off quickly. |

> Together, STE and ZCR form a **two-dimensional feature space** where voiced, unvoiced, and silent clusters are well separated тАФ a simple linear/threshold classifier suffices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the STE-Based Threshold Decision Rule

### Goal
Derive the condition for deciding that a frame $i$ is **voiced** based on its STE $E_i$.

### Step 1: Define the null hypothesis
Let $H_0$: the frame contains **only background noise** (silent or unvoiced-low).

Under $H_0$, the frame energy follows approximately a chi-squared distribution. For high $N$, by the Central Limit Theorem:

$$E_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_n, \sigma_n^2)$$

where $\mu_n$ and $\sigma_n^2$ are the mean and variance of the noise energy.

### Step 2: Define the alternative hypothesis
Let $H_1$: the frame contains **voiced speech**.

Under $H_1$:

$$E_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_v, \sigma_v^2), \quad \text{with} \quad \mu_v \gg \mu_n$$

### Step 3: Likelihood ratio test
We choose $H_1$ if:

$$\frac{P(E_i \mid H_1)}{P(E_i \mid H_0)} > \eta$$

For Gaussian distributions, this simplifies to a threshold test:

$$E_i > E_{th} \implies \text{Voiced}$$

where the threshold $E_{th}$ is computed from a desired false-alarm probability $\alpha$:

$$E_{th} = \mu_n + \sigma_n \cdot Q^{-1}(\alpha)$$

with $Q$ being the complementary Gaussian CDF.

### Step 4: Practical simplification
In the lab experiment, we use an empirical threshold (typically 3тАУ5 times the **mean energy of the silent region**):

$$E_{th} = 4 \cdot \bar{E}_{silent}$$

> This is exactly why the lab manual asks you to first identify the silent region and compute its mean energy **before** applying the classifier.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Autocorrelation-Based Pitch Estimator

### Step 1: Model the voiced signal
A voiced frame can be modeled as a periodic train of glottal pulses:

$$x(m) = p(m) * \sum_{r=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(m - r T_0)$$

where $p(m)$ is one period of the pulse and $T_0$ is the pitch period in samples.

### Step 2: Substitute into the autocorrelation formula

$$R(k) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1-k} x(m) \cdot x(m+k)$$

### Step 3: Recognize the periodic property
Because $x(m + T_0) \approx x(m)$ for voiced speech, the autocorrelation is also approximately periodic:

$$R(k + T_0) \approx R(k)$$

### Step 4: Find the first non-zero peak
Excluding the trivial peak at $k = 0$ (which is the energy), the next peak in $R(k)$ occurs at $k = T_0$.

$$T_0 = \arg\max_{k \in [k_{min}, k_{max}]} R(k), \quad k \neq 0$$

with bounds set by plausible pitch range:
- $k_{min} = F_s / 400 = 8000/400 = 20$ samples
- $k_{max} = F_s / 60 = 8000/60 \approx 133$ samples

### Step 5: Convert lag to Hz

$$F_0 = \frac{F_s}{T_0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** If $T_0 = 80$ samples at $F_s = 8000$ Hz, then $F_0 = 8000/80 = 100$ Hz, which is in the typical male adult pitch range.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix (Final Classification Rule)

The following table is **the most important deliverable** of the experiment and is graded heavily in the KTU lab record.

| Condition on STE ($E_i$) | Condition on ZCR ($Z_i$) | Classification |
|---------------------------|---------------------------|----------------|
| $E_i < E_{low}$ | Any | **Silent** |
| $E_i \geq E_{low}$ AND $E_i < E_{high}$ | $Z_i \geq Z_{th}$ | **Unvoiced** |
| $E_i \geq E_{low}$ AND $E_i < E_{high}$ | $Z_i < Z_{th}$ | **Weak voiced / transition** |
| $E_i \geq E_{high}$ | $Z_i < Z_{th}$ | **Strong voiced** |
| $E_i \geq E_{high}$ | $Z_i \geq Z_{th}$ | **Voiced fricative (mixed)** |

where typical empirical values (for 8 kHz, 25 ms frames, normalized to [-1, 1]):

$$E_{low} \approx 0.005, \quad E_{high} \approx 0.05, \quad Z_{th} \approx 30\ \text{crossings/frame}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students compute STE and ZCR but forget to **normalize the signal** to [-1, 1] first. If the raw 16-bit PCM amplitude is used (range ┬▒32768), the energy values will be off by a factor of $\approx 10^9$, making thresholding meaningless. Always normalize: $x_{norm} = x / \max(\vert x \vert)$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

Let $F_s = 8000$ Hz, frame size $N = 200$ samples, and consider a frame containing a single sinusoid of frequency $F_0 = 200$ Hz with amplitude $A = 0.5$.

### Compute STE
For a discrete sinusoid $x(m) = A \sin(2\pi F_0 m / F_s)$:

$$E_i = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} A^2 \sin^2\left(\frac{2\pi F_0 m}{F_s}\right) = A^2 \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} \frac{1 - \cos(4\pi F_0 m / F_s)}{2}$$

Since the cosine term averages to nearly zero over many full periods:

$$E_i \approx \frac{A^2 \cdot N}{2} = \frac{0.25 \times 200}{2} = 25$$

### Compute ZCR
The number of zero crossings per second of a sinusoid of frequency $F_0$ is $2 F_0$ (each period has 2 crossings). In a frame of duration $T_{frame} = N / F_s = 200/8000 = 0.025$ s:

$$Z_i \approx 2 F_0 \cdot T_{frame} = 2 \times 200 \times 0.025 = 10\ \text{crossings/frame}$$

### Decision
With $E_i = 25 \gg E_{high} = 0.05$ and $Z_i = 10 < Z_{th} = 30$, the frame is correctly classified as **voiced**. тЬУ

> If we replaced the sinusoid with white noise of the same amplitude, $Z_i$ would jump to $\approx 80$ (since white noise crosses zero at the Nyquist rate of $F_s/2$ crossings per second), and the frame would be classified as **unvoiced**. тЬУ

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]
**Differentiate between voiced, unvoiced, and silent regions of a speech signal in terms of waveform characteristics and feature values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Voiced | Unvoiced | Silent |
|----------|--------|----------|--------|
| **Source of excitation** | Periodic glottal pulses | Turbulent airflow | No excitation |
| **Waveform shape** | Quasi-periodic | Random noise-like | Near-flat |
| **Short-Time Energy (STE)** | **High** | **Medium / Low** | **Very Low** |
| **Zero Crossing Rate (ZCR)** | **Low** | **High** | **Low** |
| **Pitch (FтВА)** | **Yes (60тАУ400 Hz)** | **No** | **No** |
| **Examples** | Vowels, /b/, /d/, /g/ | /s/, /f/, /sh/ | Pauses between words |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]
**Define Short-Time Energy (STE) and Zero Crossing Rate (ZCR). State one application of each in speech processing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Short-Time Energy (STE):** It is the sum of squared samples of a windowed speech frame, defined as $E_i = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} [x_i^{(w)}(m)]^2$. **Application (1 mark):** Used for endpoint detection (locating start/end of speech) and voiced/silence discrimination.

- **Zero Crossing Rate (ZCR):** It is the number of times the speech signal changes sign within a frame, defined as $Z_i = \tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{m=1}^{N-1} \vert \operatorname{sgn}[x(m)] - \operatorname{sgn}[x(m-1)] \vert$. **Application (1 mark):** Used to discriminate unvoiced sounds (high ZCR) from voiced sounds (low ZCR), and is also a coarse measure of spectral flatness.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS тАФ INTERNAL CHOICE)

### тнР Question A (Choice 1) тАФ 14 Marks [KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]

**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the step-by-step procedure for analyzing a speech signal to identify voiced, unvoiced, and silent regions. **(7 marks)**

**b)** For a voiced speech frame sampled at $F_s = 8000$ Hz, the autocorrelation shows peaks at lags $k = 0$ and $k = 72$ samples. Compute the fundamental frequency $F_0$ and state the speaker's likely demographic. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (a) тАФ 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** **Signal Acquisition.** Record or load the speech signal $x(n)$ sampled at $F_s = 8000$ Hz or $16000$ Hz, satisfying the Nyquist criterion $F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** **Normalization.** Scale the signal: $x_{norm}(n) = x(n) / \max(\vert x(n) \vert)$ so that amplitudes lie in $[-1, +1]$. This makes threshold values reproducible.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** **Pre-emphasis.** Apply $y(n) = x(n) - 0.95 x(n-1)$ to flatten the spectrum.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **Frame Blocking + Windowing.** Divide the signal into frames of 25 ms ($N = 200$ samples) with 50% overlap, multiply each frame by a Hamming window $w(m) = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos(2\pi m/(N-1))$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Feature Computation.** For each frame $i$, compute $E_i = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} [x_i^{(w)}(m)]^2$ and $Z_i = \tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{m=1}^{N-1} \vert \operatorname{sgn}[x_i^{(w)}(m)] - \operatorname{sgn}[x_i^{(w)}(m-1)] \vert$.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** **Classification using thresholds.** Apply the decision matrix:
- If $E_i < E_{low} \Rightarrow$ Silent
- If $E_i \geq E_{low}$ and $Z_i \geq Z_{th} \Rightarrow$ Unvoiced
- If $E_i \geq E_{high}$ and $Z_i < Z_{th} \Rightarrow$ Voiced

**Step 7 (1 mark):** **Visualization & Pitch Estimation.** Plot the original waveform, STE, ZCR, and the classification labels. For voiced frames, compute $F_0$ from autocorrelation peak.

---

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (b) тАФ 7 marks

**Given:** $F_s = 8000$ Hz, autocorrelation peak at $k^* = 72$ samples (excluding the $k=0$ peak which is the energy).

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the pitch period in samples:
$$T_0 = k^* = 72\ \text{samples}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Convert to seconds:
$$T_0 = 72 / 8000 = 0.009\ \text{s} = 9\ \text{ms}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the fundamental frequency:
$$F_0 = F_s / k^* = 8000 / 72 = 111.11\ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Demographic inference тАФ Since $F_0 = 111.11$ Hz falls in the typical **adult male pitch range (85тАУ180 Hz)**, the speaker is most likely an **adult male**.

> **Verification:** $F_0 = 111.11$ Hz is well within the 60тАУ400 Hz range and is below the 165 Hz threshold typically separating male from female voices. тЬУ

---

### тнР Question B (Choice 2 тАФ Alternative) тАФ 14 Marks [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]

**a)** Explain the source-filter model of speech production. How does this model help in distinguishing voiced and unvoiced sounds? **(7 marks)**

**b)** A 25 ms speech frame sampled at 8 kHz has the following 10 consecutive sample values (normalized to [-1, 1]):
$$x = [0.10,\ 0.25,\ 0.40,\ 0.15,\ -0.20,\ -0.45,\ -0.30,\ 0.05,\ 0.35,\ 0.50]$$
Compute the Short-Time Energy and Zero Crossing Rate. Classify the frame. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (a) тАФ 7 marks

**Source-Filter Model (5 marks):** The source-filter model, proposed by **Fant (1960)**, decomposes speech production into two independent components:

1. **Source (excitation) $e(t)$** тАФ Generated at the **glottis** (vocal folds):
   - **Voiced excitation:** Quasi-periodic pulse train with period $T_0$ (= 1/$F_0$).
   - **Unvoiced excitation:** Random, stationary, zero-mean white noise.
   - **Silent:** Zero amplitude.

2. **Filter (vocal tract) $h(t)$** тАФ Models the resonance cavities (pharynx, oral, nasal). Characterized by **formants** $F_1, F_2, F_3, \dots$ which are the resonant frequencies of the vocal tract.

**Mathematical model:**
$$s(t) = e(t) * h(t)$$

In the $z$-domain:
$$S(z) = E(z) \cdot H(z)$$

**Distinguishing Voiced vs. Unvoiced (2 marks):**
- Voiced sounds have a **harmonic spectrum** with energy at integer multiples of $F_0$ (60тАУ400 Hz) and high STE.
- Unvoiced sounds have a **flat, broadband spectrum** (no harmonic structure), high ZCR, and lower STE.
- The source excitation is the only quantity that differs тАФ the vocal tract filter is similar in both cases (e.g., /s/ and /z/ share the same vocal tract shape but differ in excitation).

---

#### Model Solution тАФ Part (b) тАФ 7 marks

**Given:** $x = [0.10,\ 0.25,\ 0.40,\ 0.15,\ -0.20,\ -0.45,\ -0.30,\ 0.05,\ 0.35,\ 0.50]$

**Step 1 тАФ Compute STE (3 marks):**

$$E_i = \sum_{m=0}^{9} [x(m)]^2$$

$$E_i = (0.10)^2 + (0.25)^2 + (0.40)^2 + (0.15)^2 + (-0.20)^2 + (-0.45)^2 + (-0.30)^2 + (0.05)^2 + (0.35)^2 + (0.50)^2$$

$$E_i = 0.01 + 0.0625 + 0.16 + 0.0225 + 0.04 + 0.2025 + 0.09 + 0.0025 + 0.1225 + 0.25$$

$$\boxed{E_i = 0.9625}$$

**Step 2 тАФ Compute ZCR (3 marks):**

Check sign changes between consecutive samples:

| Transition | Sign change? |
|------------|--------------|
| $0.10 \to 0.25$ | No |
| $0.25 \to 0.40$ | No |
| $0.40 \to 0.15$ | No |
| $0.15 \to -0.20$ | **Yes (+ to -)** |
| $-0.20 \to -0.45$ | No |
| $-0.45 \to -0.30$ | No |
| $-0.30 \to 0.05$ | **Yes (- to +)** |
| $0.05 \to 0.35$ | No |
| $0.35 \to 0.50$ | No |

Total sign changes = **2**

$$Z_i = 2\ \text{crossings}$$

**Step 3 тАФ Classification (1 mark):**

With $E_i = 0.9625$ (high) and $Z_i = 2$ (low, since 25 ms frame at 8 kHz = 200 samples, and 2 crossings in 10 samples extrapolates to тЙИ 40 in full frame тАФ borderline). Given the high STE and modest ZCR, the frame is classified as **VOICED**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting normalization (тАУ1 to тАУ2 marks):** Always normalize the signal before computing features. Raw 16-bit PCM will destroy threshold-based classification.
>
> 2. **Using full-signal statistics instead of frame-based (тАУ2 marks):** STE and ZCR are **short-time** (frame-based) measures. Computing them over the entire signal defeats the purpose тАФ you cannot localize where voiced/unvoiced regions occur.
>
> 3. **Confusing ZCR with frequency (тАУ1 mark):** ZCR counts **sign changes**, not cycles. A sinusoid of frequency $F$ has ZCR = $2F$ per second, **not** $F$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the ┬╜ factor in ZCR formula (тАУ1 mark):** The standard definition includes $\tfrac{1}{2}$ to avoid double-counting the magnitude $|\operatorname{sgn}(x(m)) - \operatorname{sgn}(x(m-1))|$ which is always 2 (not 1) at a zero crossing.
>
> 5. **Not justifying threshold values (тАУ1 mark):** In the lab record, always explain **how** you chose $E_{high}$, $E_{low}$, and $Z_{th}$ (typically 3тАУ5├Ч the silent-region mean).

---

## ЁЯза Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Three speech regions** = **Voiced** (vocal-fold pulses, high STE, low ZCR, has pitch), **Unvoiced** (turbulent noise, low/medium STE, high ZCR, no pitch), **Silent** (no excitation, very low STE).
- [ ] **Source-filter model:** $s(t) = e(t) * h(t)$. Source = excitation (glottis), Filter = vocal tract.
- [ ] **STE formula:** $E_i = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} [x_i^{(w)}(m)]^2$ тАФ measures amplitude.
- [ ] **ZCR formula:** $Z_i = \tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{m=1}^{N-1} \vert \operatorname{sgn}[x(m)] - \operatorname{sgn}[x(m-1)] \vert$ тАФ measures frequency content.
- [ ] **Pitch period** = lag of first autocorrelation peak (excluding $k=0$); $F_0 = F_s / T_0$.
- [ ] **Adult human pitch range:** $\mathbf{60\text{тАУ}400\ Hz}$ (males тЙИ 85тАУ180 Hz, females тЙИ 165тАУ255 Hz, children up to 400 Hz).
- [ ] **Standard frame size:** $\mathbf{20\text{тАУ}30\ ms}$, **hop size:** $\mathbf{10\ ms}$ (50% overlap).
- [ ] **Standard sampling rate for telephony:** $\mathbf{8000\ Hz}$.
- [ ] **Window function:** **Hamming** preferred over rectangular to reduce spectral leakage.
- [ ] **Pre-emphasis filter:** $y(n) = x(n) - \alpha x(n-1)$ with $\alpha \approx 0.95$.
- [ ] **Decision rule:** $E_i < E_{low} \Rightarrow$ Silent; $E_i \geq E_{high}$ and $Z_i < Z_{th} \Rightarrow$ Voiced; $E_i$ medium and $Z_i \geq Z_{th} \Rightarrow$ Unvoiced.
- [ ] **ZCR discriminates** unvoiced from voiced; **STE discriminates** silent from speech.
- [ ] **Always normalize** $x_{norm} = x / \max(\vert x \vert)$ before feature computation.
- [ ] **Nyquist:** $F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$ where $F_{max} \approx 4$ kHz for speech.
- [ ] **Practical applications:** ASR front-ends, speech codecs (GSM, AMR), speaker recognition, hearing aids, noise cancellation.
